site.tagline: See it. Understand its scale. Compare it to anything.
site.description: The visual reference for ships — sourced spec tables, every operator, true-scale comparisons.
site.author: ships.fyi

ships.name: Legend of the Seas
ships.headline: The largest cruise ship in the world — the third Icon-class giant, and the first to call Europe home.
ships.identity: Delivered in June 2026 and a few millimetres longer than her two sisters, Legend of the Seas currently holds the title every cruise ship page on this site has to reckon with: the largest cruise ship in the world.

variants.name: Icon class
variants.note: Third of the Icon class, after Icon of the Seas (2024) and Star of the Seas (2025). Hero of the Seas follows in 2027, with three more on order through 2030. All are LNG-powered.

variants.name: The extra foot
variants.note: Legend measured out roughly a foot longer than her sisters at delivery — enough, at this scale, to take the world's-largest title on length while sharing it on tonnage.

operators.note: Royal Caribbean's 30th ship and current flagship-by-size. First Icon-class ship homeported in Europe.

prose: Legend of the Seas is what happens when a record is defended rather than set. Her sisters Icon and Star of the Seas had already redefined the top of the cruise industry at 248,663 gross tons; Legend arrived in June 2026 measuring a foot or so longer, and with that sliver of steel the largest-cruise-ship title changed hands inside the same family for the second year running.

What genuinely separates her is geography. Icon-class ships were built for the Caribbean run out of Florida; Legend is the first to homeport in Europe, sailing the Western Mediterranean out of Barcelona and Civitavecchia through her debut summer before crossing to Fort Lauderdale for the winter. She is by a wide margin the largest cruise ship ever to operate a European season.

Under the records she is a known quantity: eight neighborhoods, seven pools, the six-slide Category 6 waterpark, around 2,350 crew, and LNG dual-fuel propulsion — the fourth LNG ship in the Royal Caribbean fleet. Construction had one scare, an engine-room fire at Meyer Turku in December 2025, but the yard held the schedule and handed her over on 10 June 2026.

sources.name: Royal Caribbean — Legend of the Seas

ships.name: Icon of the Seas
ships.headline: The ship that started the Icon class — the largest cruise ship in the world from 2024 until her own sisters edged past.
ships.identity: Icon of the Seas rewrote the top of the cruise record books in January 2024 and held the world's-largest title until her sister ships matched and then narrowly exceeded her. Five times the gross tonnage of the Titanic.

variants.name: Icon class
variants.note: Lead ship of the class. Star of the Seas followed in 2025 and Legend of the Seas in 2026; Hero of the Seas arrives in 2027, with orders running through 2030.

variants.name: Category 6
variants.note: The largest waterpark ever put on a ship — six waterslides, including the first free-fall slide at sea — plus seven pools and the AquaDome, the largest single glass-and-steel structure ever lifted onto a cruise ship.

operators.note: Sails year-round seven-night Caribbean itineraries from Miami.

prose: When Icon of the Seas arrived in Miami in January 2024 she was not an incremental record — she was a step change. At 248,663 gross tons she came in around six percent larger than Wonder of the Seas, the biggest ship of the previous generation, and with nearly 10,000 people aboard at full capacity she carries the population of a small town. Comparisons to the Titanic became a meme for a reason: Icon is five times the tonnage and about 96 metres longer.

The design is organized as eight neighborhoods rather than decks-with-venues — a resort layout borrowed from the Oasis class and pushed further. The engineering signature is the AquaDome, a 363-tonne glass and steel dome assembled on the ground at Meyer Turku and lifted onto the bow section in one piece, and the LNG dual-fuel plant that made her the largest gas-powered passenger ship ever built at delivery.

Her reign as the world's largest lasted about eighteen months. Star of the Seas matched her in 2025, and Legend of the Seas measured out roughly a foot longer at delivery in June 2026. The title now lives inside her own class — which is exactly how Royal Caribbean planned it.

sources.name: Royal Caribbean — Icon of the Seas

ships.name: Star of the Seas
ships.headline: The second Icon-class ship — Port Canaveral's giant, matching her lead sister tonne for tonne.
ships.identity: Star of the Seas is Icon's twin in every number that matters, delivered in 2025 to bring the Icon-class formula to Port Canaveral.

variants.name: Icon class
variants.note: Second of the class. Structurally a sister to Icon of the Seas, with entertainment and dining variations rather than dimensional ones.

operators.note: Sails seven-night Caribbean itineraries from Port Canaveral, Florida.

prose: Star of the Seas is the proof that Icon of the Seas was a class, not a one-off. Delivered by Meyer Turku in 2025 and sailing from Port Canaveral, she matches her lead sister at 248,663 gross tons and 5,610 passengers at double occupancy — which made her, on delivery, the joint largest cruise ship in the world rather than a new record holder.

That is a deliberate strategy. Royal Caribbean learned with the Oasis class that a family of near-identical giants is worth more than a single champion: the same terminals, the same crew training, the same spare parts, and a headline ship in more than one home port at once. Star brought the Icon experience — the AquaDome, the Category 6 waterpark, the eight-neighborhood layout — to Florida's second great cruise port.

Her one unqualified superlative lasted until June 2026, when Legend of the Seas measured out fractionally longer. On this site's boards the three sisters sit together at the top of the gross tonnage table, separated by rounding errors.

sources.name: Royal Caribbean — Star of the Seas

ships.name: Wonder of the Seas
ships.headline: The fifth and largest Oasis-class ship — the world's biggest cruise ship from 2022 until the Icon class arrived.
ships.identity: Wonder of the Seas closed out the Oasis class's twelve-year run at the top of the cruise world, holding the largest-ship title from 2022 to 2024.

variants.name: Oasis class
variants.note: Fifth of six: Oasis (2009), Allure (2010), Harmony (2016), Symphony (2018), Wonder (2022), Utopia (2024). The class held the world's-largest title continuously from 2009 to 2024.

operators.note: Caribbean itineraries from Port Canaveral and Miami.

prose: Wonder of the Seas was the last and largest expression of the Oasis class — the split-superstructure design from Chantiers de l'Atlantique that put a park with real trees down the middle of a ship and held the world's-largest-cruise-ship record for fifteen straight years across five hulls.

At 236,857 gross tons she took the title from her own sister Symphony in 2022 and kept it until Icon of the Seas arrived in January 2024. Her signature remains the class's Central Park and Boardwalk neighborhoods, open to the sky between the two towers of cabins — a trick of naval architecture that also gives the class its enormous maximum beam.

She actually carries more passengers at double occupancy than the Icon-class ships that dethroned her — 5,734 against 5,610 — a reminder that gross tonnage measures volume, not people. On tonnage she now ranks behind the three Icon sisters and her younger sibling Utopia of the Seas.

sources.name: Royal Caribbean — Wonder of the Seas

ships.name: Symphony of the Seas
ships.headline: The fourth Oasis-class giant — world's largest cruise ship from 2018 to 2022.
ships.identity: Symphony of the Seas carried the world's-largest title for four years and remains one of the highest-capacity cruise ships ever built.

variants.name: Oasis class
variants.note: Fourth of six. Took the largest-ship title from sister Harmony in 2018; handed it to sister Wonder in 2022.

operators.note: Caribbean itineraries from Miami, with past Mediterranean seasons.

prose: Symphony of the Seas spent four years as the largest cruise ship in the world, and did it the Oasis-class way: not by novelty but by refinement, stacking incremental gains on Harmony's design until she measured 228,081 gross tons.

Like every Oasis-class ship she is really two towers of cabins with an open-air canyon between them — Central Park, with more than 20,000 live plants, running down the middle of the ship at deck eight. The class's genuinely strange achievement is making a vessel of this size feel like an outdoor resort rather than an enclosed hull.

The title moved on to Wonder of the Seas in 2022 and then to the Icon class, but Symphony still sits comfortably inside the world's top ten by tonnage, and her seven-night Caribbean program from Miami is among the most heavily booked itineraries in cruising.

sources.name: Royal Caribbean — Symphony of the Seas

ships.name: Harmony of the Seas
ships.headline: The third Oasis-class ship — the 2016 record-holder that brought the class back to Saint-Nazaire.
ships.identity: Harmony of the Seas returned Oasis-class construction to France and took the world's-largest crown in 2016 with the Ultimate Abyss — the tallest slide at sea.

variants.name: Oasis class
variants.note: Third of six, and the first built at Chantiers de l'Atlantique in Saint-Nazaire after the first two came from STX Finland. Marginally the longest of the class until Wonder.

operators.note: Caribbean itineraries from Galveston and Florida home ports.

prose: Harmony of the Seas restarted the Oasis class after a six-year gap and moved its construction from Finland to Saint-Nazaire — the same slipways that built the France, the Queen Mary 2's hull sections, and, forty years earlier, the largest supertankers ever floated. At delivery in May 2016 she was the largest cruise ship yet built, at 226,963 gross tons.

She introduced the Ultimate Abyss, a ten-storey dry slide dropping from the top of the aft towers, and the class's third generation of energy systems — air lubrication under the hull and a heat-recovery plant that cut fuel burn noticeably against her older sisters.
Two years later her own sister Symphony took the title, which is simply how the Oasis class operated for a decade and a half: the record never left the family, it just moved one hull along.

sources.name: Royal Caribbean — Harmony of the Seas

ships.name: Oasis of the Seas
ships.headline: The 2009 original that invented the modern mega-ship — first to carry a park at sea.
ships.identity: Oasis of the Seas made every previous cruise ship look small overnight in 2009 — forty percent bigger than anything afloat, with a living park down her spine.

variants.name: Oasis class
variants.note: Lead ship, built at STX Europe's Turku yard in Finland (the later ships moved to Saint-Nazaire, and the class's builder heritage now spans both yards credited across this site to Chantiers de l'Atlantique and Meyer Turku's predecessor). Amplified in 2019, which nudged her tonnage up to 226,838 GT.

operators.note: Caribbean and Bahamas itineraries; the 2019 amplification added the Ultimate Abyss and a full venue refresh.

prose: Every giant on this site's cruise board descends from Oasis of the Seas. When she was delivered in 2009 she was around forty percent larger by tonnage than the biggest cruise ship then afloat — a generational jump the industry had never seen and has not repeated since.

Her radical idea was the split superstructure: two parallel towers of balcony cabins with an open-air corridor between them, filled with Central Park's live trees and the Boardwalk's carousel. Balconies could now face inward as well as out to sea, which changed the economics of ship design — more sellable cabins per metre of hull.

Built at STX Europe in Turku, she was also the ship that proved the port infrastructure question: terminals, channels and even the Great Belt Bridge clearance (her funnels were designed to retract) all had to adapt to her, not the other way round. Sixteen years and one major amplification later she is still in front-line service.

sources.name: Royal Caribbean — Oasis of the Seas

ships.name: MSC World Europa
ships.headline: MSC's LNG-powered flagship class lead — the largest cruise ship ever built for a European line.
ships.identity: MSC World Europa leads the World class: the biggest ships MSC has ever ordered, LNG-powered, and unmistakable for the Y-shaped aft profile and its eleven-deck dry slide.

variants.name: World class
variants.note: Lead of MSC's World class: World Europa (2022), World America (2025), with World Asia and World Atlantic to follow. The largest ships in the MSC Cruises fleet.

operators.note: Flagship-scale Mediterranean itineraries after debuting at the FIFA World Cup in Qatar as a hotel ship.

prose: MSC World Europa is what Europe's largest privately owned shipping dynasty builds when it decides to compete at the very top of cruising. At 215,863 gross tons she is the largest cruise ship ever ordered by a European line, and the first of MSC's LNG-powered World class from Chantiers de l'Atlantique.

Her profile breaks with cruise convention: a near-vertical bow and a Y-shaped stern that opens the aft decks into an amphitheatre around the World Promenade, down which runs The Venom Drop — an eleven-deck dry slide that is the longest at sea.

She made her debut not on a cruise at all but as a floating hotel for the 2022 FIFA World Cup in Doha, before starting Gulf and then Mediterranean seasons. Sister ship MSC World America followed to Miami in 2025, aimed squarely at the Icon and Oasis classes on their home turf.

ships.name: Carnival Jubilee
ships.headline: The third Excel-class ship — Carnival's LNG-powered Texas flagship with a rollercoaster on the roof.
ships.identity: Carnival Jubilee brought the Excel class to Galveston: the biggest ships Carnival has ever operated, with the BOLT rollercoaster running around the funnel.

variants.name: Excel class
variants.note: Third of Carnival's Excel class after Mardi Gras (2021) and Carnival Celebration (2022), with Festivale following. Based on the same Meyer platform as AIDAnova and Costa Smeralda.

operators.note: Year-round Western Caribbean itineraries from Galveston, Texas.

prose: Carnival Jubilee is the third of the Excel class, the largest ships in Carnival Cruise Line's history and the first American-market ships powered by LNG. She shares her platform with AIDAnova and Costa Smeralda — Carnival Corporation ordered the same Meyer-designed LNG hull for three of its brands and let each dress it differently.

Carnival's dressing includes BOLT, an electric rollercoaster looping the funnel deck at up to 40 mph — still the only rollercoaster genre at sea — and two new ocean-themed zones, Currents and The Shores, unique to Jubilee within the class.
Stationed year-round in Galveston, she is the largest cruise ship ever based in Texas, a deliberate bet on the fastest-growing drive-to cruise market in the United States.

ships.name: Norwegian Aqua
ships.headline: The first Prima Plus-class ship — NCL's 2025 flagship with the world's first hybrid rollercoaster-waterslide.
ships.identity: Norwegian Aqua stretched the Prima design into a new class for 2025, headlined by the Aqua Slidecoaster — the first hybrid rollercoaster-waterslide at sea.

variants.name: Prima Plus class
variants.note: First of the stretched Prima Plus subclass — about ten percent larger than Norwegian Prima and Viva — with Norwegian Luna following in 2026.

operators.note: Caribbean itineraries from Port Canaveral and Miami.

prose: Norwegian Aqua is the ship NCL built when the Prima class proved slightly too small for its ambitions: the hull was stretched and the design refined into the Prima Plus class, roughly ten percent larger, with Aqua delivered by Fincantieri in March 2025 as its lead.

Her headline is the Aqua Slidecoaster, a hybrid of rollercoaster and waterslide in which magnetically launched rafts climb and dive around the funnel — a genuine first at sea, in an industry where top-deck hardware is the sharpest marketing weapon.

At 156,300 gross tons she plays in a different weight class from the Icon and Oasis giants, which is the point: NCL sells a mid-scale, no-set-dining formula where the ship is large enough for spectacle but small enough for ports the giants cannot enter.

ships.name: Disney Wish
ships.headline: The first Wish-class ship — Disney's LNG-powered flagship with the AquaMouse water coaster.
ships.identity: Disney Wish opened Disney's third generation of cruise ships in 2022: LNG-powered, castle-themed, and carrying the AquaMouse ride through 230 metres of tube above her top deck.

variants.name: Wish class
variants.note: Lead of the class, followed by Disney Treasure (2024) and Disney Destiny (2025). Disney's first LNG-powered ships and its largest to date.

operators.note: Three- and four-night Bahamas itineraries from Port Canaveral, calling at Disney's private destinations.

prose: Disney Wish is deliberately not the biggest ship on this board — Disney sells space per guest, not guests per ship. At roughly 144,000 gross tons she carries only about 2,500 passengers at double occupancy, a ratio of volume to people that the mega-ships cannot match.

She was Disney's first new class in a decade and its first LNG-powered ship, built by Meyer Werft with a fairy-tale-castle Grand Hall in place of a traditional atrium and the AquaMouse — a ride Disney insists is an 'attraction at sea' rather than a waterslide, with show scenes played inside the tube.
The class formula worked: Disney Treasure and Disney Destiny followed in 2024 and 2025, and the Wish platform now anchors Disney's Port Canaveral operation alongside the original Magic-class ships.

ships.name: AIDAnova
ships.headline: The first LNG-powered cruise ship in the world — the 2018 pioneer every gas-fuelled giant follows.
ships.identity: AIDAnova was the first cruise ship ever to run on liquefied natural gas — the 2018 proof-of-concept that Icon, Legend, World Europa and the rest all built on.

specs.label: Propulsion
specs.metric: LNG dual-fuel — world first for a cruise ship
specs.imperial: LNG dual-fuel — world first for a cruise ship

variants.name: Excellence class
variants.note: Lead of Carnival Corporation's LNG platform, later adapted as Costa Smeralda/Toscana and Carnival's Excel class. AIDAcosma joined the AIDA fleet in 2022.

operators.note: German-market itineraries — Canaries in winter, Northern Europe and the Mediterranean in summer.

prose: Every LNG-powered giant on this site — Icon, Star, Legend, World Europa, Jubilee, Wish — is following a trail AIDAnova cut in December 2018, when she became the first cruise ship in history able to run entirely on liquefied natural gas, in port and at sea.

The engineering risk was real: cryogenic tanks at minus 162 degrees Celsius inside a passenger ship, a new bunkering infrastructure that barely existed, and dual-fuel engines at a scale the industry had not certified before. Carnival Corporation absorbed that risk once and then rolled the same platform out across three brands.

Built for the German market, AIDAnova is easy to spot: the kissing lips and eyes painted across her bow are AIDA's trademark, wrapped around what was, at delivery, one of the ten largest cruise ships in the world.

ships.name: Costa Smeralda
ships.headline: Costa's LNG flagship — Italy's largest cruise ship, built on the AIDAnova platform.
ships.identity: Costa Smeralda is the Italian expression of the Carnival group's LNG platform — the largest ship ever to sail for Italy's biggest cruise line.

variants.name: Excellence platform
variants.note: Sister to AIDAnova and AIDAcosma; Costa Toscana (2021) is her direct fleetmate. Interiors by Adam Tihany, themed as a tour of Italy.

operators.note: Year-round Western Mediterranean loops — Italy, France and Spain.

prose: Costa Smeralda is the second act of Carnival Corporation's LNG gamble: the AIDAnova platform re-costumed for the Italian market and delivered by Meyer Turku in December 2019 as the largest ship in Costa's century of history.

Named for Sardinia's Emerald Coast, she was designed as a floating tour of Italy — her decks named for Italian cities and her interiors built around Italian design brands, with the Solemio spa and a private-terrace ship-within-a-ship zone at the top.

Her early career collided with history: she was among the first ships caught in the January 2020 COVID scare in Civitavecchia, weeks before the industry shut down entirely. She has since settled into the workhorse role she was built for, looping the Western Mediterranean year-round.

ships.name: Queen Mary 2
ships.headline: The only true ocean liner in service — built to cross the North Atlantic at speed, not to cruise it.
ships.identity: Queen Mary 2 is the last of a species: a genuine ocean liner, with the heavy hull, deep draft and near-30-knot speed to hold a schedule across the North Atlantic in winter.

variants.name: One of one
variants.note: No sister ship was ever built. At delivery she was the longest, largest and most expensive passenger ship ever constructed; she remains the largest ocean liner ever built.

operators.note: The scheduled Southampton–New York transatlantic crossing, continuously since 2004, plus an annual world voyage.

prose: Queen Mary 2 is routinely mistaken for a cruise ship, and the difference is the entire point of her existence. A cruise ship is a resort that moves between calm ports; an ocean liner is a vehicle built to hold a schedule across the North Atlantic in January. QM2 has forty percent more steel in her hull than a comparable cruise ship, a bow like a knife, a 10-metre draft, and four diesels plus two gas turbines driving her at speeds no cruise ship attempts.

She was built by Chantiers de l'Atlantique — the same Saint-Nazaire yard as the Normandie and the France before her — and delivered in December 2003 as the largest ocean liner ever constructed, a record she still holds because nobody has built another one since.

More than two decades on she still runs the scheduled Southampton–New York crossing her ancestors ran for Cunard since 1840, carrying kennels for transatlantic dogs, the only planetarium at sea, and passengers who want seven days where the ship is the destination and the ocean is the show.

photo.file: Stern, RMS Queen Mary 2, Liverpool Cruise Terminal (geograph 4555739).jpg
photo.author: El Pollock

ships.name: RMS Titanic
ships.headline: The most famous ship ever built — the 1912 liner every giant on this site is still measured against.
ships.identity: Titanic was the largest ship in the world for ten months and has been the world's reference object for size ever since. Every modern giant gets compared to her; on this site, she is a bar on almost every chart.

specs.label: Gross register tonnage
specs.metric: 46,328 GRT (pre-1982 measurement — not directly comparable to modern GT)

variants.name: Olympic class
variants.note: Second of three: Olympic (1911, scrapped 1935), Titanic (1912), Britannic (1915, mined 1916 as a hospital ship). Olympic was the only sister to have a full career.

operators.note: White Star's challenge to Cunard's speed records was comfort and scale rather than pace.

prose: Titanic is on this site for the same reason she is in everyone's head: she is the unit people actually think in. 'Five Titanics' means something to a reader in a way that 248,663 gross tons never will, which is why she appears as a reference bar on the scale charts of nearly every modern giant here.

As a ship she was the second of White Star's Olympic class, built by Harland & Wolff in Belfast as the largest moving objects yet made — 269 metres, 46,328 gross register tons, and a first-class experience aimed at beating Cunard on luxury rather than speed. Four days into her maiden voyage she struck an iceberg at 23:40 on 14 April 1912 and sank in under three hours, with the loss of around 1,500 of the 2,224 people aboard — lifeboat capacity existed for barely half of them.

The disaster rewrote maritime law: the first SOLAS convention of 1914, lifeboats for all, 24-hour radio watch, and the International Ice Patrol all trace directly to that night. And her size, remarkable then, is the sharpest illustration of a century of shipbuilding: the tonnage board on this site starts at more than five of her.

ships.name: Star Princess
ships.headline: Princess Cruises' flagship — second of the Sphere class, and with her sister the largest ships ever built in Italy.
ships.identity: Star Princess is the 2025 flagship of Princess Cruises: 177,882 GT of LNG-powered Sphere class crowned by a glass Dome that turns from pool retreat to evening arena, delivered by Fincantieri at Monfalcone.

variants.name: Sphere class
variants.note: Second of the class after Sun Princess (February 2024) — Princess Cruises' largest ships and its first powered by LNG. Together the pair are the largest cruise ships ever built in Italy.

operators.note: Mediterranean inaugural season, Caribbean winters from Fort Lauderdale, Alaska from 2026 — she made her first Panama Canal transit in April 2026.

prose: Princess spent a decade watching rivals build ever-bigger hulls and then answered with the Sphere class: two 345-metre, 177,882-ton sisters from Fincantieri's Monfalcone yard that are the largest cruise ships ever constructed in Italy. Star Princess is the second of them, delivered in September 2025, eighteen months after Sun Princess opened the class — and at 345 metres she stands eye to eye with Queen Mary 2 on the length board while carrying nearly four times the modern liner's berths per metre of hull.

The class signature is the Dome, a geodesic glass crown over the bow inspired by Santorini terraces — a pool retreat by day that converts to an aerial-show arena by night — backed by an Arena theater with moving seating and a three-deck Piazza. She is also Princess's second LNG-powered ship, part of parent Carnival Corporation's long dual-fuel programme.

Star Princess is the third ship to wear the name, and the first with a tequila christening: Camila and Matthew McConaughey broke a bottle of their own label across her bow at Port Everglades in November 2025. By April 2026 she had already threaded the Neopanamax locks for her first Panama transit — at 47 metres of beam, she clears the 51.25-metre gauge with under two metres of margin per side.
facts: With sister Sun Princess, the largest ships ever built in Italy — eclipsing MSC Seashore's 170,412 GT record at Monfalcone.
The Dome over her bow is the first fully glass-enclosed structure of its kind at sea.
Christened with a giant bottle of Pantalones tequila by Camila and Matthew McConaughey in November 2025.
Made her first Panama Canal transit in April 2026 through the Neopanamax locks.

ships.name: Arvia
ships.headline: The largest cruise ship ever built for Britain — P&O's 185,581 GT Excellence-class flagship.
ships.identity: Arvia is the biggest ship ever created for the British cruise market: a 185,581 GT, LNG-powered Excellence-class resort from Meyer Werft, sailing year-round for Southampton's P&O Cruises since December 2022.

variants.name: Excellence (Helios) class
variants.note: Sister to Iona (2020, 184,089 GT) within P&O, and cousin to Carnival Jubilee, AIDAnova and Costa Smeralda across the Carnival Corporation family platform.

operators.note: Southampton-based year-round — Mediterranean and Norway summers, Caribbean fly-cruise winters.

prose: Britain's biggest ship is not a warship or a tanker — she is a Southampton-based holiday resort named Arvia, 'from the seashore'. At 185,581 gross tons she out-measures every vessel ever built for the UK market, edging past her own sister Iona by some 1,500 tons, and she was designed unapologetically around the British holiday calendar: Norway and the Mediterranean in summer, Caribbean fly-cruises through the winter.

She is the ninth hull of Carnival Corporation's Excellence platform — the same LNG-powered family as Carnival Jubilee, AIDAnova and Costa Smeralda — built by Meyer Werft in Papenburg and squeezed down the River Ems in November 2022. Her SkyDome carries a 970-square-metre glass roof of 340 panes weighing 105 tons, installed by one of the largest mobile cranes in Europe.

Named in Barbados by Nicole Scherzinger in March 2023 — P&O's first beachside christening — Arvia represents the world's oldest cruise company at its newest: the line traces its passenger routes to 1822, a full ninety years before Titanic, and its modern flagship now carries more people than the population of many of the ports she visits.
facts: The largest ship ever built for the British market — 185,581 GT against sister Iona's 184,089.
Her SkyDome glass roof spans 970 square metres in 340 panes and weighs 105 tons.
P&O is the world's oldest cruise company, carrying passengers since 1822.
Christened on a Barbados beach by Nicole Scherzinger in March 2023.

ships.name: Celebrity Xcel
ships.headline: Celebrity's 2025 flagship — fifth of the Edge class, and the fleet's first methanol-ready ship.
ships.identity: Celebrity Xcel closed out the Edge class in October 2025 as Celebrity's flagship: ≈141,420 GT from Chantiers de l'Atlantique, carrying the cantilevered Magic Carpet and the industry's first tri-fuel, methanol-capable engines in the fleet.

variants.name: Edge class
variants.note: Fifth and largest-generation Edge ship after Edge (2018), Apex (2020), Beyond (2022) and Ascent (2023); sister Xcite follows in 2028.

operators.note: Caribbean seven-night itineraries from Fort Lauderdale, moving to the Mediterranean from April 2026.

prose: Celebrity Xcel is what a cruise line builds when it decides the arms race is optional. At roughly 141,000 gross tons and 3,260 passengers she is nowhere near the giants' table — and that is precisely her pitch: Royal Caribbean Group's premium brand deliberately holds its flagship at two-thirds the tonnage of an Icon, trading waterparks for design.

The Edge-class party trick carries over: the Magic Carpet, a tangerine cantilevered platform the size of a tennis court that rides up and down thirteen decks of her starboard side — restaurant at one height, bar at another, tender lobby at the waterline. New for Xcel is The Bazaar, a venue that reprograms itself around every port on the itinerary.

Beneath the styling sits the genuinely historic part: Wärtsilä converted two of her engines to accept methanol alongside conventional fuels, making Xcel the first methanol-capable ship in the fleet and one of the first cruise ships anywhere built ready for the fuel — a quiet bet on what the next thirty years of her life will burn.
facts: First methanol-capable ship in the Celebrity fleet — tri-fuel Wärtsilä engines fitted from delivery.
The cantilevered Magic Carpet platform travels 13 decks up and down her starboard side.
Delivered October 23, 2025 at Saint-Nazaire — the same yard that built Queen Mary 2.
Her published tonnage varies by source (140,600–141,420 GT); we flag it approximate.

ships.name: Rotterdam
ships.headline: Holland America's flagship — the seventh Rotterdam in a line stretching back to 1872.
ships.identity: Rotterdam is Holland America Line's 2021 flagship and the seventh ship of the name since 1872: a 99,935 GT Pinnacle-class ship from Fincantieri built around live music, from a 270-degree World Stage to a Rolling Stone rock room.

variants.name: Pinnacle class
variants.note: Third of the class after Koningsdam (2016) and Nieuw Statendam (2018) — the largest ships Holland America has ever operated.

operators.note: Longer itineraries and grand voyages — she recreated the line's original 1872 Rotterdam–New York crossing for its 150th anniversary in 2022.

prose: Some ships carry a name; Rotterdam carries a dynasty. She is the seventh Holland America vessel to wear it since 1872, renamed from Ryndam mid-construction in 2020 specifically to keep the oldest name in the fleet alive — and in 2022 she marked the line's 150th anniversary by recreating the original Rotterdam's maiden crossing to New York, a route first sailed three years before the telephone was invented.

At 99,935 gross tons she is the largest ship Holland America has ever operated, yet deliberately half the size of the mega-resorts: the Pinnacle class bets on space per passenger and live music instead of waterslides. Her entertainment district, the Music Walk, runs from a Billboard piano bar through a B.B. King's blues club to the Rolling Stone Rock Room, all feeding the World Stage — a theater wrapped in a 270-degree LED horizon.

Princess Margriet of the Netherlands christened her in the city she is named for in May 2022 — the fifth Holland America ship the princess has named, a run stretching back four decades. Dutch-flagged and Rotterdam-registered, she is that increasingly rare thing: a flagship whose registry, name and heritage all point at the same place.
facts: Seventh ship named Rotterdam in Holland America's fleet since 1872.
Recreated the line's original 1872 maiden crossing for its 150th anniversary in October 2022.
Christened by Princess Margriet of the Netherlands — her fifth Holland America naming.
The largest ship in Holland America's history, yet barely a third of an Icon-class ship's tonnage.

ships.name: MSC World America
ships.headline: MSC's largest ship and its first purpose-built for the American market.
ships.identity: The second World-class ship and the biggest MSC has ever operated, MSC World America took the LNG-powered World platform to Miami and aimed it directly at the Icon and Oasis classes.

variants.name: World class
variants.note: Second of the class after MSC World Europa (2022), with MSC World Asia following in 2026. MSC has ordered eight World-class ships in total, running to 2031.

operators.note: Year-round Caribbean itineraries from PortMiami — MSC's first ship designed around the North American market.

prose: MSC World America is the point at which MSC stopped being a European line with American itineraries and became a genuine competitor on Royal Caribbean's home water. At 216,638 gross tons she is the largest ship the company has ever operated, marginally bigger than her sister MSC World Europa, and she was designed from the keel up for Miami rather than the Mediterranean.

The World-class platform carries over intact: the near-vertical bow, the Y-shaped stern that opens the aft decks into a terraced amphitheatre, twenty-two decks, and LNG dual-fuel propulsion. What changed is the interior logic — seven districts rather than a single spine, a family district with its own pools, and an expanded Yacht Club ship-within-a-ship at the bow.

She matters commercially more than she does technically. Icon of the Seas and the Oasis class had the Caribbean mega-ship market largely to themselves; World America is the first non-Royal ship built at that scale specifically to contest it, and the eight-ship World-class order behind her signals MSC intends to keep contesting it into the 2030s.

ships.name: MSC Euribia
ships.headline: MSC's second LNG ship — and the one that sailed a net-zero voyage to prove the fuel chain works.
ships.identity: The largest of the Meraviglia-Plus ships at 184,011 GT, MSC Euribia is named for the ancient goddess of seafaring and carries the fleet's most advanced environmental package.

variants.name: Meraviglia-Plus
variants.note: Fifth and final Meraviglia-family ship, after Meraviglia, Bellissima, Grandiosa and Virtuosa. Slightly larger than her Plus sisters and the only LNG ship in the family.

variants.name: Net-zero voyage
variants.note: In June 2023 she sailed Saint-Nazaire to Copenhagen on bio-LNG in a demonstration voyage MSC reported as net-zero greenhouse gas emissions.

operators.note: Northern Europe and Mediterranean seasons; christened in Copenhagen in June 2023.

prose: MSC Euribia is the environmental argument of the MSC fleet made into steel. She is the second LNG-powered ship the company took delivery of, the largest of the Meraviglia-Plus group at 184,011 gross tons, and the one MSC points to when the subject of cruise emissions comes up.

The claim was tested immediately. In June 2023, on her delivery voyage from Saint-Nazaire to Copenhagen, she ran on bio-LNG across the full four-day passage in what MSC reported as a net-zero greenhouse gas voyage. The exercise was as much about the fuel supply chain as the ship — bio-LNG had to be produced, certified and bunkered at scale to make it work.

Underneath the environmental headline she is a conventional Meraviglia-Plus: 331 metres, sixteen passenger decks, the two-deck Galleria promenade under an LED dome, and the Yacht Club enclave forward. Her hull art, chosen from a children's competition, shows two dolphins — a deliberate contrast to the industrial subject matter of her machinery.

ships.name: MSC Virtuosa
ships.headline: The second Meraviglia-Plus ship — delayed a year by the pandemic, then sent to restart British cruising.
ships.identity: MSC Virtuosa shares her 181,541 GT hull with MSC Grandiosa, and became the first large cruise ship to sail from a UK port when the industry restarted in May 2021.

variants.name: Meraviglia-Plus
variants.note: Second of the sub-class, sister to MSC Grandiosa. Identical tonnage and length; both stretched from the original Meraviglia platform.

variants.name: Delayed debut
variants.note: Originally scheduled to enter service in November 2020; delivery and debut slipped by roughly six months because of the COVID-19 pandemic.

operators.note: Mediterranean and Northern Europe; ran the first UK domestic cruise season of the restart from Southampton.

prose: MSC Virtuosa is a ship whose story is mostly about timing. Floated out in November 2019 and due in service a year later, she instead sat finished and empty through the worst of the pandemic, and was finally handed over in February 2021 into an industry that had almost entirely stopped sailing.

What she then did was unusual: rather than wait, MSC sent her to Southampton for a season of UK-domestic round-trips, making her one of the first large cruise ships back in service anywhere and the ship that effectively reopened British cruising in May 2021.

Technically she is the second Meraviglia-Plus, sharing her 181,541-ton, 331-metre hull exactly with MSC Grandiosa. The signature spaces are the same — the Galleria promenade beneath a 98-metre LED dome, the Yacht Club forward, and a shopping galleria that MSC bills as the largest at sea — along with Rob, a humanoid robot bartender, which is either delightful or exactly what you feared, depending on temperament.

ships.name: MSC Grandiosa
ships.headline: The first Meraviglia-Plus — and the ship that restarted Mediterranean cruising in 2020.
ships.identity: MSC Grandiosa opened the stretched Meraviglia-Plus sub-class at 181,541 GT, and in August 2020 became the first major cruise ship back in service after the global shutdown.

variants.name: Meraviglia-Plus
variants.note: Lead ship of the stretched sub-class, followed by MSC Virtuosa. Roughly 10,000 GT larger than the original Meraviglia pair.

variants.name: The 2020 restart
variants.note: Resumed Mediterranean sailings in August 2020 under a bubble protocol — the first major cruise ship to return to service after the worldwide suspension.

operators.note: Western Mediterranean year-round, with Caribbean seasons from PortMiami from 2024.

prose: MSC Grandiosa introduced the Meraviglia-Plus: the same architecture as MSC Meraviglia stretched by about fifteen metres and ten thousand gross tons, which bought an extra deck of cabins and a longer promenade without redesigning the platform.

Her real significance is historical. In August 2020, with essentially the entire world cruise fleet laid up, Grandiosa resumed Western Mediterranean sailings under a strict protocol — reduced capacity, universal pre-boarding testing, and shore excursions restricted to organised bubbles. She was the first major cruise ship anywhere to carry paying passengers again, and the template she ran became the industry's restart playbook.

In normal operation she is a Mediterranean workhorse: nineteen decks, the Galleria promenade under its LED ceiling, a Yacht Club enclave at the bow, and from 2024 a Caribbean season out of Miami alongside her newer fleetmates.

ships.name: MSC Meraviglia
ships.headline: The class that changed MSC's scale — and the ship that put an LED sky over the promenade.
ships.identity: MSC Meraviglia was, at delivery in 2017, the largest ship ever built by a European cruise line, and the lead ship of the class that carried MSC into the mega-ship era.

variants.name: Meraviglia class
variants.note: Lead ship, followed by MSC Bellissima (2019). The stretched Meraviglia-Plus ships — Grandiosa, Virtuosa and Euribia — developed from the same platform.

variants.name: Project Vista
variants.note: Developed under the internal name Project Vista, the design that took MSC from roughly 140,000 GT ships to over 170,000 GT in a single generation.

operators.note: Mediterranean summers and North American seasons; extensively refitted for the North American market.

prose: MSC Meraviglia is the ship that moved MSC up a weight class. Before her the fleet topped out around 139,000 gross tons; she arrived in 2017 at 171,598 GT — the largest ship a European cruise line had ever built — and every big MSC ship since descends from her.

The defining space is the Galleria Meraviglia, a two-deck indoor promenade running most of the ship's length beneath a 480-square-metre LED ceiling that plays sky, aurora and animation depending on the hour. It solved a real problem for a line whose ships work northern Europe as well as the Caribbean: an outdoor-feeling main street that works in bad weather.

Mechanically she is conventional and well proven — diesel-electric with two 19.2 MW ABB Azipod units, 22.7 knots flat out. Her importance is architectural rather than technical: the Meraviglia platform, stretched and then LNG-converted, produced five ships in six years and remains the backbone of the MSC fleet.

ships.name: MSC Bellissima
ships.headline: The second Meraviglia — christened by Sophia Loren, with the largest LED dome at sea.
ships.identity: MSC Bellissima repeats the 171,598 GT Meraviglia hull with a longer LED dome and a full digital concierge layer, and was the ship MSC used to open its Arabian Gulf and later Asian seasons.

variants.name: Meraviglia class
variants.note: Second and final ship of the original Meraviglia pair, before the stretched Meraviglia-Plus trio.

variants.name: The LED dome
variants.note: Her Galleria ceiling is the longest LED dome at sea — roughly 80 metres — and the feature MSC marketed the ship on.

operators.note: Mediterranean, Arabian Gulf and Asian deployments; christened at Southampton in 2019.

prose: MSC Bellissima is the second and last of the original Meraviglia pair, and a near-repeat of her lead ship — same 171,598 gross tons, same 316-metre hull, same two-deck Galleria as the spine of the interior.

Where she differs is in degree rather than kind. Her LED dome runs longer than Meraviglia's, and she launched MSC's ZOE, an in-cabin voice assistant offered in seven languages — an early and slightly awkward attempt at the digital concierge idea that most of the industry has since adopted in some form.

Sophia Loren christened her at Southampton in March 2019, continuing a run as godmother that covers most of the modern MSC fleet. She has since worked the Mediterranean, the Arabian Gulf and Asia, and remains one of the most widely deployed ships in the fleet.

ships.name: MSC Seascape
ships.headline: The second Seaside EVO — 339 metres of ship built around the outdoors.
ships.identity: MSC Seascape stretches the Seaside idea to 170,412 GT and 339 metres, with more than 13,000 square metres of outdoor deck and the glass-walled Bridge of Sighs cantilevered over the side.

variants.name: Seaside EVO
variants.note: Second of the two stretched Seaside ships, after MSC Seashore. Roughly 16 metres longer and 17,000 GT larger than the original Seaside pair, with an extra deck.

operators.note: Year-round Caribbean from PortMiami.

prose: MSC Seascape is the fullest expression of the Seaside concept: a ship designed around being outside. Where most mega-ships pull passengers inward to an enclosed promenade, the Seaside platform wraps the hull in exterior decks, waterfront promenades at sea level and glass-floored walkways, giving Seascape over 13,000 square metres of open deck.

As the second Seaside EVO she is the stretched version — 339 metres against 323, 170,412 gross tons against 153,516, and an additional deck over the original Seaside pair. The signature detail is the Bridge of Sighs, a glass-bottomed span cantilevered out over the ship's side eighteen decks above the water.
She was delivered by Fincantieri at Monfalcone in November 2022 and went straight onto year-round Caribbean itineraries from Miami, where the outdoor-first layout makes the most obvious sense.

ships.name: MSC Seashore
ships.headline: The first Seaside EVO — the Seaside hull stretched by sixteen metres.
ships.identity: MSC Seashore opened the Seaside EVO sub-class in 2021, taking the outdoor-focused Seaside design to 339 metres and 170,412 GT.

variants.name: Seaside EVO
variants.note: Lead ship of the stretched sub-class, followed by MSC Seascape. Built at Monfalcone rather than Trieste, with an extra deck and a longer hull than the original Seaside pair.

operators.note: Caribbean and Mediterranean seasons.

prose: MSC Seashore is where the Seaside platform grew up. Fincantieri took the 2017 design, inserted a sixteen-metre section and an additional deck, and produced a ship of 170,412 gross tons — a little over 10 per cent larger than the original pair without redrawing the naval architecture.

The extra length went mostly into public space rather than cabins: a longer waterfront promenade, more aft terracing, and an enlarged Yacht Club. The result keeps the defining Seaside characteristic, which is how much of the ship faces outward rather than inward.

Delivered in July 2021 into a market still recovering from the shutdown, she has since alternated between Caribbean winters and Mediterranean summers, with sister ship MSC Seascape following a year behind her.

ships.name: MSC Seaview
ships.headline: The second Seaside ship — built for sunshine, sent to South America.
ships.identity: MSC Seaview repeats the 153,516 GT Seaside hull, with the same wraparound waterfront promenades and glass-floored skywalks aimed squarely at warm-weather cruising.

variants.name: Seaside class
variants.note: Second of the original pair with MSC Seaside, before the stretched Seaside EVO ships Seashore and Seascape.

operators.note: Mediterranean summers and South American seasons out of Santos.

prose: MSC Seaview is the sister to MSC Seaside and the second ship built on the design that reoriented MSC's thinking about deck space. The Seaside concept borrows from resort architecture rather than ocean liners: two waterfront promenades at sea level, a wraparound boardwalk, and glass-floored bridges that let passengers look straight down the hull's side.

At 153,516 gross tons and 323 metres she is the smaller of the two Seaside generations, delivered in June 2018 — the third new MSC ship in twelve months during the company's most aggressive expansion phase.
She has spent much of her career on Mediterranean summers and South American winters out of Santos, an itinerary pattern that suits a ship whose best spaces are all outdoors.

ships.name: MSC Seaside
ships.headline: The ship that turned a cruise ship inside out.
ships.identity: MSC Seaside led a class designed around exterior space rather than an enclosed promenade — the first MSC ship built specifically for the North American market.

variants.name: Seaside class
variants.note: Lead ship of four: Seaside (2017), Seaview (2018), then the stretched EVO pair Seashore (2021) and Seascape (2022).

operators.note: Debuted year-round from PortMiami; MSC's first ship designed for the Caribbean market.

prose: MSC Seaside was a deliberate break from how large cruise ships were being drawn in the 2010s. Instead of pulling life inward into a covered promenade, Fincantieri and MSC pushed it outward: waterfront promenades at sea level running most of the ship's length, a wraparound boardwalk, glass-floored skywalks over the hull's edge, and an aft that steps down in terraces to the water.

The design brief was geographic. Seaside was MSC's first ship built specifically for the Caribbean out of Miami, a market where an enclosed weather-proof main street solves a problem that does not exist, and where deck space is the product.

She entered service in December 2017 at 153,516 gross tons and founded a class of four. The idea proved durable enough that the stretched Seaside EVO ships repeated it almost unchanged five years later — unusual in a segment where each generation normally rethinks the layout entirely.

ships.name: Carnival Celebration
ships.headline: The second Excel-class ship — joint-largest in the Carnival fleet, and named for the line's 50th year.
ships.identity: Carnival Celebration shares the 183,521 GT Excel hull with Carnival Jubilee, and arrived in 2022 as the ship marking Carnival Cruise Line's fiftieth anniversary.

variants.name: Excel class
variants.note: Second of the class after Mardi Gras (2020), followed by Carnival Jubilee (2023). A subclass of Carnival Corporation's wider Excellence-class platform, shared with AIDA and Costa.

variants.name: Fifty years
variants.note: Delivered in Carnival Cruise Line's fiftieth-anniversary year and named for the 1987 Holiday-class Celebration.

operators.note: Caribbean itineraries from PortMiami; arrived via a 14-day transatlantic from Southampton.

prose: Carnival Celebration is the middle ship of the Excel class and, with Carnival Jubilee, the joint-largest Carnival has ever operated at 183,521 gross tons. She is a near-identical repeat of Mardi Gras with roughly 1,700 more tons of internal volume and ninety more passengers at double occupancy.

The Excel platform is built around zones rather than a single promenade: six themed areas including Celebration Central, a two-deck venue that doubles as theatre and nightclub, and Grand Central, an atrium with three-deck windows facing the sea rather than inward. On the top deck runs BOLT, the roller coaster that Mardi Gras introduced as the first at sea.

She is also the second LNG ship in the fleet. Carnival Corporation committed early to LNG across its brands — AIDAnova came first in 2018 — and the Excel ships are the Carnival Cruise Line expression of that platform. Celebration crossed from Southampton on a fourteen-day maiden transatlantic in November 2022 and has worked Miami ever since.

ships.name: Mardi Gras
ships.headline: Carnival's first LNG ship, first Excel-class ship, and the first ship anywhere with a roller coaster.
ships.identity: Mardi Gras opened the Excel class in 2020 at 181,808 GT — Carnival's first LNG-powered ship, and deliberately named without the Carnival prefix after the line's original 1972 vessel.

variants.name: Excel class
variants.note: Lead ship of the class, followed by Carnival Celebration (2022) and Carnival Jubilee (2023), with two more ordered for 2027 and 2028.

variants.name: The name
variants.note: Carnival was explicit that she is Mardi Gras, not Carnival Mardi Gras — a deliberate nod to the line's original 1972 ship of 27,200 GT.

operators.note: Caribbean itineraries from Port Canaveral, Florida.

prose: Mardi Gras is the ship that reset what a Carnival ship is. Before her the fleet's largest was Carnival Panorama at 133,500 gross tons; she arrived at 181,808 GT — roughly 40 per cent larger — and brought LNG propulsion, a zoned layout and a top-deck roller coaster with her.

BOLT is the detail everyone remembers: an 800-foot electric coaster track running around the funnel, the first roller coaster fitted to a ship, with riders controlling their own speed. It reads as a gimmick and largely is one, but it did something useful for Carnival — it gave a mass-market brand a genuinely unmistakable silhouette.

Her introduction was badly disrupted. Delivered from Meyer Turku in December 2020 into a shut-down industry, she sat for over seven months before finally sailing from Port Canaveral on 31 July 2021 — one of the first US-based ships back in service. She carries no Carnival prefix, a deliberate reference to the line's original 1972 Mardi Gras, a converted transatlantic liner of 27,200 tons that started the whole company.

ships.name: Carnival Vista
ships.headline: The class that carried Carnival through the 2010s — and the first ship with a bike track in the sky.
ships.identity: Carnival Vista led a three-ship class at 133,500 GT, stretching the Dream platform by eighteen metres and adding the SkyRide suspended cycle track.

variants.name: Vista class
variants.note: Lead ship of three for Carnival — Vista (2016), Horizon (2018), Panorama (2019). The design was also built for Costa and Adora Cruises, and spawned the Venice sub-class.

operators.note: Caribbean itineraries from Galveston and Port Canaveral.

prose: Carnival Vista was the fleet's flagship for four years and the last generation before the Excel ships changed the scale entirely. Fincantieri took the Dream-class hull, added about eighteen metres, redesigned the stern and the atrium, and produced 133,500 gross tons of ship.

The signature feature is SkyRide, a suspended recumbent-cycle track running a lap of the top deck with the sea directly below — Carnival's habit of putting a ride rather than a view at the top of the ship, which BOLT later took further. She also carried the first brewery at sea on a North American ship, brewing on board rather than serving in bottles.
Three ships followed the design at Carnival and several more across Carnival Corporation's other brands, making Vista one of the most reproduced cruise-ship platforms of the decade.

ships.name: Carnival Horizon
ships.headline: The second Vista-class ship — same hull, an IMAX theatre and a Dr. Seuss water park.
ships.identity: Carnival Horizon repeats the 133,500 GT Vista platform, delivered in 2018 and christened by Queen Latifah.

variants.name: Vista class
variants.note: Second of three Carnival ships on the platform, between Carnival Vista (2016) and Carnival Panorama (2019).

operators.note: Caribbean itineraries from Miami.

prose: Carnival Horizon is the second Vista-class ship and close to a straight repeat of her lead — the same 133,500 gross tons, the same 15 decks, the same SkyRide loop around the top deck.

The differences are in the fit-out rather than the naval architecture: an IMAX theatre, a Havana section of Cuban-themed cabins with their own pool and bar, and a Dr. Seuss-branded water park, part of a licensing arrangement Carnival has run across the fleet since 2014.

She was christened at New York in 2018 by Queen Latifah, and has worked Caribbean itineraries out of Miami for most of her career — the reliable middle of a fleet whose extremes are now the Excel ships above her and the ageing Fantasy-class ships below.

ships.name: Carnival Panorama
ships.headline: The third Vista-class ship — and the first new Carnival ship on the West Coast in twenty-five years.
ships.identity: Carnival Panorama closed the Vista class in 2019 and was placed at Long Beach, the first purpose-built Carnival ship for the California market since the 1990s.

variants.name: Vista class
variants.note: Third and final Carnival ship of the class. Originally considered for P&O Cruises Australia before being assigned to Carnival.

variants.name: Sky Zone
variants.note: Carries a trampoline park in place of her sisters' IMAX theatre — the clearest interior difference within the class.

operators.note: Year-round Mexican Riviera itineraries from Long Beach, California.

prose: Carnival Panorama completed the Vista class in December 2019, and her deployment mattered more than her specification. Carnival placed her at Long Beach on year-round Mexican Riviera runs — the first brand-new Carnival ship based on the US West Coast in about twenty-five years.
Structurally she is a Vista: 133,500 gross tons, fifteen decks, SkyRide overhead, the Havana cabins aft. The notable swap is a Sky Zone trampoline park where Vista and Horizon carry an IMAX screen.

She was also the last Carnival ship delivered before the pandemic, entering service barely three months before the industry stopped — and the last of the pre-Excel generation, which makes her a useful marker of where Carnival's thinking stood before the LNG mega-ships arrived.

ships.name: Carnival Dream
ships.headline: The lead Dream-class ship — Carnival's largest for six years.
ships.identity: Carnival Dream opened a four-ship class in 2009 at around 128,000 GT, and held the title of largest Carnival ship until Carnival Vista arrived in 2016.

variants.name: Dream class
variants.note: Lead ship of four: Carnival Dream (2009), Carnival Magic (2011), Carnival Breeze (2012) and Costa Diadema (2014), which went to the Costa brand.

operators.note: Caribbean itineraries from Galveston, Texas.

prose: Carnival Dream is where the modern Carnival ship took shape. She evolved directly from the Conquest class but grew to about 128,000 gross tons and 306 metres, adding a half-mile open-air promenade wrapped around Deck 5 with bars and restaurants opening onto it — a feature the line has repeated on every large ship since.

She was the largest ship Carnival had ever operated for six years, until Carnival Vista displaced her in 2016, and remains a good illustration of how quickly the ceiling has moved: Dream would now be the eighth or ninth largest ship in her own fleet.

Three sisters followed, one of them — Costa Diadema — delivered to Carnival Corporation's Italian brand rather than to Carnival Cruise Line, which is a recurring pattern across the corporation's shared platforms.

ships.name: Carnival Magic
ships.headline: The second Dream-class ship — and the one that introduced the ropes course at sea.
ships.identity: Carnival Magic repeats the 306-metre Dream hull, delivered in 2011 with SportSquare, the top-deck ropes course Carnival has fitted fleet-wide since.

variants.name: Dream class
variants.note: Second of four, between Carnival Dream (2009) and Carnival Breeze (2012).

variants.name: SportSquare
variants.note: First Carnival ship with the outdoor ropes course and sports deck, since retrofitted across much of the fleet.

operators.note: Caribbean itineraries from Norfolk, Virginia and Port Canaveral.

prose: Carnival Magic is the middle Dream-class ship and the one that added the feature Carnival has since put nearly everywhere: SportSquare, an open-air sports deck with a suspended ropes course running above it.

Otherwise she is her class — 306 metres, roughly 128,000 gross tons, the wraparound Deck 5 promenade, fourteen passenger decks. She also introduced Cucina del Capitano, an Italian restaurant themed around the ship's officers, and RedFrog Pub, both of which became fleet standards.
Carnival's habit of prototyping one new venue per delivery and then retrofitting the winners across the fleet is unusually visible in this class, and Magic is the clearest example of it.

ships.name: Carnival Breeze
ships.headline: The last Dream-class ship for Carnival — and the fleet's flagship until Vista arrived.
ships.identity: Carnival Breeze closed the Dream class for Carnival in 2012 and held flagship status for four years, the longest gap between new ships in the line's modern history.

variants.name: Dream class
variants.note: Third and last Dream-class ship built for Carnival Cruise Line; the fourth hull became Costa Diadema in 2014.

operators.note: Caribbean itineraries from Galveston, Texas.

prose: Carnival Breeze was the last of the Dream class delivered to Carnival Cruise Line, entering service in March 2012, and she then held flagship status for four years — the longest Carnival went without a new ship in its modern era.

She dropped the water park of her sisters in favour of a larger adults-only Serenity deck, and carried the debut of Guy's Burger Joint, the Guy Fieri partnership that has since been fitted to almost every ship in the fleet. Subsequent refits added Guy's Pig & Anchor BBQ and the Alchemy Bar.

By current standards she is mid-sized — about 128,000 gross tons against the Excel ships' 183,000 — but the Dream platform she completes is still the most numerous large-ship family Carnival operates, and the direct ancestor of the Vista class.

ships.name: Norwegian Encore
ships.headline: NCL's largest ship — the last Breakaway-Plus, with the biggest go-kart track at sea.
ships.identity: Norwegian Encore closed the Breakaway-Plus class in 2019 at 169,116 GT, and remains the largest ship Norwegian Cruise Line operates.

variants.name: Breakaway-Plus class
variants.note: Fourth and final ship of the sub-class, after Escape (2015), Joy (2017) and Bliss (2018). The class grew roughly 4,000 GT with each hull.

variants.name: Hull art
variants.note: Her hull was painted to a design by Catalan artist Eduardo Arranz-Bravo, continuing NCL's practice of commissioning artists rather than using corporate livery.

operators.note: Caribbean and Alaska seasons; christened by Kelly Clarkson in November 2019.

prose: Norwegian Encore is the biggest ship Norwegian Cruise Line has ever operated, at 169,116 gross tons, and the last of a four-ship run that grew steadily larger without ever changing its underlying architecture — each Breakaway-Plus hull added a few thousand tons over the one before.

The feature she is known for sits on the top deck: a two-level electric go-kart circuit, the largest at sea, running over 300 metres of track above the funnel line. NCL introduced racetracks on Norwegian Joy for the Chinese market and then kept them, which is why the class ends with a ship whose most photographed feature is a road.

Below that she is a conventional Meyer Werft product — twenty decks, 3,998 lower berths, around 2,100 crew, and The Haven, NCL's keycard-only suite enclave forward. Delivered in October 2019, she had four months of service before the industry shut down, and now works Caribbean winters and Alaska summers.

ships.name: Norwegian Bliss
ships.headline: The Breakaway-Plus ship built for Alaska — and the first with a racetrack designed in from the start.
ships.identity: Norwegian Bliss arrived in 2018 at 168,028 GT, purpose-configured for Alaska with an observation lounge wrapping the bow and a Wyland whale mural down the hull.

variants.name: Breakaway-Plus class
variants.note: Third of four, between Norwegian Joy (2017) and Norwegian Encore (2019).

variants.name: Built for Alaska
variants.note: Specified with improved energy efficiency to meet Alaskan environmental requirements, and given an oversized Observation Lounge for glacier viewing.

operators.note: Alaska summers from Seattle, Caribbean and Mexican Riviera in winter.

prose: Norwegian Bliss is the Breakaway-Plus ship built with a specific coastline in mind. NCL specified her for Alaska: a two-deck Observation Lounge wrapping the bow with floor-to-ceiling glass, and an efficiency package chosen to meet the environmental rules that govern ships working Alaskan waters.

Her hull carries a humpback whale mural by marine artist Wyland, spanning most of the ship's side — the largest of the artist-designed hulls NCL has commissioned, and a deliberate signal about where she was going to work.

At 168,028 gross tons she sits second in the fleet behind Norwegian Encore, and carries the same two-level go-kart track and Haven suite complex as her sisters. She has spent her career alternating Seattle-based Alaska summers with Caribbean and Mexican Riviera winters.

ships.name: Norwegian Joy
ships.headline: Built for China, rebuilt for America — the ship that introduced go-karts to cruising.
ships.identity: Norwegian Joy was designed specifically for the Chinese market in 2017, then substantially reconfigured for North America two years later when that market did not materialise.

variants.name: Breakaway-Plus class
variants.note: Second of four. Sister to Norwegian Escape, Bliss and Encore.

variants.name: The China refit
variants.note: Originally fitted with karaoke rooms, a tea house and expanded retail for Chinese guests; redeployed to Alaska and Mexico in 2019 after a substantial North America refit, with a further adults-focused refurbishment in 2024.

operators.note: Alaska, Panama Canal and Caribbean itineraries after redeployment from Shanghai and Tianjin.

prose: Norwegian Joy is the most interesting ship in the Breakaway-Plus class because she was built for a market that never arrived. NCL specified her for China — karaoke suites, a tea house, a different retail mix, different dining rhythms — and based her at Shanghai and Tianjin from 2017.

The Chinese cruise market contracted rather than grew, and in 2019 NCL pulled her out, sent her for a substantial refit and redeployed her to Alaska and the Panama Canal. Much of what made her China-specific was removed; a further 2024 refurbishment pushed her further toward adult travellers with an expanded Vibe Beach Club and thermal spa.

Her lasting contribution is the go-kart track. It was specified for Joy as a Chinese-market attraction, proved popular enough to keep, and has since appeared on Bliss, Encore, Prima and Viva — an idea that outlived the reason it existed.

ships.name: Norwegian Escape
ships.headline: The first Breakaway-Plus ship — and the one that carries the most passengers in the fleet.
ships.identity: Norwegian Escape opened the Breakaway-Plus sub-class in 2015 and, at 4,266 guests at double occupancy, still carries more people than any other NCL ship.

variants.name: Breakaway-Plus class
variants.note: Lead ship of the sub-class. Closest of the four to the original Breakaway design; no go-kart track, laser tag or splash park, which is why she fits more cabins.

operators.note: Caribbean and Europe; christened by Pitbull in Miami in November 2015.

prose: Norwegian Escape started the Breakaway-Plus class, and there is a neat trade-off buried in her specification: she is the smallest of the four by tonnage but carries the most passengers of any ship in the NCL fleet, 4,266 at double occupancy.

The reason is what she does not have. Her sisters gave over large areas of the upper decks to a go-kart circuit, a laser-tag arena and a water park; Escape put cabins there instead. She is the most conventionally efficient ship of the group.

She was christened in Miami by Pitbull in 2015, carries a Guy Harvey marine mural across her hull, and has alternated Caribbean and European seasons since. As the transition ship between the Breakaway and Breakaway-Plus designs she is also the clearest illustration of how little the underlying naval architecture changed across seven years and eight hulls.

ships.name: Norwegian Luna
ships.headline: The second Prima-Plus ship — sister to Norwegian Aqua, delivered a year behind her.
ships.identity: Norwegian Luna repeats the stretched 156,300 GT Prima-Plus hull, arriving in 2026 as the fourth ship of the Prima family.

variants.name: Prima-Plus class
variants.note: Second of the stretched sub-class after Norwegian Aqua (2025), roughly 14,000 GT larger than the original Prima pair. Two further Prima-Plus-Plus ships of 172,000 GT follow from 2027.

operators.note: Caribbean itineraries; debuted April 2026.

prose: Norwegian Luna is the second Prima-Plus ship and a close repeat of Norwegian Aqua, delivered by Fincantieri in March 2026 and in service the following month.

The Prima-Plus ships are the stretched version of the Prima platform — about 14,000 gross tons larger than Norwegian Prima and Viva, with the extra volume going into public rooms and an enlarged spa rather than a proportional increase in berths. The result is a ship carrying roughly 3,570 guests in the space some lines would use for 4,500.

Her additions over Aqua are mostly programming rather than structure: a larger Mandara Spa, a carnival-themed games area, and new production shows. Two further ships of 172,000 GT — a second stretch again — follow from 2027, which will make the Prima family NCL's largest ever by some margin.

ships.name: Norwegian Epic
ships.headline: A class of one — the strangest large cruise ship of her generation.
ships.identity: Norwegian Epic arrived in 2010 at 155,873 GT as a one-off, and is remembered as much for her wave-shaped cabin walls as for her size.

variants.name: Epic class
variants.note: Intended as the lead ship of a pair; the second was never ordered, leaving her a class of one between the Jewel class and the Breakaway class.

variants.name: The curved cabins
variants.note: Her standard cabins used a wave-shaped partition and a split bathroom with separate toilet and shower pods either side of the entrance — widely disliked, and not repeated on any later NCL ship.

operators.note: Mediterranean summers and Caribbean winters.

prose: Norwegian Epic is the most experimental large cruise ship of her era, and the clearest example of a line trying several new ideas at once and discovering which ones passengers actually wanted.

Some worked and became standard: The Haven, NCL's keycard-gated suite complex, appeared here first and is now on every large ship in the fleet; so did studio cabins designed and priced for solo travellers, with their own shared lounge — genuinely novel in 2010 and since copied widely.

Others did not. Her standard cabins used a curved wave-shaped wall and split the bathroom into separate toilet and shower pods flanking the entrance, with frosted glass between. It saved space and was almost universally disliked, and no subsequent NCL ship repeated it. A planned sister was never ordered, leaving Epic as a class of one at 155,873 gross tons — for five years the largest ship NCL had.

ships.name: Norwegian Getaway
ships.headline: The second Breakaway-class ship — Miami's ship, with a Miami hull to match.
ships.identity: Norwegian Getaway repeats the 145,655 GT Breakaway hull, delivered in 2014 and based at Miami with a David 'LEBO' Le Batard hull mural.

variants.name: Breakaway class
variants.note: Second of two for NCL with Norwegian Breakaway; the design then continued as the Breakaway-Plus sub-class and the Genting Dream class.

operators.note: Caribbean itineraries from Miami; Europe in summer.

prose: Norwegian Getaway is Norwegian Breakaway's sister and near-identical twin — the same 324-metre Meyer Werft hull, the same 145,655 gross tons, the same eighteen decks and the same Waterfront, an outdoor boardwalk running around Deck 8 with restaurants opening straight onto it.

Where Breakaway was themed on New York, Getaway was themed on Miami, with a hull mural by Miami artist David 'LEBO' Le Batard and an interior leaning on the city's colour palette. NCL's habit of tying each ship to its homeport city was at its most literal in this pair.

She has worked Caribbean itineraries from Miami for most of her career, with European seasons interspersed, and remains a good demonstration of how durable the Breakaway platform proved: eight hulls across three sub-classes and three operators over six years.

ships.name: Norwegian Breakaway
ships.headline: The lead Breakaway-class ship — and the platform NCL built eight hulls on.
ships.identity: Norwegian Breakaway opened the most productive class in NCL's history in 2013, introducing the outdoor Waterfront promenade that every later NCL ship has carried.

variants.name: Breakaway class
variants.note: Lead ship of a family that ran to eight hulls: two Breakaway, four Breakaway-Plus, and the Genting Dream ships built on the same platform for other operators.

variants.name: The Waterfront
variants.note: Introduced the quarter-mile outdoor promenade on Deck 8 with bars and restaurants opening onto it — since carried by every subsequent NCL ship.

operators.note: Originally New York year-round; now Caribbean and European itineraries.

prose: Norwegian Breakaway matters more than her size suggests. She introduced the platform that NCL then built on for six straight years — two Breakaway ships, four Breakaway-Plus ships, and three more hulls for Genting's own brands — making it the most reproduced design the line has ever ordered.

The idea that carried forward is The Waterfront: a quarter-mile outdoor promenade wrapped around Deck 8, with restaurants and bars opening directly onto it rather than facing inward. Before it, large ships were pulling social space inside; Breakaway pushed it back out to the rail, and every NCL ship since has kept it.

She was built for New York, with a Peter Max hull design and a Rockettes-themed interior, and ran year-round from Manhattan — an unusual commitment for a ship of that size in a seasonal climate. She has since moved to Caribbean and European deployments.

ships.name: Norwegian Viva
ships.headline: The second Prima-class ship — NCL going smaller and more spacious on purpose.
ships.identity: Norwegian Viva repeats the 142,500 GT Prima hull delivered in 2023, part of a class deliberately smaller than the Breakaway-Plus ships that preceded it.

variants.name: Prima class
variants.note: Second of two original Prima ships, before the stretched Prima-Plus pair Aqua (2025) and Luna (2026).

operators.note: Mediterranean summers and Caribbean winters.

prose: Norwegian Viva is the second Prima-class ship and, with her lead sister, represents a genuine reversal of direction: after four Breakaway-Plus ships that grew steadily to 169,116 gross tons, NCL went back down to 142,500 and cut passenger numbers by nearly a quarter.

The trade was space per person. The Prima ships carry around 3,100 guests where the Breakaway-Plus ships carry close to 4,000, and the reclaimed volume went into wider outdoor decks, a three-deck glass atrium and Ocean Boulevard, a promenade wrapping the entire ship at Deck 8.

She keeps the three-level go-kart track and adds a free-fall dry slide, but the intent of the class is calmer than that suggests — the reviews that matter to NCL were the ones about crowding, and this is the design answer to them.

ships.name: Norwegian Prima
ships.headline: The class that reversed the arms race — NCL's first Fincantieri ship, and deliberately smaller.
ships.identity: Norwegian Prima led a new class in 2022 at 142,500 GT, ending NCL's twelve-year run of ever-larger ships in favour of more space per passenger.

variants.name: Prima class
variants.note: Lead ship of a six-ship programme: Prima (2022), Viva (2023), the stretched Prima-Plus pair Aqua (2025) and Luna (2026), and two Prima-Plus-Plus ships of 172,000 GT from 2027.

variants.name: Project Leonardo
variants.note: Developed under the project name Leonardo, and the first NCL ships built by Fincantieri rather than Meyer Werft.

operators.note: Northern Europe and Caribbean; christened in Reykjavík in August 2022.

prose: Norwegian Prima is the ship where NCL stopped chasing tonnage. Every class since 2010 had been larger than the last, ending with Norwegian Encore at 169,116 gross tons and nearly 4,000 passengers. Prima came in at 142,500 GT carrying around 3,100 — smaller, and deliberately so.

What the reduction bought is visible everywhere on board: Ocean Boulevard, a promenade circling the entire ship at Deck 8 with infinity pools cantilevered over the sides; a three-deck atrium with a glass sculpture rather than a shopping arcade; and the widest outdoor deck space per passenger in the fleet.

She was also the first NCL ship built by Fincantieri, ending a long relationship with Meyer Werft, and the first of six under the Prima programme. Christened in Reykjavík in August 2022, she signalled a strategy that the industry has broadly followed since — that beyond a certain size, the selling point stops being how much ship there is and becomes how much of it you can actually use.

ships.name: Sun Princess
ships.headline: The lead Sphere-class ship — the largest Princess has ever built, and its first on LNG.
ships.identity: Sun Princess opened the Sphere class in 2024 at 177,882 GT, built around a geodesic glass dome that gives the class its name.

variants.name: Sphere class
variants.note: Lead ship of two with Star Princess (2025) — the largest ships ever built for Princess, and the largest cruise ships built in Italy at the time of order.

variants.name: The Dome
variants.note: A geodesic glass structure on Deck 17, inspired by Santorini, working as a pool deck by day and a theatre for aerial performance by night. It gives the class its name.

operators.note: Mediterranean and Caribbean seasons; christened by Hannah Waddingham in April 2024.

prose: Sun Princess is the largest ship Princess Cruises has ever operated and the first it has run on LNG — a jump of more than 30,000 gross tons over the Royal class that preceded her, and the point at which Princess finally built at the scale its parent company's other brands had reached years earlier.

The design is organised around The Dome, a geodesic glass structure sitting high on the ship that Princess describes as inspired by the cliff architecture of Santorini. It is a pool deck in daylight and converts to a theatre for aerial and acrobatic performance after dark — an attempt to solve the problem that large glass spaces at sea usually sit empty in the evening.

She also carries a three-deck Piazza atrium spanning the ship's width, twenty-one decks and around 4,300 guests at double occupancy. Delivered from Fincantieri at Monfalcone in February 2024, she was joined by sister Star Princess the following year.

ships.name: Discovery Princess
ships.headline: The last Royal-class ship — Princess's flagship until the Sphere class arrived.
ships.identity: Discovery Princess closed the Royal class in 2022 at 145,281 GT and held flagship status for two years until Sun Princess displaced her.

variants.name: Royal class
variants.note: Sixth and final Princess ship of the class, which also produced P&O Cruises' Britannia. The last three — Sky, Enchanted and Discovery — share the enlarged 145,281 GT specification.

operators.note: Alaska summers from Seattle and Mexican Riviera winters from Los Angeles.

prose: Discovery Princess is the last of six Royal-class ships and the most refined version of a design Princess ran for nine years. She arrived in March 2022 at 145,281 gross tons — roughly 2,500 more than the class lead — and was the fleet's largest ship until Sun Princess arrived two years later.

The Royal-class signature is the Piazza, a three-deck atrium at the ship's centre with the shops and cafés stacked around it, and the SeaWalk — a glass-floored walkway cantilevered out over the side of the ship, eighteen decks above the water.

Her delivery slipped by two years because of the pandemic, and she entered service into a market still recovering. She has since settled into the deployment pattern Princess is best known for: Alaska in summer out of Seattle, Mexican Riviera in winter out of Los Angeles.

ships.name: Enchanted Princess
ships.headline: The fifth Royal-class ship — delivered into a shut-down industry and laid up for a year.
ships.identity: Enchanted Princess was handed over in 2020 with nowhere to sail, and did not carry a paying passenger until late 2021.

variants.name: Royal class
