variants.note: Fifth Princess ship of the class, sister to Sky Princess and Discovery Princess at the enlarged 145,281 GT specification.

operators.note: Caribbean itineraries from Fort Lauderdale; Europe in summer.

prose: Enchanted Princess is a useful reminder of how badly the 2020 shutdown hit shipbuilding schedules. Fincantieri completed her that year, Princess took delivery, and then she sat — christened remotely, with no itinerary to run, for well over a year before finally carrying paying passengers in late 2021.

Structurally she is the second of the three enlarged Royal-class ships at 145,281 gross tons, with the same Piazza atrium, the same cantilevered SeaWalk over the ship's side, and the same nineteen decks as Sky and Discovery.
The class as a whole is Princess's longest-running modern platform — six ships across nine years, plus P&O Cruises' Britannia on the same hull — and Enchanted sits in its most developed phase.

ships.name: Sky Princess
ships.headline: The fourth Royal-class ship — and the first of the enlarged 145,000-ton group.
ships.identity: Sky Princess arrived in 2019 as the first Royal-class ship built to the stretched 145,281 GT specification, with a Sky Suites complex added at the top of the ship.

variants.name: Royal class
variants.note: Fourth Princess ship of the class and the first at the enlarged 145,281 GT figure, followed by Enchanted and Discovery.

variants.name: Sky Suites
variants.note: Two top-deck suites with balconies of over 700 square feet — the largest balconies in the Princess fleet, and the feature she takes her name from.

operators.note: Caribbean winters from Fort Lauderdale, European summers.

prose: Sky Princess marks the point where the Royal class stopped being a straight repeat. The first three ships came in around 142,700 to 143,700 gross tons; Sky was built to 145,281, and the two ships that followed matched her.

The extra volume went upward. Her Sky Suites sit at the top of the ship with balconies over 700 square feet — the largest in the fleet, and directly above the theatre rather than aft, which is unusual placement for premium accommodation.

She was christened in 2019 by the surviving principal cast of The Love Boat, a piece of branding Princess has leaned on for decades — the show was filmed aboard Pacific Princess in the late 1970s and did more for the line's name recognition than any advertising campaign since.

ships.name: Majestic Princess
ships.headline: The Royal-class ship built for China — and later redeployed to Australia.
ships.identity: Majestic Princess was the third Royal-class ship, specified for the Chinese market in 2017 and reassigned when that market did not develop.

variants.name: Royal class
variants.note: Third Princess ship of the class, between Regal Princess (2014) and Sky Princess (2019).

variants.name: Built for China
variants.note: Fitted with a hot-pot restaurant, expanded duty-free retail and Mandarin-language service for a Shanghai deployment; redeployed to Australia and North America after the Chinese market contracted.

operators.note: Australia and New Zealand seasons from Sydney; Alaska in the northern summer.

prose: Majestic Princess is the Princess equivalent of Norwegian Joy — a large ship specified in detail for the Chinese market at the moment the industry was convinced China would become its largest growth region, and redeployed when it did not.

Her China fit-out included a hot-pot restaurant, a substantially enlarged retail area and Mandarin-language service throughout, and she was based at Shanghai from 2017. Princess pulled her out and sent her to Australia and North America, where she works Sydney-based seasons and Alaska summers.

Underneath the deployment story she is a standard Royal-class ship: 330 metres, the three-deck Piazza atrium, the glass-floored SeaWalk projecting over the ship's side, and around 3,560 guests at double occupancy.

ships.name: Regal Princess
ships.headline: The second Royal-class ship — sister to the class lead, a year behind her.
ships.identity: Regal Princess repeats the 142,714 GT Royal Princess hull, delivered in 2014 and christened by the original cast of The Love Boat.

variants.name: Royal class
variants.note: Second of six Princess ships on the platform, sister to Royal Princess (2013).

operators.note: Caribbean and Northern Europe itineraries.

prose: Regal Princess is the second Royal-class ship and a direct repeat of her lead sister at 142,714 gross tons — the same 330-metre hull, the same Piazza, the same pair of SeaWalk and SeaView glass structures projecting from either side of the ship.

She was christened in 2014 by the principal cast of The Love Boat, the television series filmed aboard Princess ships in the late 1970s that gave the line a level of American name recognition it has traded on ever since.

Her career has been split between Caribbean winters and Northern European summers, and she remains one of the more heavily deployed ships in a fleet Princess pruned considerably during the pandemic — five ships left the fleet permanently in 2020 and 2021.

ships.name: Royal Princess
ships.headline: The class lead that gave Princess the SeaWalk — and its modern shape.
ships.identity: Royal Princess opened the Royal class in 2013 at 142,714 GT, the largest Princess ship of its day and the first with a glass walkway cantilevered over the sea.

variants.name: Royal class
variants.note: Lead ship of six for Princess plus P&O Cruises' Britannia — the most productive design in the line's history, built from 2013 to 2022.

variants.name: The SeaWalk
variants.note: A glass-floored walkway extending roughly 8.5 metres out beyond the ship's side, 39 metres above the water. Introduced here and carried by every Royal-class ship since.

operators.note: Caribbean and Mediterranean itineraries; christened by the Duchess of Cambridge in Southampton, June 2013.

prose: Royal Princess is the ship that set the shape of the modern Princess fleet. She arrived in 2013 at 142,714 gross tons — around 27,000 more than the largest Grand-class ship — and every Princess ship built in the following decade descended from her.

Two ideas came with her. The Piazza, a three-deck atrium at the centre of the ship with cafés and shops stacked around it, replaced the traditional single-height lobby. And the SeaWalk, a glass-floored walkway cantilevered about 8.5 metres beyond the hull and 39 metres above the water, gave Princess a feature no competitor had.
She was christened at Southampton in June 2013 by the Duchess of Cambridge, in her first public engagement after her wedding — and the third Princess ship to carry the Royal name.

ships.name: Diamond Princess
ships.headline: Japanese-built, and the widest of the Grand-class ships — with a name swapped after a shipyard fire.
ships.identity: Diamond Princess is one of only two Princess ships built in Japan, and carries the name originally assigned to her sister after a 2002 fire at Nagasaki forced the hulls to be exchanged.

variants.name: Gem class
variants.note: A Grand-class sub-class of two — Diamond and Sapphire Princess — the only Princess ships built by Mitsubishi at Nagasaki, and the widest Grand-class hulls at 37.5 m against 36 m.

variants.name: The name swap
variants.note: A fire during construction in October 2002 damaged the hull then intended to be Diamond Princess. The two ships' names were exchanged so the undamaged hull could be delivered on schedule.

operators.note: Japan and Asia in the northern summer, Australia in the southern summer.

prose: Diamond Princess is unusual in the Princess fleet on two counts: she was built in Japan rather than Italy, and she is not, strictly, the ship that was meant to carry her name.

Mitsubishi Heavy Industries built her and her sister at Nagasaki. In October 2002 a fire swept through the hull then designated Diamond Princess, causing extensive damage. Rather than delay both ships, the names were exchanged — the undamaged hull became Diamond Princess and was delivered in 2004, while the repaired one became Sapphire Princess.

At 37.5 metres she and her sister are the widest Grand-class ships, a metre and a half broader than the Fincantieri-built hulls, which gives them a noticeably different profile. She has spent most of her career working Japan and Asia in the northern summer and Australia in the southern, and was re-registered from Bermuda to London in 2014.

ships.name: Sapphire Princess
ships.headline: The second Japanese-built Princess — the repaired hull that changed its name.
ships.identity: Sapphire Princess is the sister to Diamond Princess and the hull originally intended to carry that name, rebuilt after a fire at Nagasaki in 2002.

variants.name: Gem class
variants.note: Second of the two Mitsubishi-built Grand-class ships, sharing the wider 37.5 m beam with Diamond Princess.

operators.note: Asia, Alaska and Australia deployments.

prose: Sapphire Princess is the other half of the Nagasaki pair, and the hull that was originally to be called Diamond Princess before an October 2002 fire at Mitsubishi Heavy Industries forced a rethink.

The damaged sections were rebuilt, the two ships exchanged names so the intact hull could be delivered on time, and Sapphire Princess entered service in 2004 — a few months behind her sister but otherwise identical.

Like Diamond she carries the 37.5-metre beam that makes the Gem-class pair the widest Grand-class ships, and she has worked Asian, Alaskan and Australian itineraries throughout her career. Both ships were re-flagged from Bermuda to the United Kingdom in 2014.

ships.name: Ruby Princess
ships.headline: The last Grand-class ship — the ninth and final hull of an eleven-year programme.
ships.identity: Ruby Princess closed the Grand class for Princess in 2008 at 113,561 GT, and was the fleet's largest ship until Royal Princess arrived five years later.

variants.name: Crown class
variants.note: Third of three modified Grand-class ships with Crown Princess (2006) and Emerald Princess (2007), distinguished by the nightclub moved aft of the funnel rather than suspended above the stern.

variants.name: Grand class
variants.note: The ninth and last Grand-class ship delivered to Princess, closing a programme that ran from 1998.

operators.note: Caribbean, Alaska and Panama Canal itineraries.

prose: Ruby Princess is the last of the Grand class, a programme that ran from 1998 to 2008 and produced nine ships for Princess plus two more for P&O — the design that carried the line through a decade of expansion.

She belongs to the final Crown sub-class, which made a visible change to the Grand-class silhouette: the nightclub, which on the earlier ships was a distinctive structure suspended above the stern like a spoiler, was moved forward to sit just aft of the funnel. It is the easiest way to date a Grand-class ship from a photograph.
At 113,561 gross tons she was Princess's largest ship for five years until Royal Princess displaced her in 2013, and she has since settled into Caribbean, Alaska and Panama Canal work.

ships.name: Caribbean Princess
ships.headline: The Grand-class ship with an extra deck — and the first with Movies Under the Stars.
ships.identity: Caribbean Princess added a full deck of cabins to the Grand-class hull in 2004, and introduced the poolside outdoor cinema that became a Princess signature.

variants.name: Caribbean class
variants.note: A one-ship Grand-class variant: the original hull with an additional cabin deck, the Riviera deck, making her larger than Grand, Golden and the original Star Princess.

variants.name: Movies Under the Stars
variants.note: The first Princess ship with the poolside outdoor cinema screen, since fitted across the entire fleet.

operators.note: Caribbean itineraries year-round, as the name implies.

prose: Caribbean Princess is a Grand-class ship with a deck inserted. Fincantieri added the Riviera deck — a full additional level of cabins — to the standard hull, which took her to 112,894 gross tons and gave her the largest passenger capacity in the Princess fleet until Royal Princess arrived nine years later.

She is more significant for what she introduced than for her size. Movies Under the Stars, the large outdoor screen above the main pool, debuted on her in 2004 and has since been fitted to every ship in the fleet — one of the few genuinely durable ideas in an industry that adds and abandons features constantly.
Built specifically for year-round Caribbean deployment, she has done almost exactly that for her entire career, working Florida-based itineraries with occasional repositioning to Europe.

ships.name: Grand Princess
ships.headline: The largest and most expensive cruise ship in the world when she launched in 1998.
ships.identity: Grand Princess led an eleven-ship class and was, at delivery, the biggest passenger ship ever built — and the first too wide for the original Panama Canal locks.

variants.name: Grand class
variants.note: Lead ship of eleven built between 1998 and 2010 for Princess, P&O Cruises and P&O Cruises Australia — the longest-running cruise ship class of its era.

variants.name: The spoiler
variants.note: Her Skywalkers nightclub originally sat in a structure suspended 45 metres above the stern, giving the early Grand-class ships an unmistakable profile. It was removed in a 2011 refit to cut weight and drag.

operators.note: Alaska summers and California-based itineraries.

prose: Grand Princess was the largest and most expensive passenger ship in the world when she entered service in May 1998 at 107,517 gross tons — and the first cruise ship too wide to transit the original Panama Canal locks, which was treated at the time as a significant statement of intent.

Her defining feature was the Skywalkers nightclub, housed in a structure suspended about 45 metres above the stern and projecting aft like a spoiler. It gave the early Grand-class ships a profile no other ship had. It was also heavy and aerodynamically awkward, and Princess removed it during a 2011 refit — so photographs of her before and after look like two different ships.

The class she started ran to eleven hulls over twelve years across three brands, making it one of the most successful cruise ship designs ever built. Nearly thirty years on she remains in service, the oldest ship in the Princess fleet, working Alaska summers and California itineraries.

ships.name: Celebrity Ascent
ships.headline: The fourth Edge-class ship — the stretched hull, fully worked out.
ships.identity: Celebrity Ascent arrived in 2023 at 140,600 GT on the twenty-metre-longer Edge hull, with the cantilevered Magic Carpet platform on her starboard side.

variants.name: Edge class
variants.note: Fourth of the class, sharing the stretched 327-metre hull with Celebrity Beyond. A sixth ship, Celebrity Xcite, follows in 2028.

operators.note: Caribbean itineraries from Fort Lauderdale.

prose: Celebrity Ascent is the fourth Edge-class ship and the second built on the lengthened hull — twenty metres longer than Edge and Apex, and about 10,000 gross tons larger, with the extra volume going into suites and dining rather than a proportional rise in passenger numbers.

The class feature is the Magic Carpet: a cantilevered platform roughly the size of a tennis court mounted on the starboard side, which moves vertically between decks and changes function as it goes — a tender platform at the waterline, a bar amidships, a restaurant at the top. It is the most genuinely unusual piece of engineering on any modern cruise ship.

Ascent refined rather than reinvented. The Edge design had already dispensed with the traditional wraparound promenade in favour of Infinite Verandas — cabins whose entire outer wall opens to the sea — and by the fourth ship the arrangement was settled enough that the changes were mostly to restaurants and the retreat-class suite complex.

ships.name: Celebrity Beyond
ships.headline: The third Edge-class ship — where the hull was stretched by twenty metres.
ships.identity: Celebrity Beyond broke from her two sisters in 2022 by adding twenty metres of hull and a full deck, taking the Edge class from 130,818 GT to 140,600 GT.

variants.name: Edge class
variants.note: Third of the class and the first with the stretched hull — 327 m against 306 m, and an additional deck over Edge and Apex.

operators.note: Mediterranean summers and Caribbean winters.

prose: Celebrity Beyond is where the Edge class changed shape. Celebrity took the 306-metre hull of Edge and Apex, inserted twenty metres and an extra deck, and arrived at 140,600 gross tons — the specification the two later ships have followed.

The additional length went into public space more than cabins: a two-deck Sunset Bar terraced at the stern, an expanded Retreat sundeck for suite guests, and a longer Grand Plaza. Passenger numbers rose by only a few hundred, which is the point — Celebrity positions itself above the mass-market lines on space per guest.

She carries the Magic Carpet like her sisters, the cantilevered moving platform on the starboard side, and the Infinite Veranda cabins whose outer wall folds down to open the room to the sea. Her interiors were led by designer Kelly Hoppen and architect Nate Berkus, which is unusual naming for a cruise ship and entirely deliberate.

ships.name: Celebrity Apex
ships.headline: The second Edge-class ship — finished in 2020, and left waiting fourteen months for passengers.
ships.identity: Celebrity Apex repeats the original 130,818 GT Edge hull, and was delivered into the industry shutdown with her debut pushed from April 2020 to June 2021.

variants.name: Edge class
variants.note: Second of the class, identical in size to Celebrity Edge at 130,818 GT before the hull was stretched for Beyond.

operators.note: Mediterranean and Northern Europe; Caribbean in winter.

prose: Celebrity Apex is the second and last Edge-class ship at the original size, sharing the 306-metre, 130,818-ton specification with her lead sister before Celebrity lengthened the design.

Her career started fourteen months late. She was scheduled to debut in April 2020, was finished on time, and then had nowhere to go — she was eventually christened in a virtual ceremony and did not carry paying passengers until June 2021, from Athens.

Everything that defines the class is present: the Magic Carpet moving platform on the starboard side, the Infinite Veranda cabins, the Grand Plaza atrium with its sculptural chandelier, and Eden, a three-deck glass-walled space at the stern that Celebrity has since reworked on later ships.

ships.name: Celebrity Edge
ships.headline: The ship that hung a moving platform off its side.
ships.identity: Celebrity Edge led a new class in 2018 with the Magic Carpet — a cantilevered platform that travels vertically up the ship's starboard side — and cabins with no fixed balcony wall.

variants.name: Edge class
variants.note: Lead ship of six, built 2018–2028. The first two are 306 m and 130,818 GT; from Celebrity Beyond the hull was stretched to 327 m and 140,600 GT.

variants.name: The Magic Carpet
variants.note: A cantilevered platform roughly 27 m by 12 m mounted on the starboard side, moving between deck 2 and deck 16 and changing function at each level — tender platform, bar, or restaurant.

operators.note: Caribbean and Mediterranean itineraries.

prose: Celebrity Edge is the most architecturally adventurous mainstream cruise ship of the last decade, and it starts with the thing bolted to her side. The Magic Carpet is a cantilevered platform about the size of a tennis court that rides vertically up and down the starboard hull, serving as a tender platform at sea level, a bar in the middle of the ship and a restaurant at the top.

The second departure is the Infinite Veranda. Instead of a balcony separated from the cabin by a sliding door, the entire outer wall of the room opens — the window drops, a bifold door retracts, and the balcony becomes part of the living space. It divided opinion sharply and Celebrity kept it anyway.

She also dropped the traditional wraparound promenade deck entirely, which is why her profile looks less like a conventional cruise ship than almost anything else afloat. Six ships have been ordered on the design, running to 2028 — so the gamble evidently paid off.

ships.name: Celebrity Reflection
ships.headline: The last Solstice-class ship — and the only one with an extra deck.
ships.identity: Celebrity Reflection closed the Solstice class in 2012 with an additional cabin deck her four sisters do not have, making her the largest of the group.

variants.name: Solstice class
variants.note: Fifth and final ship of the class, built with one more cabin deck than Solstice, Equinox, Eclipse and Silhouette — roughly 4,000 GT larger as a result.

operators.note: Caribbean and transatlantic itineraries.

prose: Celebrity Reflection is the odd one out in an otherwise uniform class. Meyer Werft built five Solstice-class ships between 2008 and 2012; the first four are effectively identical, and then Celebrity added a full deck of cabins to the last one.

The change took her to roughly 126,000 gross tons against the 122,000 of her sisters and gave her about 200 more passengers, at the cost of a slightly taller and less balanced profile — a trade the line has not repeated.

She keeps the class signature, the Lawn Club: half an acre of real growing grass on the top deck, maintained at sea. It remains the most impractical and most memorable idea in Celebrity's fleet, and the Solstice ships are the only ones that have it.

ships.name: Celebrity Silhouette
ships.headline: The fourth Solstice-class ship — and a regular from Southampton.
ships.identity: Celebrity Silhouette entered service in 2011 on the standard Solstice hull, and has spent much of her career on UK-based itineraries.

variants.name: Solstice class
variants.note: Fourth of five. The first four ships share the same 317-metre hull; only Reflection differs.

operators.note: Frequently deployed from Southampton for UK-market European itineraries.

prose: Celebrity Silhouette is the fourth Solstice-class ship and a close repeat of the three before her — 317 metres, around 122,000 gross tons, seventeen decks, and the Lawn Club growing real grass on the roof.

Her small departure from her sisters is the Lawn Club Grill, an outdoor grill restaurant built into the lawn area where the earlier ships used the space for games and events. It was popular enough to be retrofitted to the rest of the class.

She is the Solstice ship British passengers know best, having spent repeated seasons based at Southampton on European itineraries, and she went through the line's Revolution refit programme in 2020 — a fleet-wide modernisation that reworked cabins and public rooms across the older ships.

ships.name: Celebrity Eclipse
ships.headline: The Solstice-class ship that was sent to rescue stranded travellers before she ever carried a paying passenger.
ships.identity: Celebrity Eclipse entered service in 2010 — but her first voyage was an unscheduled run to Bilbao to collect British travellers stranded by the Icelandic ash cloud.

variants.name: Solstice class
variants.note: Third of five, between Celebrity Equinox (2009) and Celebrity Silhouette (2011).

variants.name: The ash cloud voyage
variants.note: In April 2010, before her naming ceremony, she was diverted to Bilbao to repatriate around 2,000 British travellers stranded when the Eyjafjallajökull eruption closed European airspace.

operators.note: European and Caribbean itineraries; frequently based at Southampton.

prose: Celebrity Eclipse has the best origin story of any ship in the Celebrity fleet. In April 2010 the Eyjafjallajökull eruption in Iceland closed European airspace and left tens of thousands of travellers stranded. Eclipse was newly delivered and had not yet been named.

Celebrity cancelled her naming ceremony, sent her to Bilbao instead, and brought roughly 2,000 stranded British travellers home to Southampton free of charge. Her first working voyage was a repatriation run, which is not a sentence that applies to many cruise ships.

Beyond that she is a standard Solstice: 317 metres, about 122,000 gross tons, seventeen decks, the half-acre Lawn Club of real grass on top, and the glass-blowing studio the class carried in partnership with the Corning Museum of Glass. She remains a Southampton regular.

ships.name: Celebrity Equinox
ships.headline: The second Solstice-class ship — and the one commanded by the first American woman to captain a mega-ship.
ships.identity: Celebrity Equinox followed her lead sister in 2009 on the same 122,000 GT hull, and later became the ship most associated with Captain Kate McCue.

variants.name: Solstice class
variants.note: Second of five, delivered a year after the class lead and effectively identical to her.

operators.note: Caribbean and European itineraries.

prose: Celebrity Equinox is the second Solstice-class ship and a straight repeat of the lead — the same 317-metre Meyer Werft hull, the same roughly 122,000 gross tons, the same Lawn Club of real turf on the top deck.

She is best known outside the specification sheet as the ship commanded by Kate McCue, who in 2015 became the first American woman to captain a mega cruise ship and who built an unusually large public following while doing it — a rare case of a specific ship becoming associated with a specific master.

Like the rest of the class she went through Celebrity's Revolution refit programme, a fleet-wide modernisation that reworked staterooms and public spaces across the older ships to bring them closer to the Edge-class standard.

ships.name: Celebrity Solstice
ships.headline: The ship that put half an acre of real grass on a roof at sea.
ships.identity: Celebrity Solstice led a five-ship class in 2008, the line's first post-Panamax ship, and introduced the Lawn Club — living turf on the top deck.

variants.name: Solstice class
variants.note: Lead ship of five built by Meyer Werft between 2008 and 2012 — the class that took Celebrity from around 91,000 GT to 122,000 GT.

variants.name: The Lawn Club
variants.note: Roughly half an acre of real growing grass on the top deck, irrigated and mown at sea. Unique to the Solstice class.

operators.note: Alaska summers and Australia and New Zealand seasons.

prose: Celebrity Solstice was a step change for the line — around 30,000 gross tons larger than the Millennium ships before her, and the first Celebrity ship too wide for the original Panama Canal locks.

What she is remembered for is the grass. The Lawn Club is roughly half an acre of real turf laid on the top deck, irrigated, drained and mown while the ship is under way. It is expensive, heavy and entirely impractical, and it gave Celebrity a feature no competitor was willing to copy.

She also carried a glass-blowing studio operated with the Corning Museum of Glass, with live demonstrations at sea. The class ran to five ships in four years and remains the backbone of the fleet by capacity; Solstice herself went through a 45-day modernisation refit in early 2026.

ships.name: Celebrity Constellation
ships.headline: The last Millennium-class ship — gas-turbine powered, and still working.
ships.identity: Celebrity Constellation closed the Millennium class in 2002, one of four mid-sized ships that remain the smallest ocean-going vessels in the fleet.

specs.label: Propulsion
specs.metric: Gas turbine electric, 2 × Mermaid azimuth pods
specs.imperial: Gas turbine electric, 2 × Mermaid azimuth pods

variants.name: Millennium class
variants.note: Fourth and last of the class, after Millennium (2000), Infinity (2001) and Summit (2001).

variants.name: The Celebrity prefix
variants.note: Originally delivered simply as Constellation; the Celebrity prefix was added later across the class.

operators.note: Mediterranean, Caribbean and repositioning itineraries.

prose: Celebrity Constellation is the last of the four Millennium-class ships and, at around 91,000 gross tons, roughly 30 per cent smaller than the Solstice ships that followed — which is now her selling point rather than her limitation.

The class is technically unusual. Instead of the diesel-electric arrangement almost every cruise ship uses, the Millennium ships were built with gas turbines driving Mermaid azimuth pods. The turbines burn cleaner but cost considerably more to run, and the pods gave the class a difficult decade of reliability problems in the 2000s.

All four ships went through Celebrity's Revolution refit programme, which reworked cabins and public rooms extensively enough that the interiors no longer read as early-2000s. Their smaller size lets them reach ports the Edge and Solstice ships cannot, which is why they have outlasted several larger contemporaries.

ships.name: Celebrity Summit
ships.headline: The third Millennium-class ship — small enough to go where the big ships can't.
ships.identity: Celebrity Summit is one of four 294-metre Millennium-class ships, sized for ports the Edge and Solstice classes cannot reach.

variants.name: Millennium class
variants.note: Third of four. Originally delivered as Summit, with the Celebrity prefix added later.

operators.note: Bermuda, Caribbean and Canada/New England itineraries.

prose: Celebrity Summit is the third Millennium-class ship and, like her sisters, a demonstration that a mid-sized ship can outlive larger and newer ones if it can reach ports they cannot.
At 294 metres and around 91,000 gross tons she works Bermuda, Canada and New England, and smaller Caribbean calls — itineraries where berth length and draft matter more than onboard water parks.

She was rebuilt inside under the Revolution programme, Celebrity's fleet-wide modernisation, which replaced staterooms, added the Retreat suite complex and reworked the public rooms. The hull and machinery are early-2000s; almost nothing a passenger touches is.

ships.name: Celebrity Infinity
ships.headline: The second Millennium-class ship — a South America and Alaska regular.
ships.identity: Celebrity Infinity is the second of four Millennium-class ships, working long-haul itineraries where her size is an advantage.

variants.name: Millennium class
variants.note: Second of four, between Celebrity Millennium (2000) and Celebrity Summit (2001).

operators.note: South America, Alaska and Pacific Coast itineraries.

prose: Celebrity Infinity is the second Millennium-class ship and spends much of her time on the itineraries that suit a mid-sized hull: South America, the Chilean fjords, Alaska and the Pacific coast.

The class shares an unusual specification — gas turbines rather than diesels, driving Mermaid azimuth pods. The arrangement was chosen for cleaner emissions and a quieter ship, and its main legacy was a long run of pod reliability problems that affected all four vessels through the 2000s.

She came through the Revolution refit with reworked staterooms, a rebuilt spa and the Retreat suite area, and now sits in the fleet as a small, long-range ship in a line whose newest vessels are 50,000 tons larger.

ships.name: Celebrity Millennium
ships.headline: The oldest ship in the Celebrity fleet — and the one that gave the class its name.
ships.identity: Celebrity Millennium entered service in 2000 as the lead of four gas-turbine-powered ships, and remains the fleet's oldest ocean-going vessel.

variants.name: Millennium class
variants.note: Lead ship of four built at Saint-Nazaire between 1999 and 2002, succeeding the Century class and preceding the Solstice class.

variants.name: Gas turbine power
variants.note: One of very few cruise ship classes built with gas turbines rather than diesel engines — cleaner and quieter, but markedly more expensive to fuel.

operators.note: Asia, Alaska and Pacific itineraries; extensively refitted in 2019.

prose: Celebrity Millennium is the oldest ocean-going ship in the fleet and the lead of a class that made a bet almost nobody else made: gas turbines instead of diesel engines.

Two General Electric turbines drive the electrical plant, feeding a pair of 19 MW Mermaid azimuth pods. The turbines are lighter, cleaner and much quieter than medium-speed diesels; they are also considerably more expensive to run on marine fuel, and the pods proved troublesome enough across all four ships that the arrangement was never repeated by the line.

She was rebuilt in 2019 under the Revolution programme — new staterooms, a new suite complex, reworked public rooms — which is why a ship approaching thirty years old still sells at premium prices. At 294 metres she also reaches ports the Edge class cannot, which is increasingly the argument for keeping her.

ships.name: Disney Adventure
ships.headline: Disney's largest ship — an abandoned hull bought out of bankruptcy for a fraction of its cost.
ships.identity: Disney Adventure was laid down as Global Dream for Genting's Dream Cruises, left half-finished when the owner collapsed, and bought by Disney for €40 million against an original value of around €1.8 billion.

variants.name: Global class
variants.note: Ordered as Global Dream for Genting Hong Kong's Dream Cruises and left incomplete at MV Werften when Genting entered insolvency in 2022. A planned sister was scrapped unfinished.

variants.name: The €40 million ship
variants.note: Disney acquired the partially built hull for around €40 million — a fraction of the roughly €1.8 billion the completed ship had been valued at — and had Meyer Wismar finish her.

operators.note: Year-round short itineraries from Singapore — Disney's first ship based in Asia.

prose: Disney Adventure is the most unusual acquisition in modern cruising. She began as Global Dream, the lead ship of a class Genting Hong Kong was building at MV Werften for its Dream Cruises brand — at 208,000 gross tons, among the largest cruise ships ever laid down. Genting collapsed into insolvency in 2022 with the hull roughly three-quarters complete.

Disney bought her for about €40 million. The completed ship had been valued at somewhere near €1.8 billion. Her intended sister was never finished and was broken up. Meyer Wismar completed the conversion under the supervision of Meyer Werft, which had built every other Disney ship.

At around 45 per cent larger than the Wish class she is by a wide margin Disney's biggest ship, carrying roughly 6,000 guests with 2,300 crew, and she carries the longest roller coaster at sea. She is also based in Singapore rather than Florida — Disney's first year-round Asian deployment, and a very different bet from the Caribbean short cruises the line is built on.

ships.name: Disney Destiny
ships.headline: The third Wish-class ship — themed on heroes and villains, with Marvel front and centre.
ships.identity: Disney Destiny joined the fleet in November 2025 on the 144,000 GT Wish-class hull, the first Disney ship to build its central theme around villains as much as heroes.

variants.name: Wish class
variants.note: Third of the class after Disney Wish (2022) and Disney Treasure (2024), also known as the Triton class. A further ship joins in 2028 for the Japanese market.

variants.name: Heroes and villains
variants.note: The first Disney ship to take a Marvel character as its Grand Hall centrepiece, with the atrium themed around heroes and their antagonists rather than a single film.

operators.note: Bahamas and Western Caribbean itineraries from Florida.

prose: Disney Destiny is the third Wish-class ship and follows the pattern the line has settled into: an identical 144,000-ton hull with an entirely different theme layered over it.

Where Disney Wish took enchantment and a castle-style Grand Hall, and Treasure took adventure and exploration, Destiny is built around heroes and villains — the first Disney ship to put a Marvel character at the centre of its atrium rather than a princess or a classic animated lead.

The mechanical package is unchanged: LNG dual-fuel, fourteen passenger decks, around 4,000 guests and 1,555 crew, and the AquaMouse water ride running above the top deck. Building three near-identical ships in four years is a marked change of pace for a line that went thirteen years between its first pair and its second.

ships.name: Disney Treasure
ships.headline: The second Wish-class ship — adventure and exploration, with an Aladdin-themed grand hall.
ships.identity: Disney Treasure repeats the 144,000 GT Wish hull, delivered in 2024 with its interiors themed on travel and discovery.

variants.name: Wish class
variants.note: Second of the class, between Disney Wish (2022) and Disney Destiny (2025).

variants.name: Rotational dining
variants.note: Restores Animator's Palate, which Disney Wish omitted — the rotational dining system moves guests and their serving team between three themed restaurants across a voyage.

operators.note: Caribbean itineraries from Port Canaveral.

prose: Disney Treasure is the second Wish-class ship, structurally identical to her lead sister and thematically unrelated to her — the Grand Hall is themed on Aladdin and the ship as a whole on travel and discovery rather than enchantment.

One notable correction: Disney Wish was the only ship in the fleet without Animator's Palate, the restaurant where the room's artwork animates around diners over the course of the meal. Treasure brought it back, alongside the rotational dining system that moves guests and their waiting team between three restaurants across a cruise.

She carries the same LNG dual-fuel machinery, fourteen passenger decks and the AquaMouse ride, and slots into the fleet as the middle ship of three near-identical vessels delivered between 2022 and 2025.

ships.name: Disney Fantasy
ships.headline: The second Dream-class ship — and half of the pair that made Disney a big-ship operator.
ships.identity: Disney Fantasy completed the Dream class in 2012 at around 130,000 GT, more than 50 per cent larger than the two ships Disney started with.

variants.name: Dream class
variants.note: Second of two, sister to Disney Dream (2011). Both built by Meyer Werft and roughly 46,000 GT larger than the Magic class.

operators.note: Caribbean itineraries from Port Canaveral.

prose: Disney Fantasy is the second of the Dream-class pair and the ship that finished Disney's move into large-ship cruising. Her Art Nouveau interiors are the counterpart to Disney Dream's Art Deco — the two ships are structurally identical and deliberately styled as a matched but distinguishable pair.

Both carry the AquaDuck, a 765-foot clear-tube water coaster that loops out over the side of the ship and back — the first water coaster fitted to a ship, and the feature that made the class recognisable from a distance.

The Dream class was Disney's answer to a specific problem: two ships from 1998 and 1999 could not meet demand, and the line had gone thirteen years without adding a hull. Fantasy and her sister roughly doubled Disney's capacity in two years.

ships.name: Disney Dream
ships.headline: The ship that put a water coaster around the funnel.
ships.identity: Disney Dream led a new class in 2011 at around 130,000 GT and introduced the AquaDuck — the first water coaster ever fitted to a ship.

variants.name: Dream class
variants.note: Lead ship of two with Disney Fantasy (2012), and the first Disney ships built by Meyer Werft rather than Fincantieri.

variants.name: AquaDuck
variants.note: A 765-foot transparent-tube water coaster running around the top deck and cantilevered out over the ship's side — the first of its kind at sea.

operators.note: Short Bahamas itineraries from Florida.

prose: Disney Dream ended a thirteen-year gap in Disney's shipbuilding and jumped the line's scale by more than 45,000 gross tons in a single step. She is also where Disney moved from Fincantieri to Meyer Werft, which has built every ship the line has ordered since.

The AquaDuck is the reason people remember her. It is a 765-foot water coaster in a clear acrylic tube, propelled by water jets, that runs a circuit of the top deck and swings out over the side of the ship four decks above the promenade. No ship had carried one before, and the idea has since been copied widely.

Her styling is Art Deco to Disney Fantasy's Art Nouveau, and both keep the line's deliberate ocean-liner profile: black hull, white superstructure, two red funnels with black tops. Only the aft funnel does any work — the forward one holds youth activity spaces, which is a very Disney solution to a naval architecture problem.

ships.name: Disney Magic
ships.headline: The ship that started it all — and the one that made cruise ships look like liners again.
ships.identity: Disney Magic launched the cruise line in 1998 with a deliberately retro silhouette: black hull, white superstructure and two red funnels, at a time when every competitor was building white boxes.

variants.name: Magic class
variants.note: Lead ship of two with Disney Wonder (1999), built by Fincantieri. The smallest and oldest ships in the fleet.

variants.name: The liner look
variants.note: Designed with a raked bow, black hull and twin funnels to evoke the transatlantic liners — only the aft funnel is functional; the forward one contains youth and crew spaces.

operators.note: Caribbean, Europe and Canada itineraries.

prose: Disney Magic is the ship that made Disney a shipping company. She entered service in July 1998, built by Fincantieri, and she looks the way she does because Disney made a decision nobody else was making in the 1990s: build a ship that reads as an ocean liner rather than a floating hotel.

The result is a black hull, a raked bow, a white superstructure and two red funnels with black caps — plus, in place of a coat of arms, a Mickey silhouette worked into the funnel markings and a Goofy figure hanging over the stern with a paint pot. Only the aft funnel actually vents anything; the forward one is filled with youth club space.

She also introduced rotational dining, where guests and their serving team move together between three themed restaurants across a voyage rather than staying in one room — a genuinely original piece of hospitality design that Disney still runs on every ship. At around 84,000 gross tons she is now the smallest ship in a fleet whose largest is 208,000.

ships.name: Disney Wonder
ships.headline: The second Disney ship — the smallest in the fleet, and an Alaska regular.
ships.identity: Disney Wonder followed her sister in 1999 on the same Fincantieri hull, with Art Nouveau interiors in place of Disney Magic's Art Deco.

variants.name: Magic class
variants.note: Second of two. Refitted extensively in 2016 and again in 2019 under the line's modernisation programme.

variants.name: The stern figure
variants.note: Where Disney Magic carries Goofy over the stern, Wonder carries Donald Duck with Huey — the ships are otherwise near-identical externally.

operators.note: Alaska summers from Vancouver, Mexican Riviera and Caribbean in winter.

prose: Disney Wonder is the smallest ship in the Disney fleet and the second the line ever ordered, delivered by Fincantieri a year after Disney Magic and effectively identical to her.

The differences are decorative and easy to miss: Art Nouveau interiors against Magic's Art Deco, and Donald Duck rather than Goofy suspended over the stern. Externally the pair are twins — same 294-metre hull, same twin-funnel liner profile, same eleven passenger decks.

Her size is now her advantage. At around 83,000 gross tons she can work Alaska out of Vancouver and reach ports the Wish-class ships cannot, and two substantial refits have kept her interiors current — which matters for a ship approaching thirty years old in a fleet where the newest vessel is two and a half times her size.

ships.name: Iona
ships.headline: P&O's first LNG ship — and the largest ever built for the British market at her debut.
ships.identity: Iona arrived in 2021 as the first LNG-powered cruise ship built for a British line, with a glass SkyDome over the midships pool.

variants.name: Excel class
variants.note: Built on Carnival Corporation's Excel platform, shared with Carnival's Mardi Gras group, AIDAnova and Costa Smeralda. Sister to Arvia (2022).

variants.name: The SkyDome
variants.note: A glass dome over the midships pool that opens the deck to daylight in bad weather and converts to an aerial performance space at night — a direct answer to the British climate.

operators.note: Norwegian fjords and Northern Europe in summer, Canaries and Mediterranean in winter, all from Southampton.

prose: Iona was the largest cruise ship ever built for the British market when she entered service, and P&O's first on LNG — a jump of about 40,000 gross tons over Britannia, which had been the flagship six years earlier.

The design problem she answers is weather. P&O sails almost everything out of Southampton, including Norwegian fjord itineraries where an open pool deck is unusable for much of the voyage. The SkyDome puts a glass roof over the midships pool, keeping it usable in the North Sea and converting to an aerial performance venue after dark.

Her introduction was delayed by more than a year — she was completed in 2020 and could not be named until August 2021, when she was finally christened at Southampton. She shares the Excel platform with Arvia, and with Carnival, AIDA and Costa ships built on the same hull, which is how a British brand ended up with a German-built ship on an Italian-designed platform.

ships.name: Britannia
ships.headline: Named by Queen Elizabeth II — the last ship she ever christened.
ships.identity: Britannia entered service in 2015 as P&O's flagship, a Royal-class ship shared with Princess Cruises, and was named by the Queen at Southampton.

variants.name: Royal class
variants.note: The only P&O ship on Princess Cruises' Royal-class hull, sitting between Regal Princess (2014) and Majestic Princess (2017) in the build order.

variants.name: No ocean-view cabins
variants.note: Unusually for the fleet, Britannia has no outside cabins with fixed windows — accommodation is inside or balcony only.

operators.note: Mediterranean and Caribbean itineraries from Southampton.

prose: Britannia was P&O's flagship for six years and is still described that way by the line, even though Iona and Arvia are both around 40,000 gross tons larger. She was named at Southampton in March 2015 by Queen Elizabeth II — the last ship the Queen ever christened.

She is a Royal-class ship, the same Fincantieri hull as Princess Cruises' Royal, Regal, Majestic, Sky, Enchanted and Discovery — the most reproduced design in Carnival Corporation's portfolio. The British-market fit-out is what differs: a large outdoor promenade, UK-sourced spirits, and dining programmes built around British chefs.

One structural oddity sets her apart from her fleetmates: she has no ocean-view cabins with fixed windows at all. Accommodation is inside or balcony, with nothing in between — a decision no other P&O ship repeats.

ships.name: Ventura
ships.headline: The Grand-class ship built for British families — named by Helen Mirren.
ships.identity: Ventura entered service in 2008 at 116,017 GT, the first of two P&O ships on Princess Cruises' Grand-class hull.

variants.name: Ventura class
variants.note: A Grand-class sub-class of two built for P&O — Ventura (2008) and Azura (2010) — with 19 total decks against the original Grand ships' 17.

operators.note: Mediterranean, Caribbean and Northern Europe from Southampton.

prose: Ventura is P&O's version of the Grand class — the same Fincantieri platform that gave Princess Cruises nine ships, fitted out for British families and named by Dame Helen Mirren in 2008.

She and near-twin Azura form the Ventura sub-class, which added two decks over the original Grand-class ships. Six swimming pools, one under a retractable roof, is another concession to Southampton departures and North Atlantic weather.

Carnival Corporation's habit of running one hull across several brands is unusually visible in the P&O fleet: Ventura and Azura are Grand class, Britannia is Royal class, Arcadia is a Vista-class hull, and Iona and Arvia are Excel class. Four platforms, four sister brands, one shipyard group.

ships.name: Azura
ships.headline: Ventura's near-twin — and the ship that arrived as P&O reflagged to Bermuda.
ships.identity: Azura entered service in 2010 at 115,055 GT, the second Ventura-class ship and almost identical to her sister.

variants.name: Ventura class
variants.note: Second of two, effectively identical to Ventura externally and in layout.

variants.name: Reflagged
variants.note: Registered at Southampton on delivery in 2010, then re-registered to Hamilton, Bermuda in 2011 along with the rest of the fleet — a change that allows captains to conduct legally binding weddings at sea.

operators.note: Caribbean winters and Mediterranean summers from Southampton.

prose: Azura is the second Ventura-class ship and about as close to her sister as two ships get — 290 metres, 115,055 gross tons, the same nineteen decks and the same six Wärtsilä diesels producing 67.2 MW.

Her delivery in 2010 sits either side of a change in the fleet's paperwork. She was registered at Southampton when new, and re-registered to Hamilton, Bermuda in 2011 with the rest of P&O's ships — a move that let captains legally marry couples at sea, which UK registry does not permit.
She carries an open-air cinema over the pool deck and a slightly more social layout than Ventura, but the two are routinely mistaken for each other, which is fair enough.

ships.name: Arcadia
ships.headline: Laid down as a Cunard Queen — finished as an adults-only P&O ship.
ships.identity: Arcadia was ordered as Cunard's Queen Victoria, transferred to P&O mid-build, and entered service in 2005 as an adults-only ship carrying some 3,000 works of art.

variants.name: Vista class
variants.note: Built on the Vista-class hull shared with Holland America and Cunard. Ordered as Cunard's Queen Victoria and reassigned to P&O during construction; Cunard's Queen Victoria was subsequently built as a separate, larger ship delivered in 2007.

variants.name: Adults only
variants.note: One of two adults-only ships in the P&O fleet with Aurora. Carries around 3,000 works of art and glass-fronted external lifts.

operators.note: World cruises and long-haul itineraries from Southampton; adults only.

prose: Arcadia has the most confusing paperwork in the P&O fleet. She was ordered from Fincantieri as Queen Victoria for Cunard, and reassigned to P&O partway through construction — Cunard's actual Queen Victoria was then built separately and delivered two years later, larger and to a revised design.

What P&O got was a Vista-class hull, the platform Fincantieri also built for Holland America, finished as an adults-only ship. She carries around 3,000 works of art, glass-fronted lifts on the ship's exterior, and a layout aimed at longer voyages rather than short Mediterranean runs.

At around 84,000 gross tons she is the second smallest ship in the fleet and does the work that suits that: world cruises and long-haul itineraries where a smaller hull reaches more ports and a quieter ship is the selling point.

ships.name: Aurora
ships.headline: The oldest and smallest P&O ship — whose champagne bottle failed to break.
ships.identity: Aurora entered service in 2000 at 76,152 GT and remains the fleet's smallest and oldest ship, best known for a naming ceremony and maiden voyage that both went wrong.

variants.name: Adults only
variants.note: Converted to adults-only operation, alongside Arcadia — the two smallest ships in the fleet.

variants.name: A bad start
variants.note: At her naming in April 2000 the champagne bottle failed to break against the hull. Her maiden voyage was then abandoned days later with a propulsion-bearing fault, and passengers were refunded.

operators.note: World cruises and long itineraries from Southampton; adults only.

prose: Aurora is the smallest and oldest ship P&O operates, and she began about as badly as a ship can. At her naming ceremony in April 2000 the champagne bottle swung against the hull and refused to break — traditionally read as an omen — and days later her maiden voyage was abandoned with a propulsion-bearing failure, every passenger refunded.

She recovered into a long and useful career. At 76,152 gross tons and 270 metres she is small by modern standards, which is exactly why she works: she takes the world cruises and long-haul itineraries where port access matters more than water slides.

She now sails adults-only alongside Arcadia, giving P&O two quiet ships at the bottom of a fleet whose top end is the 345-metre Arvia. A twenty-six-year-old hull at one end and an LNG mega-ship at the other is a wide spread for a seven-ship line.

ships.name: Nieuw Statendam
ships.headline: The second Pinnacle-class ship — carrying a name Holland America has used since 1898.
ships.identity: Nieuw Statendam entered service in 2018 at 99,863 GT, the middle ship of the three largest Holland America has ever operated.

variants.name: Pinnacle class
variants.note: Second of three, between Koningsdam (2016) and Rotterdam (2021). The largest ships in the fleet, and the only ones that will not fit the old Panama Canal locks.

variants.name: The Statendam name
variants.note: The sixth Holland America ship to carry Statendam, a name the line has used since 1898 — hence the Nieuw prefix.

operators.note: Caribbean and Northern Europe itineraries.

prose: Nieuw Statendam is the second of three Pinnacle-class ships and, with Rotterdam, shares a gross tonnage of 99,863 — Holland America having stopped deliberately just short of the 100,000-ton mark that every other mainstream line raced past a decade earlier.

That restraint is the brand's whole position. Where competitors added water parks and go-kart tracks, the Pinnacle ships spent their volume on a two-deck World Stage theatre with a 270-degree LED screen, a classical music venue, and more specialty restaurants than passenger numbers would normally justify.

The name is older than most cruise lines. Holland America has run a Statendam since 1898, and this is the sixth — the Nieuw prefix distinguishing her from her predecessors, in the same way Nieuw Amsterdam distinguishes that ship from the 1938 liner of the same name.

ships.name: Koningsdam
ships.headline: The first Pinnacle-class ship — and the first new Holland America ship in six years.
ships.identity: Koningsdam led a new class in 2016 at 99,863 GT, named for the Dutch king and ending a six-year gap in the line's shipbuilding.

variants.name: Pinnacle class
variants.note: Lead ship of three. Fits the new Panama Canal locks but not the original ones — the first Holland America ship for which that is true.

variants.name: Koning
variants.note: Dutch for king. The name honours King Willem-Alexander, and the ship was christened by Queen Máxima in Rotterdam.

operators.note: Alaska summers, Mediterranean and Caribbean itineraries.

prose: Koningsdam broke a six-year pause in Holland America's shipbuilding and reset the line's upper size limit, arriving about 13,000 gross tons larger than the Signature ships that preceded her.

She introduced the two-deck World Stage theatre with its wraparound 270-degree screen, the fleet's first purpose-built family staterooms, and a musical programme unusual for a mainstream line — a dedicated classical venue alongside blues and rock rooms. The interior was designed by Adam D. Tihany, with a hull whose curved balconies were shaped to suggest a musical instrument.

The name is a straightforward tribute: koning is Dutch for king, honouring Willem-Alexander, and Queen Máxima named her at Rotterdam in 2016. Like every Holland America ship since 1873, she ends in -dam.

ships.name: Nieuw Amsterdam
ships.headline: The second Signature-class ship — and the only one with a Morimoto restaurant.
ships.identity: Nieuw Amsterdam entered service in 2010 at around 86,700 GT, the larger of the two Signature-class ships.

variants.name: Signature class
variants.note: Second of two with Eurodam (2008). A stretched development of the Vista class, roughly 4,000 GT larger.

variants.name: Morimoto by Sea
variants.note: A 2023 refit added a restaurant with chef Masaharu Morimoto — the only permanent installation of its kind in the fleet.

operators.note: Caribbean, Alaska and Panama Canal itineraries.

prose: Nieuw Amsterdam is the second Signature-class ship, a stretched development of the Vista design that added around 4,000 gross tons and a couple of hundred passengers without changing the essential proportions.

Her name carries weight in the line's history — the 1938 Nieuw Amsterdam was Holland America's most celebrated liner, a ship that served as a troop transport through the Second World War and returned to Atlantic service afterwards. This is the fourth to carry it.

A 2023 refit gave her Morimoto by Sea, built with chef Masaharu Morimoto, which no other ship in the fleet has as a permanent venue. Otherwise the work was the unglamorous kind — carpets, soft furnishings, the parts of a fourteen-year-old ship that wear out.

ships.name: Eurodam
ships.headline: The first Signature-class ship — christened by Queen Beatrix.
ships.identity: Eurodam entered service in 2008 at around 86,273 GT, introducing the Signature class and several restaurants the fleet still runs.

variants.name: Signature class
variants.note: Lead ship of two. Fits both the original and the new Panama Canal locks, unlike the Pinnacle ships.

operators.note: Caribbean, Alaska and European itineraries.

prose: Eurodam opened the Signature class in 2008 and was christened by Queen Beatrix of the Netherlands — Holland America is American-owned and Seattle-based, but the Dutch connection is maintained deliberately, from the registry in Rotterdam to the -dam suffix on every hull.

She introduced Tamarind, the pan-Asian restaurant, and the private Retreat cabanas, both of which spread across the fleet afterwards. At around 86,000 gross tons she is comfortably mid-sized by current standards, which suits the line's long itineraries.

Crucially she fits the original Panama Canal locks as well as the new ones — a constraint the larger Pinnacle ships gave up, and one that still matters for a line that sails to every continent and builds itineraries around canal transits.

ships.name: Noordam
ships.headline: North — the last of four Vista-class ships named for the compass points.
ships.identity: Noordam completed the Vista class in 2006, the fourth of a quartet named for north, south, east and west.

variants.name: Vista class
variants.note: Fourth and last of four. The class is named for its extensive use of glass in the superstructure, not for a compass direction — the ships' individual names supply that.

variants.name: The compass four
variants.note: Zuiderdam (south), Oosterdam (east), Westerdam (west) and Noordam (north) — a complete set of Dutch compass points across one class.

operators.note: Alaska summers and long-haul itineraries.

prose: Noordam closed the Vista class in 2006 and completed one of the tidiest naming schemes in cruising: her three sisters are Zuiderdam, Oosterdam and Westerdam — south, east and west in Dutch — and she is north.

The class name refers to something else entirely: the extensive glazing in the superstructure, which gave the ships far more exterior glass than Holland America's earlier vessels. It is the visual signature of the group.

She carries museum-quality art including an 1842 oil painting of Utrecht, which is characteristic of the line — Holland America spends real money on fleet art collections and treats them as part of the product rather than decoration. At around 83,000 gross tons she is favoured for Alaska and longer voyages.

ships.name: Westerdam
ships.headline: West — the third Vista-class ship, and an Asia regular.
ships.identity: Westerdam entered service in 2004, the third of the four compass-named Vista-class ships.

variants.name: Vista class
variants.note: Third of four. The name has been used by Holland America since 1946; this is the fourth Westerdam.

operators.note: Asia, Alaska and Pacific itineraries.

prose: Westerdam is the third Vista-class ship and, like her sisters, built around a diesel-electric plant driving Azipod thrusters — pods that both propel and steer, doing away with conventional rudders and shafts.

At around 83,000 gross tons and 285 metres she sits in the size band that has become unusual: too big to be a boutique ship, small enough to work Asian and Alaskan ports that the 200,000-ton generation cannot approach.

She spends much of her year in Asia, which is a deliberate deployment for the class. Holland America builds long, port-heavy itineraries rather than short Caribbean loops, and the Vista ships are the right size for that work.

ships.name: Oosterdam
ships.headline: East — the second Vista-class ship.
ships.identity: Oosterdam entered service in 2003, the second of the four compass-named Vista-class ships.

variants.name: Vista class
variants.note: Second of four, delivered eight months after Zuiderdam.

operators.note: Alaska, Mexico and Pacific itineraries.

prose: Oosterdam is the second Vista-class ship, delivered less than a year after the lead vessel — Fincantieri built all four between 2002 and 2006, which is a fast cadence for ships of this size.

Her name means east, completing the second corner of the class's compass set. Holland America has recycled ship names since the nineteenth century, but the Vista quartet is the only time it has built a naming scheme into a single class from the start.

The machinery is the class's most interesting feature: a combined arrangement of diesel engines and a General Electric gas turbine feeding electrical propulsion through Azipod thrusters. Very few cruise ships carry a gas turbine at all, and fewer still combine one with diesels.

ships.name: Zuiderdam
ships.headline: South — the Vista-class lead ship, with a gas turbine on board.
ships.identity: Zuiderdam led the Vista class in 2002 at 81,769 GT, running a General Electric gas turbine alongside five diesels.

specs.label: Propulsion
specs.metric: Azipod, diesel + GE LM2500 gas turbine
specs.imperial: Azipod, diesel + GE LM2500 gas turbine

variants.name: Vista class
variants.note: Lead ship of four, built by Fincantieri at Marghera between 2002 and 2006. The class name refers to the extensive glazing in the superstructure.

variants.name: Shared platform
variants.note: The Vista hull also underpins Cunard's Queen Victoria and P&O's Arcadia — the same Fincantieri design finished three different ways for three Carnival Corporation brands.

operators.note: Caribbean, Panama Canal and Antarctica-adjacent South America itineraries.

prose: Zuiderdam is the lead Vista-class ship and carries an unusual power plant: five Sulzer diesels working alongside a single General Electric LM2500 gas turbine, together producing 51,840 kW and driving the ship through Azipod thrusters.

The turbine was fitted for emissions rather than speed. Running it in port and in sensitive waters such as Alaska cuts local pollution sharply, at a fuel cost the line accepted — the same reasoning that put gas turbines in Celebrity's Millennium class around the same time.

Her hull is one of the most widely reused designs in the industry. The Vista platform also produced Cunard's Queen Victoria and P&O's Arcadia, all three from Fincantieri, all three finished to look like completely different ships. Zuiderdam's name means south — the first corner of her class's compass set.

ships.name: Zaandam
ships.headline: A ship with a pipe organ in the atrium and guitars signed by Queen.
ships.identity: Zaandam entered service in 2000 at around 61,396 GT, an R-class ship themed on music and among the smallest in the fleet.

variants.name: Rotterdam class (R class)
variants.note: The oldest and smallest ships in the fleet, with Volendam. Small enough to reach ports the Pinnacle ships cannot.

variants.name: The music theme
variants.note: Fitted with a decorative Dutch street organ in the atrium and a collection of instruments signed by musicians including Queen and Iggy Pop.

operators.note: Alaska, South America and long exploratory itineraries.

prose: Zaandam is one of the two oldest ships Holland America operates and the more characterful of the pair. Her interior theme is music, and the line committed to it properly: a Dutch street organ in the atrium, and a collection of instruments signed by artists including Queen and Iggy Pop.

At around 61,000 gross tons and 237 metres she is less than a third the size of the largest cruise ships afloat, which is the point. R-class ships reach ports that are physically closed to the current generation, and Holland America builds long exploratory itineraries around exactly that.

She had a difficult 2020 — like several ships she spent weeks at sea during the pandemic while ports refused entry, before eventually disembarking passengers in Florida. She returned to service and continues to work the long-haul routes the class was bought for.

ships.name: Volendam
ships.headline: The oldest ship in the Holland America fleet — and the third to carry the name.
ships.identity: Volendam entered service in 1999 at around 60,906 GT, the smallest and oldest ship the line operates.

variants.name: Rotterdam class (R class)
variants.note: Sister to Zaandam. The two are the oldest ships in the fleet by more than two years.

variants.name: The Volendam name
variants.note: The third Holland America ship to carry it, after vessels of 1922 and 1958.

operators.note: Grand voyages, Alaska and long exploratory itineraries.

prose: Volendam is the oldest ship Holland America runs and the smallest, at around 60,900 gross tons — roughly a quarter the size of the largest cruise ship in service today.

That is not a weakness in this fleet. The line's identity is built on long voyages to remote places rather than short Caribbean loops, and a 237-metre hull carrying 1,432 passengers can enter harbours where a 360-metre ship must anchor offshore or skip the call entirely.

She is the third Volendam, following ships of 1922 and 1958 — the line has been recycling its names for over a century, and every one of them has ended in -dam since Rotterdam sailed in 1872. It is the most consistently applied naming convention in passenger shipping.

ships.name: Queen Anne
ships.headline: The 249th ship to fly the Cunard house flag.
ships.identity: Queen Anne joined in 2024 at 113,000 GT — Cunard's largest by passenger capacity, though barely three-quarters the tonnage of Queen Mary 2.

variants.name: Extended Pinnacle platform
variants.note: Built on a lengthened version of the Pinnacle design used for Holland America's Koningsdam, Nieuw Statendam and Rotterdam — larger than any of them.

variants.name: 249th Cunarder
variants.note: The 249th vessel to fly the Cunard house flag since Samuel Cunard founded the line in 1839. Named at Liverpool in June 2024, the company's ancestral home port.

operators.note: Mediterranean, Northern Europe and world voyages from Southampton.

prose: Queen Anne is the first new Cunarder in fourteen years and, in a fleet of four, an unusual proposition: she carries more passengers than any of her fleetmates while being roughly 35,000 gross tons smaller than Queen Mary 2.

The hull is Carnival Corporation's Pinnacle platform, stretched — the same basic design as Holland America's Koningsdam, Nieuw Statendam and Rotterdam, but longer and larger than all three. Cunard's contribution is the fit-out: Adam D. Tihany coordinated the interiors, with London's David Collins Studio brought in from luxury hotel work rather than shipbuilding.

She is the 249th ship to fly the Cunard house flag since Samuel Cunard founded the line in 1839, and she was named at Liverpool — the company's original home port — in June 2024, six months later than planned after pandemic-era supply problems delayed her delivery.

ships.name: Queen Elizabeth
ships.headline: The larger of Cunard's two Vista-class Queens — by one deck and a squared-off stern.
ships.identity: Queen Elizabeth entered service in 2010 at around 90,901 GT, differing from her sister mainly in a straight stern that bought extra staterooms.

variants.name: Vista class
variants.note: Sister to Queen Victoria on the Fincantieri Vista platform — shared with Holland America's Zuiderdam group, P&O's Arcadia and Costa Luminosa.

variants.name: The squared stern
variants.note: Built with a straight transom rather than Queen Victoria's original sloping stern, which allowed additional staterooms aft and about 850 more gross tons.

operators.note: Japan, Alaska and Australia seasons plus world voyages.

prose: Queen Elizabeth is the third Cunard ship to carry the name, following the 1938 liner and the 1969 Queen Elizabeth 2. She is also, by a small margin, the larger of the line's two Vista-class Queens.

The difference is at the back. Queen Victoria was built with a sloping cruise-ship stern; Queen Elizabeth got a squared transom, which allowed more staterooms aft and about 850 extra gross tons. It is a minor change that gives the two ships noticeably different profiles from astern.

Her hull is the Fincantieri Vista platform, one of the most widely shared designs afloat — Holland America runs four of them, P&O has Arcadia, Costa has Luminosa. Cunard's version is fitted out with panelled walls, a double-height Queens Room ballroom and a games deck with croquet, which is a very particular set of choices for a ship built to the same drawings as a mass-market Caribbean vessel.

ships.name: Queen Victoria
ships.headline: The second Queen Victoria — the first became P&O's Arcadia before she was finished.
ships.identity: Queen Victoria entered service in 2007 at around 90,049 GT, built after the original hull bearing her name was reassigned mid-construction to P&O.

variants.name: Vista class
variants.note: The smallest ship in the Cunard fleet. Sister to Queen Elizabeth (2010).

variants.name: The first Queen Victoria
variants.note: An earlier Vista-class hull was ordered as Queen Victoria and transferred to P&O during construction, entering service in 2005 as Arcadia. This ship was then built to a revised and larger design.

operators.note: Mediterranean, Northern Europe and world voyages from Southampton.

prose: There have been two Queen Victorias, and the first one is sailing for someone else. Cunard ordered a Vista-class ship under the name, then Carnival Corporation reassigned the hull to P&O partway through construction — she entered service in 2005 as Arcadia, and remains in the P&O fleet today.

Cunard then had this ship built to a revised, larger specification, and she took the name in 2007. She is the smallest vessel in the fleet at around 90,049 gross tons, roughly 850 less than her sister Queen Elizabeth, whose squared stern gained a little extra volume.

Her sloping stern was later modified, narrowing the visual gap between the two sisters. Both are Fincantieri Vista hulls, the same platform underneath Holland America's four compass-named ships — three Carnival Corporation brands running the same drawings and selling completely different experiences on top of them.

ships.name: Costa Toscana
ships.headline: Costa's largest ship — and half of the LNG pair the fleet was rebuilt around.
ships.identity: Costa Toscana entered service in 2021 at 185,010 GT, the second LNG-powered Excel-class ship for the line and the largest it operates.

variants.name: Excel class
variants.note: Carnival Corporation's LNG platform, shared with AIDAnova, Costa Smeralda, P&O's Iona and Arvia, and Carnival's Mardi Gras group — the most widely reproduced large-ship design in the industry.

variants.name: Sister to Smeralda
variants.note: Identical in size to Costa Smeralda (2019); the two are the only Costa ships built on the Excel hull.

operators.note: Western Mediterranean itineraries from Savona.

prose: Costa Toscana is the larger half of the pair the modern Costa fleet is organised around. She and Costa Smeralda are the line's only LNG ships, and at 185,010 gross tons they are roughly 50,000 tons bigger than anything else Costa runs.

The hull is Carnival Corporation's Excel platform — the same design underneath AIDAnova, P&O's Iona and Arvia, and Carnival Cruise Line's Mardi Gras group. Seven brands, one set of drawings, and in Costa's case an interior themed around Italian regions rather than the Nordic minimalism or British formality the other operators chose.

Liquefied natural gas cuts sulphur oxides and particulates to near zero and carbon dioxide by roughly a fifth against marine gas oil. The catch is bunkering: LNG is available at a limited number of ports, which is part of why Toscana works a fixed Western Mediterranean rotation rather than roaming.

ships.name: Costa Diadema
ships.headline: The largest Dream-class ship ever built — and the only one not flying the Carnival flag.
ships.identity: Costa Diadema entered service in 2014 at 132,500 GT, the fourth and biggest ship on Fincantieri's Dream platform.

variants.name: Dream class
variants.note: The fourth and largest of the class, after Carnival Dream (2009), Carnival Magic (2011) and Carnival Breeze (2012) — about 4,000 GT bigger than any of them.

variants.name: Il Diadema
variants.note: Italian for the tiara. She carries a 500-metre outdoor promenade running the full length of the ship, unusual on a modern hull.

operators.note: Mediterranean itineraries from Savona; Arabian Gulf in winter.

prose: Costa Diadema is the biggest ship ever built on Fincantieri's Dream platform, and the only one of the four that did not go to Carnival Cruise Line. She is roughly 4,000 gross tons larger than Carnival Breeze, the previous largest.

Her distinguishing feature is old-fashioned: a 500-metre outdoor promenade running the full circuit of the ship. Modern hulls tend to lose the wraparound deck to cabins and specialty venues, and Costa kept it deliberately as a Mediterranean-cruising feature.

She was the flagship for eight years until the LNG pair arrived, and remains the largest non-LNG ship in the fleet. Her name is Italian for a tiara, continuing Costa's habit of naming ships for qualities and objects rather than places or people.

ships.name: Costa Fascinosa
ships.headline: The last Concordia-class ship — delivered months after her lead sister was lost.
ships.identity: Costa Fascinosa entered service in May 2012 at around 113,200 GT, completing a class whose name ship had sunk four months earlier.

variants.name: Concordia class
variants.note: The sixth and final ship of the class. Costa now operates four: Serena, Pacifica, Favolosa and Fascinosa.

operators.note: Mediterranean and Northern Europe itineraries.

prose: Costa Fascinosa was delivered in May 2012, four months after the loss of Costa Concordia — the ship her class was named for. She was already built and fitting out when the disaster happened, and entered service into a company being restructured around it.
Mechanically she is a straightforward Concordia-class ship: around 113,000 gross tons, 290 metres, thirteen passenger decks and capacity for some 3,780 people at maximum occupancy.

Her interiors follow the class theme of Italian cinema, with public rooms named for films and film-makers. Costa's design language across this generation is unapologetically maximalist, which sets the older ships apart sharply from the restrained LNG pair that now leads the fleet.

ships.name: Costa Favolosa
ships.headline: The fifth Concordia-class ship, delivered the year before the class lost its name ship.
ships.identity: Costa Favolosa entered service in 2011 at around 113,200 GT, the penultimate ship of the Concordia class.

variants.name: Concordia class
variants.note: Fifth of six, and near-identical to Costa Fascinosa (2012).

operators.note: Mediterranean, Caribbean and South America itineraries.

prose: Costa Favolosa is the fifth of six Concordia-class ships and effectively a twin of Costa Fascinosa, delivered ten months apart from the same yard to the same drawings.

The class is the backbone of what Costa still operates: four of the six remain with the line, and at around 113,000 gross tons they handle the itineraries the LNG flagships cannot — smaller Mediterranean ports, South America, and repositioning runs where a 185,000-ton ship would be uneconomic.

Costa's ship names are adjectives rather than places — Favolosa means fabulous, Fascinosa fascinating, Deliziosa delightful, Serena serene. It is a naming convention no other major line uses, and it makes the fleet instantly identifiable on a berth list.

ships.name: Costa Pacifica
ships.headline: The Concordia-class ship with a recording studio on board.
ships.identity: Costa Pacifica entered service in 2009 at around 114,500 GT, themed entirely on music and delivered alongside Costa Luminosa on the same day.

variants.name: Concordia class
variants.note: Third of six. Christened on the same day as Costa Luminosa in a double naming ceremony at Genoa — the first time Costa named two ships together.

variants.name: The music ship
variants.note: Themed on music throughout, with a working recording studio where passengers can cut a track.

operators.note: Mediterranean and Northern Europe itineraries.

prose: Costa Pacifica is themed on music from end to end, and unlike most ships that claim a theme she carries the hardware to back it: a working recording studio where passengers can produce a track, alongside public rooms named for songs and genres.

She was christened at Genoa in June 2009 in a double ceremony alongside Costa Luminosa — the first time Costa named two ships on the same day, and a marker of how fast the fleet was expanding before 2012.

The two ships were very different underneath. Pacifica is a 114,500-ton Concordia-class hull; Luminosa was a 92,700-ton Vista-class ship, and later left the fleet entirely to become Carnival Luminosa. Costa's remaining Vista ship is Costa Deliziosa.

ships.name: Costa Serena
ships.headline: The second Concordia-class ship — and the last to be rebuilt under Costa's five-year refit plan.
ships.identity: Costa Serena entered service in 2007 at around 114,500 GT and completed a major modernisation in late 2025.

variants.name: Concordia class
variants.note: Second of six, after Costa Concordia (2006). Themed on classical antiquity and the Roman gods.

variants.name: 2025 refit
variants.note: The final ship in Costa's five-year, roughly €200 million fleet modernisation — renovated suites, redesigned pools and dining rooms, and a new food court.

operators.note: World voyage from Tokyo to Buenos Aires in 2026; returning to a Savona base from 2027.

prose: Costa Serena is the second Concordia-class ship and the one Costa chose to finish its modernisation programme on — a roughly €200 million, five-year effort across the fleet that concluded with her dry-docking in late 2025.

Her theme is classical antiquity, with public spaces named for Roman gods and a decorative programme heavy on statuary. It is a long way from the Nordic restraint of the Excel ships, and entirely deliberate: Costa sells an Italian product to a mostly Southern European market.

After the refit she was sent long-haul — a world voyage from Tokyo to Buenos Aires through 2026, before returning to a Savona base in 2027. For a nineteen-year-old ship that is a substantial vote of confidence.

ships.name: Costa Deliziosa
ships.headline: Costa's last Vista-class ship — the same hull as two Cunard Queens.
ships.identity: Costa Deliziosa entered service in 2010 at around 92,720 GT, the only Vista-class ship remaining in the Costa fleet.

variants.name: Vista class
