variants.note: The Fincantieri platform behind Holland America's four compass ships, Cunard's Queen Victoria and Queen Elizabeth, and P&O's Arcadia. Costa's other Vista ship, Costa Luminosa, left for Carnival Cruise Line in 2022.

variants.name: The 2020 voyage
variants.note: She was mid-world-cruise when the pandemic closed ports, and completed an extended repatriation passage before disembarking passengers in Italy.

operators.note: World cruises, Indian Ocean and Arabian Gulf itineraries.

prose: Costa Deliziosa is the smallest ship in the fleet and the most widely related. Her hull is Fincantieri's Vista platform — the same drawings that produced Holland America's Zuiderdam group, Cunard's Queen Victoria and Queen Elizabeth, and P&O's Arcadia.

That makes her a useful demonstration of how little the platform determines the product. The same steel underneath is sold as British ocean-liner formality by Cunard, Dutch long-voyage cruising by Holland America, and an Italian holiday by Costa.

She is the line's long-range ship, working world cruises and Indian Ocean itineraries where her smaller size is the advantage. Her sister Costa Luminosa left for Carnival Cruise Line in 2022, leaving Deliziosa as Costa's only Vista hull.

ships.name: Costa Fortuna
ships.headline: The oldest ship in the fleet — leaving Costa in September 2026.
ships.identity: Costa Fortuna entered service in 2003 at 102,669 GT and has been sold to Margaritaville at Sea, departing the Costa fleet in September 2026.

variants.name: Fortuna class
variants.note: One of two, with Costa Magica (2004). Magica was sold to Seajets in 2023 and renamed Mykonos Magic.

variants.name: Sold on
variants.note: Acquired by Paradise Cruise Line Operator for Margaritaville at Sea, transferring in September 2026 and re-registering from Genoa to Nassau.

operators.note: Mediterranean and Northern Europe until September 2026, when she transfers to Margaritaville at Sea.

prose: Costa Fortuna is the oldest and shortest ship in the fleet, and she is on her way out — sold to Paradise Cruise Line Operator and transferring to Margaritaville at Sea in September 2026, after which she re-registers from Genoa to Nassau.

Her interiors are a tribute to the Italian liners of the twentieth century, including scale models of earlier Costa and Italian Line ships suspended in the atrium. It is an unusually self-aware piece of design for a mass-market ship.

Her departure completes a long contraction. Costa ran more than fifteen ships before 2012; the 2026 fleet is eight, after Costa Venezia, Costa Firenze and Costa Luminosa were transferred to Carnival Cruise Line and Costa Magica was sold outright. What remains is younger, larger and built around the two LNG flagships.

ships.name: AIDAcosma
ships.headline: The second LNG cruise ship ever built — sister to the first.
ships.identity: AIDAcosma entered service in 2022 at 183,858 GT, completing the two-ship class that began with the world's first LNG-powered cruise ship.

variants.name: Excellence class
variants.note: Meyer Werft calls the platform Excellence or Helios; Carnival Corporation calls it Excel. Same design as Costa Smeralda and Toscana, P&O's Iona and Arvia, and Carnival's Mardi Gras group.

variants.name: Second of two
variants.note: AIDA ordered a third Helios ship, later cancelled — the class closed at two.

operators.note: Mediterranean summers, Canaries in winter, from German home ports.

prose: AIDAcosma is the sister to the most historically important cruise ship of the last decade. AIDAnova was the first cruise ship in the world able to run entirely on liquefied natural gas; AIDAcosma was the second, and the class stopped there — a planned third ship was cancelled.

The platform went on to become the most reproduced large-ship design in the industry, appearing across four Carnival Corporation brands. Confusingly it carries two names: Meyer Werft calls it Excellence or Helios, Carnival calls it Excel. Same steel either way.

AIDA's product is unusual for a ship this size. The line runs a German-market club-ship concept — informal, buffet-led, no fixed dining times and no formal nights — which is a very different proposition from the Cunard or Holland America ships built on comparable hulls.

ships.name: AIDAperla
ships.headline: The second of two ships that drove Mitsubishi out of cruise shipbuilding.
ships.identity: AIDAperla entered service in 2017 at around 124,500 GT, the second Hyperion-class ship and the last large cruise ship Mitsubishi Heavy Industries built.

variants.name: Hyperion class
variants.note: Two ships, AIDAprima (2016) and AIDAperla (2017), both built at Nagasaki. Notable for a glass-roofed covered pool area designed for northern European weather.

operators.note: Canaries and Mediterranean itineraries.

prose: AIDAperla is the second and final Hyperion-class ship, and part of one of the most costly episodes in modern shipbuilding. Mitsubishi Heavy Industries took the two-ship order to break into the European cruise market and ended up writing off the equivalent of billions of dollars on delays, redesigns and cost overruns.

The company withdrew from large cruise ship construction afterwards, leaving the segment effectively to Fincantieri, Meyer, Chantiers de l'Atlantique and the Korean yards. AIDAperla is the last big passenger ship Mitsubishi delivered.

The ships themselves are well suited to their market: a glass-roofed covered pool and lounge area runs down the centre of the ship, which matters when your customers sail from Hamburg and Kiel rather than Miami.

ships.name: AIDAprima
ships.headline: The ship that cost Mitsubishi its cruise shipbuilding business.
ships.identity: AIDAprima entered service in 2016 at around 124,500 GT after repeated delays that pushed Mitsubishi Heavy Industries to enormous losses.

variants.name: Hyperion class
variants.note: Lead ship of two. Delivery slipped by roughly a year against the original schedule.

variants.name: The Nagasaki order
variants.note: Mitsubishi Heavy Industries won the two-ship contract to enter the European cruise market, absorbed very large losses on it, and subsequently exited large cruise ship construction.

operators.note: Baltic and northern European itineraries from Kiel and Hamburg.

prose: AIDAprima is a cautionary tale as much as a ship. Mitsubishi Heavy Industries took the order as a route into European cruise shipbuilding, hit repeated delays and redesigns, and absorbed losses running into the billions before delivering her roughly a year late.

The consequence reshaped the industry. Mitsubishi withdrew from large cruise ship construction, and the segment consolidated around Fincantieri, Meyer Werft, Meyer Turku and Chantiers de l'Atlantique — which is why almost every ship on this site comes from one of four yards.

She is a good ship for her market regardless: a covered, glass-roofed central pool and promenade area designed around German departure ports, where an open lido deck is unusable for much of the season.

ships.name: AIDAstella
ships.headline: The last Sphinx-class ship — christened by ten women at once.
ships.identity: AIDAstella entered service in 2013 at 71,304 GT, the seventh and final ship of the Sphinx series.

variants.name: Sphinx class
variants.note: Seventh and last of the series, built at Meyer Werft between 2007 and 2013. The four later ships are about 2,100 GT larger than the first three.

variants.name: Ten godmothers
variants.note: Christened at Warnemünde in March 2013 by ten women together rather than a single godmother.

operators.note: Mediterranean itineraries from Palma de Mallorca.

prose: AIDAstella closed the Sphinx series in 2013 — seven ships in six years, all from Meyer Werft at Papenburg, and the class that turned AIDA from a small German operator into Carnival Corporation's biggest brand in that market.

She was christened at Warnemünde by ten women simultaneously, which is unusual: naming ceremonies almost always have a single godmother, and the choice fitted a line that markets itself as informal and unstuffy.

Like every AIDA ship she carries the hull art the brand is built on — a pair of eyes and red lips painted across the bow. It is the only mainstream fleet in the world with a face on the front, and it makes an AIDA ship identifiable at a distance from any angle.

ships.name: AIDAmar
ships.headline: The sixth Sphinx-class ship.
ships.identity: AIDAmar entered service in 2012 at 71,304 GT, one of four enlarged Sphinx-series ships.

variants.name: Sphinx class
variants.note: Sixth of seven, between AIDAsol (2011) and AIDAstella (2013).

operators.note: Baltic itineraries from Hamburg and Warnemünde.

prose: AIDAmar is the sixth Sphinx-series ship and one of the four enlarged versions — Meyer Werft raised the class from 69,203 to 71,304 gross tons partway through the run, adding cabins without changing the basic hull.

At around 253 metres the Sphinx ships are small by modern standards, which is why they still do the work they were built for: Baltic and Norwegian itineraries out of German ports, where berth length and port access matter more than onboard scale.

AIDA operates them on the club-ship model the brand invented for the German market — open seating, buffet-led dining, no formal nights and no assigned tables. It is a genuinely different product from anything the American or British brands sell.

ships.name: AIDAsol
ships.headline: The fifth Sphinx-class ship — and a shore-power pioneer.
ships.identity: AIDAsol entered service in 2011 at 71,304 GT and was among the first cruise ships fitted to draw shore power in port.

specs.label: Beam
specs.imperial: 105 ft 8 in waterline, 123 ft 4 in max

variants.name: Sphinx class
variants.note: Fifth of seven. Ordered alongside an unnamed sister in December 2007, a contract that also triggered the enlargement of AIDAblu then under construction.

variants.name: Shore power
variants.note: Among the first cruise ships able to shut down her generators and run on landside electricity while alongside, tested at Hamburg-Altona.

operators.note: Baltic and Norwegian itineraries from Kiel and Hamburg.

prose: AIDAsol is the fifth Sphinx-series ship and one of the more quietly significant ones. She was among the first cruise ships anywhere fitted to take shore power — shutting her diesel generators down entirely while alongside and drawing electricity from the grid instead.

That matters more than it sounds. A large cruise ship at berth burns fuel continuously to run hotel services, and in city-centre ports like Hamburg that is the single biggest local air-quality complaint against the industry. Shore power removes it.

Her order in December 2007 also prompted a change to a ship already on the slipway: AIDAblu was enlarged mid-build to the 71,000-ton specification, which is why the last four Sphinx ships are bigger than the first three.

ships.name: AIDAblu
ships.headline: The Sphinx-class ship that was made bigger while she was being built.
ships.identity: AIDAblu entered service in 2010 at 71,304 GT after being enlarged mid-construction from the original 69,203-ton design.

specs.label: Beam
specs.imperial: 105 ft 8 in waterline, 123 ft 4 in max

variants.name: Sphinx class
variants.note: Fourth of seven, and the first of the enlarged group. The decision to increase her tonnage was taken while she was already under construction.

operators.note: Greek islands and eastern Mediterranean itineraries from Corfu.

prose: AIDAblu is the hinge of the Sphinx class. She was laid down to the original 69,203-ton design and then enlarged while she was on the slipway, coming out at 71,304 gross tons — and every ship after her was built to the bigger specification.

Changing a ship's dimensions mid-construction is not a small decision. It was taken in December 2007 alongside the order for two further sisters, at a point when AIDA was growing fast enough to justify redesigning a hull already in build.
She works the Greek islands and eastern Mediterranean out of Corfu, which suits a 253-metre hull. The Sphinx ships reach ports the 337-metre AIDAnova and AIDAcosma cannot approach at all.

ships.name: AIDAluna
ships.headline: The third Sphinx-class ship.
ships.identity: AIDAluna entered service in 2009 at 69,203 GT, the last of the three original-size Sphinx ships.

specs.label: Beam
specs.imperial: 105 ft 8 in waterline, 123 ft 4 in max

variants.name: Sphinx class
variants.note: Third of seven and the last at 69,203 GT — from AIDAblu onward the class was enlarged.

operators.note: Iceland, Norway and Baltic itineraries from Kiel.

prose: AIDAluna is the third Sphinx-series ship and the last built to the original 69,203-ton size before the class was enlarged. She and her two earlier sisters are the smallest ships AIDA operates.

The Sphinx design is efficient rather than showy: four Caterpillar MaK diesels producing 36,000 kW, two shafts with fixed-pitch propellers, twin rudders and a hull that runs at nearly 22 knots — quick for a mid-size cruise ship, which suits the long northern itineraries she works.

She spends her summers on Iceland and Norway runs out of Kiel, the kind of high-latitude, port-heavy schedule that the class was designed around and that the line's two LNG giants are far less suited to.

ships.name: AIDAbella
ships.headline: The second Sphinx-class ship.
ships.identity: AIDAbella entered service in 2008 at 69,203 GT, the second of seven Sphinx-series ships.

specs.label: Beam
specs.imperial: 105 ft 8 in waterline, 123 ft 4 in max

variants.name: Sphinx class
variants.note: Second of seven, delivered a year after AIDAdiva.

operators.note: Baltic itineraries from Kiel; long-haul repositioning in winter.

prose: AIDAbella is the second Sphinx-series ship, a straight repeat of AIDAdiva delivered twelve months later — Meyer Werft turned out seven of these hulls between 2007 and 2013 at roughly one a year.

That cadence is what built AIDA. The line went from a niche German operator to Carnival Corporation's dominant brand in continental Europe on the back of this class, before moving up to the Hyperion pair and then the LNG giants.

She carries the fleet's signature bow art — eyes and red lips across the stem, a design that has been on every AIDA ship since the first. No other major cruise line paints a face on its ships, and it remains the most distinctive livery afloat.

ships.name: AIDAdiva
ships.headline: The oldest ship in the AIDA fleet — and the start of the class that built the brand.
ships.identity: AIDAdiva entered service in 2007 at 69,203 GT, the lead ship of the seven-strong Sphinx series.

specs.label: Beam
specs.imperial: 105 ft 8 in waterline, 123 ft 4 in max

variants.name: Sphinx class
variants.note: Lead ship of seven built at Meyer Werft between 2007 and 2013 — the class that established AIDA as a large-fleet operator.

operators.note: Baltic itineraries from Warnemünde; Canaries in winter.

prose: AIDAdiva is the oldest ship AIDA operates and the lead of the class that made the line what it is. Seven Sphinx-series ships came out of Meyer Werft between 2007 and 2013, and the fleet still runs all seven.

The engineering is straightforward and durable: four Caterpillar MaK 9M43C diesels producing 36,000 kW, twin shafts, fixed-pitch propellers, twin rudders, stabilisers and bow and stern thrusters. She makes nearly 22 knots, which is brisk for a 69,000-ton ship and reflects the long distances between northern European ports.

Every AIDA ship wears the same face — a pair of eyes and a red mouth painted across the bow, by the artist Feliks Büttner. It began as a marketing decision for a single ship in 1996 and became the identity of an entire fleet; on a berth list of grey and white hulls, an AIDA ship is unmistakable.

ships.name: MSC Irina
ships.headline: The largest container ship in the world — 24,346 TEU, the current ceiling of the box-ship arms race.
ships.identity: MSC Irina holds the record every container line has chased for twenty years: the highest nominal capacity ever put on one hull, 24,346 TEU.

variants.name: Irina class
variants.note: Lead of a sister series including MSC Loreto, delivered from 2023. The class edged OOCL Spain's brand-new record by around 150 TEU.

operators.note: Asia–Europe service for the world's largest container line.

prose: MSC Irina is the current answer to a question the container industry has asked since 2006: how many boxes can one ship carry? Her nominal 24,346 TEU — stacked up to 26 tiers high — took the world record in 2023 by roughly 150 TEU over OOCL Spain, and as of mid-2026 it still stands.

The margin tells its own story. The top of the table is now separated by rounding errors because the designs have converged on the same wall: about 400 metres of length and 61.5 metres of beam, the practical ceiling set by the Suez Canal, berth lengths and the reach of the world's biggest cranes. Ships stopped growing outward and started packing smarter — Irina's record came from stacking height and hull-form efficiency, including an air-lubrication system bubbling friction away under the hull.

She was built by Yangzijiang, the largest private shipbuilder in China, for MSC — the family-owned Geneva giant that, in the same stretch of years, overtook Maersk to become the world's largest container line and, by 2026, the first to operate a thousand ships. Irina is the flagship-by-numbers of the largest fleet ever assembled.

sources.name: Adani Ports — MSC Irina at Vizhinjam (dimensions)

ships.name: Ever Alot
ships.headline: The first container ship past 24,000 TEU — Evergreen's 2022 record-breaker.
ships.identity: Ever Alot broke the 24,000 TEU barrier in June 2022 — the first ship in history rated to carry more than twenty-four thousand boxes.

variants.name: A class (second batch)
variants.note: The Chinese-built continuation of Evergreen's A class; the Samsung-built first batch (led by Ever Ace) topped out just under 24,000 TEU.

operators.note: Asia–Europe loops for Evergreen, whose entire fleet carries the 'Ever' prefix.

prose: Ever Alot's four extra boxes are one of shipping's better jokes: rated at 24,004 TEU, she cleared the 24,000 barrier by exactly the margin needed to make the claim, and in June 2022 became the first ship in history past it.

She is the Chinese-built continuation of Evergreen's A class — the Samsung-built lead ships, headed by Ever Ace, had stopped agonisingly short at 23,992 — and was delivered by Hudong-Zhonghua, the CSSC yard that also built the LNG-powered CMA CGM giants.

Her record lasted less than a year before OOCL Spain and then MSC Irina inched past, which is the modern pattern: with every hull pressed against the same 400-by-61.5-metre envelope, records now change hands by dozens of TEU rather than thousands.

ships.name: Ever Ace
ships.headline: The 2021 record-holder that missed 24,000 TEU by eight boxes.
ships.identity: Ever Ace took the world's-largest title in 2021 at 23,992 TEU — eight TEU short of the round number her own sister would claim a year later.

variants.name: A class (first batch)
variants.note: Lead of Evergreen's Samsung-built A class. The follow-on Chinese-built batch, led by Ever Alot, nudged capacity past 24,000.

operators.note: Asia–Europe service; took the largest-ship title from HMM Algeciras in 2021.

prose: Ever Ace arrived in July 2021 as the largest container ship ever built — 23,992 TEU, twenty-eight more than HMM Algeciras — and made her maiden Europe call weeks after her fleetmate Ever Given had made the company famous for entirely the wrong reasons.
Built by Samsung Heavy Industries on Geoje Island, she leads Evergreen's A class: twelve ships pressed against the megamax envelope, 61.53 metres across with twenty-four rows of containers abreast.

Eight TEU is all that separates her from the 24,000 milestone, which her Chinese-built sister Ever Alot duly collected in 2022. Records at the top of this table are now measured in single stacks of boxes.

ships.name: Ever Given
ships.headline: The ship that blocked the Suez Canal — six days that stopped roughly ten billion dollars of trade a day.
ships.identity: Ever Given is not the biggest ship on this site, but for six days in March 2021 she was the most important object on Earth: wedged diagonally across the Suez Canal with global trade queuing behind her.

variants.name: Golden class
variants.note: One of eleven Imabari-built 20,000 TEU sisters owned by Shoei Kisen Kaisha and chartered to Evergreen. Her sisters had entirely uneventful careers.

operators.note: Chartered from Japanese owner Shoei Kisen Kaisha; operated by Evergreen on Asia–Europe rotations — via the Suez Canal.

prose: At 07:40 on 23 March 2021, in high winds and poor visibility, Ever Given sheered off course in the southern Suez Canal and drove her bow into the eastern bank. Four hundred metres of ship jammed diagonally across a channel barely wider than she is long. For six days nothing moved: more than 400 vessels queued at both ends, estimates put the value of goods held up at around ten billion dollars a day, and some carriers began routing 6,500 kilometres around the Cape of Good Hope.

The refloating on 29 March took thirteen tugs, specialist dredgers that removed roughly 30,000 cubic metres of sand from around the bow, and a usefully high spring tide. Egypt then impounded her for three months in the Great Bitter Lake while a compensation claim — initially $916 million — was negotiated down to a confidential settlement, cargo still aboard the whole time.

The ship herself is ordinary: a 2018 Imabari-built, Japanese-owned, Evergreen-chartered 20,000 TEU workhorse, one of eleven near-identical sisters nobody outside the industry could name. That is precisely why the grounding mattered — it took one routine ship and one bad morning to show how much of the world economy fits through a channel 200 metres wide.

ships.name: OOCL Spain
ships.headline: The ship that held the world record for three months in 2023 — 24,188 TEU.
ships.identity: OOCL Spain took the largest-container-ship record in early 2023 and lost it to MSC Irina within months — the shortest reign in the modern record's history.

variants.name: Spain class
variants.note: Lead of six megamax sisters built for OOCL from 2023, all named for countries on the Asia–Europe run.

operators.note: Operated by OOCL — the Hong Kong-based line owned by COSCO Shipping Group — on Asia–Europe services.

prose: OOCL Spain briefly wore the crown in the spring of 2023: at 24,188 TEU she out-rated Ever Alot by 184 boxes and became the largest container ship ever built — for roughly three months, until MSC Irina was rated 150 higher.

That she measures 399.99 metres — one centimetre inside 400 — says everything about where the record now lives. Length is capped by berths and canal geometry; beam by crane outreach; so the contest has become a packing problem, won in stack height, lashing systems and hull efficiency.

She sails for OOCL, the Hong Kong line that pioneered the 21,000 TEU class in 2017 with OOCL Hong Kong and has been part of China's COSCO Shipping Group since 2018 — meaning the group holds two separate entries in this size race under two brands.

ships.name: OOCL Hong Kong
ships.headline: The first ship past 21,000 TEU — the 2017 record-breaker that opened the megamax era.
ships.identity: OOCL Hong Kong was the first container ship in history rated above 21,000 TEU — the ship that made the modern megamax generation inevitable.

variants.name: G class
variants.note: Lead of six sisters (Germany, Japan, United Kingdom, Scandinavia, Indonesia) delivered 2017–18 — the first class of ships past 21,000 TEU.

operators.note: Operated by OOCL, part of COSCO Shipping Group since 2018; Asia–Europe rotations.

prose: When OOCL Hong Kong was delivered by Samsung in May 2017 she was a genuine threshold: the first ship in history rated above 21,000 TEU, at a moment when 20,000 had only just been touched by MOL Triumph weeks earlier.

Her G class held the outright record for over a year and, more importantly, standardised the megamax template — just under 400 metres, twenty-three to twenty-four rows across — that every subsequent record ship from HMM Algeciras to MSC Irina has followed rather than exceeded.

Within a year of her delivery OOCL itself was acquired by COSCO Shipping, folding Hong Kong's most storied container line into the Chinese state giant — the brand, and this ship's name, survived the takeover.

ships.name: CMA CGM Jacques Saadé
ships.headline: The first giant container ship powered by LNG — 23,112 TEU built around an 18,600 m³ gas tank.
ships.identity: CMA CGM Jacques Saadé proved a 400-metre container ship could run on liquefied natural gas — the vessel was literally built around its tank.

variants.name: Jacques Saadé class
variants.note: Nine LNG-powered sisters built by CSSC's Hudong-Zhonghua and Jiangnan yards, delivered 2020–22 and named for Paris landmarks — the largest ships ever registered in France.

operators.note: Group flagship, named for the founder; French Asia Line rotation between Asia and Northern Europe.

prose: CMA CGM Jacques Saadé answered the question AIDAnova had answered for cruise ships two years earlier, at four times the scale: can the largest class of merchant ship run on gas? Delivered in September 2020 by Hudong-Zhonghua in Shanghai, she was the first ultra-large container ship powered by LNG, with dual-fuel engines that were the largest gas-burning marine diesels ever built.

The defining engineering decision sits amidships: a single 18,600 cubic metre membrane fuel tank, designed by France's GTT and cooled to minus 161 degrees Celsius, big enough for an Asia–Europe round trip on one fill. As CMA CGM put it, the ship was built around the tank.

Named for the group's late founder and registered in Marseille — the largest ship ever to fly the French flag — she set a record on her maiden voyage by departing Singapore with 20,723 laden containers, then broke it herself the following spring at 21,433. Nine Paris-landmark-named sisters followed.

ships.name: HMM Algeciras
ships.headline: The 2020 record-breaker — 23,964 TEU, delivered as the largest container ship ever built.
ships.identity: HMM Algeciras arrived in April 2020 as the largest container ship in history — South Korea's statement that its national line was back.

variants.name: Algeciras class
variants.note: Twelve 24,000 TEU-class sisters delivered in 2020 — seven from DSME, five slightly smaller from Samsung — all named for European ports.

operators.note: Asia–Europe loop for South Korea's flag carrier; maiden voyage departed with a then-record 19,621 laden TEU.

prose: HMM Algeciras was delivered into the strangest month imaginable — April 2020, with world trade in pandemic freefall — as the largest container ship ever built, and promptly departed on a maiden voyage loaded with a then-record 19,621 full containers as locked-down demand for goods exploded.

She was also a national project. HMM is the rebuilt successor to Hyundai Merchant Marine, salvaged by the Korean state after the 2016 Hanjin collapse left the country without a major carrier; ordering twelve of the largest ships on Earth from Korean yards was industrial policy as much as fleet planning.
Her class held the record until Ever Ace slipped 28 TEU past in 2021 — by which time the dozen Algeciras sisters, all named for European ports, had become the backbone of HMM's Asia–Europe network.

ships.name: Emma Mærsk
ships.headline: The 2006 ship that broke the size scale — powered by the most powerful diesel engine ever built.
ships.identity: Emma Mærsk redefined what a container ship could be in 2006 — so much bigger than anything before her that the industry argued for years about how many boxes she actually carried.

variants.name: E class
variants.note: Lead of eight sisters from Odense (2006–08), all named for members of the Møller family. The last great ships of Denmark's own shipbuilding industry.

operators.note: Two decades on the Asia–Europe trade; still in Maersk service.

prose: Emma Mærsk's arrival in 2006 was the single biggest jump in container-ship history: the largest ships then afloat carried around 9,500 TEU, and here was a hull the industry reckoned at roughly 15,500. Maersk insisted on 11,000 — counting only laden 14-tonne boxes, its own conservative method — and the resulting argument about her true size ran for years. Both numbers appear on her spec table above, because both are true on their own terms.

Her heart remains a record that still stands: the Wärtsilä 14RT-flex96C, a fourteen-cylinder, 2,300-tonne two-stroke producing 80,080 kW — the most powerful diesel engine ever built. She could run the Asia–Europe leg at 25 knots, a speed the slow-steaming era that followed made permanently unfashionable.

Built at Maersk's own Odense Steel Shipyard and named for the late wife of Mærsk Mc-Kinney Møller, she was also a Danish swansong: the E class were among the final large ships from Odense before the yard closed in 2012, and the follow-on Triple-Es went to Korea.

sources.name: M/S Museet for Søfart (Danish Maritime Museum)

ships.name: Mærsk Mc-Kinney Møller
ships.headline: The first Triple-E — the 2013 ship that made efficiency, not speed, the point of being huge.
ships.identity: Mærsk Mc-Kinney Møller launched the Triple-E era in 2013: Economy of scale, Energy efficiency, Environmental performance — the manifesto ship of slow steaming.

variants.name: Triple-E class
variants.note: Lead of twenty ships from DSME (2013–15); the second-generation Triple-Es led by Madrid Mærsk followed from 2017. Named for the group's legendary long-time chief.

operators.note: Asia–Europe network mainstay since 2013.

prose: The Triple-E was a philosophical U-turn expressed in steel. Emma Mærsk's generation chased speed; Mærsk Mc-Kinney Møller, delivered in 2013 as the largest ship in the world, was designed around going slower — a fuller, boxier hull, twin smaller engines instead of one monster, and a design speed cut by a quarter that halved fuel burn per container.

The name spells out the manifesto — Economy of scale, Energy efficiency, Environmentally improved — and honours Mærsk Mc-Kinney Møller, the shipping patriarch who ran the group for over half a century and died the year before her delivery.

Twenty Triple-Es came from DSME in Korea, and the template — maximum boxes per tonne of fuel rather than maximum knots — is the one every megamax since has followed. Her 18,270 TEU record stood until MOL Triumph crossed 20,000 in 2017.

ships.name: Madrid Mærsk
ships.headline: The first Mærsk ship past 20,000 TEU — lead of the second-generation Triple-Es.
ships.identity: Madrid Mærsk opened the second Triple-E generation in 2017 and carried Maersk past the 20,000 TEU line for the first time.

variants.name: Triple-E Mk II
variants.note: Lead of eleven second-generation Triple-Es (2017–19), all named for capital cities, with a raised bridge and extra tier of boxes over the first generation.

operators.note: Asia–Europe service; now operating within the Gemini network with Hapag-Lloyd.

prose: Madrid Mærsk is the quiet record-holder of the 2017 size war: delivered days after MOL Triumph became the first ship rated past 20,000 TEU, she carried a higher figure — 20,568 — and briefly made Maersk the owner of the world's largest ship again.

She leads the Triple-E Mark II generation: the same efficiency-first philosophy as Mærsk Mc-Kinney Møller with the deckhouse pushed higher and forward to stack one more tier of containers, squeezing over two thousand extra TEU from essentially the same hull envelope.

The capital-city-named class became eleven strong, and today they run inside the Gemini Cooperation — the hub-and-spoke network Maersk launched with Hapag-Lloyd in 2025 that reorganised the Asia–Europe trade around schedule reliability.

ships.name: MOL Triumph
ships.headline: The first container ship ever rated past 20,000 TEU — March 2017.
ships.identity: MOL Triumph crossed the line the whole industry had been circling: the first ship in history with a nominal capacity above 20,000 TEU.

variants.name: Triumph class
variants.note: Lead of six 20,000 TEU sisters ordered by Japan's MOL; the fleet transferred to Ocean Network Express (ONE) when Japan's three lines merged their container arms in 2018.

operators.note: Delivered to MOL, operated within ONE — the combined container line of MOL, NYK and K Line — since 2018.

prose: Twenty thousand TEU was container shipping's four-minute mile, and MOL Triumph ran it first: delivered by Samsung in March 2017 at a nominal 20,170, she was the first ship in history past the line — for about two weeks, until Madrid Mærsk was handed over with a higher rating.

Ordered by Mitsui O.S.K. Lines at the bottom of the freight-rate cycle, she was a bet that scale would win the coming consolidation. It half-worked: within a year Japan's three great lines — MOL, NYK and K Line — merged their container businesses into Ocean Network Express, and Triumph now sails in ONE's unmistakable magenta.

Her record footnote is short but real, and her class marks the exact moment the industry's growth curve began to flatten: nine years later, the largest ship afloat carries only about twenty percent more than she does.

ships.name: Barzan
ships.headline: The 2015 giant from a vanished line — UASC's flagship, now sailing for Hapag-Lloyd.
ships.identity: Barzan was the largest ship of United Arab Shipping Company, the Gulf line whose entire fleet — flagship included — was absorbed by Hapag-Lloyd in 2017.

variants.name: Barzan class
variants.note: Lead of six A18-class sisters built for UASC in 2015–16, among the most efficient large ships of their generation; all now in Hapag-Lloyd colours.

operators.note: Absorbed with the UASC fleet in the 2017 merger; Asia–Europe rotations.
Original owner United Arab Shipping Company ceased to exist as a brand in 2017.
operators.name: UASC (historical)

prose: Barzan is the ship that outlived her owner. Delivered by Hyundai in 2015 as the flagship of United Arab Shipping Company — the Gulf states' joint national line — she was briefly among the largest ships afloat and, by her designers' claims, the most carbon-efficient per container of her generation.

UASC had bet its future on a fleet of giants ordered at the top of the market; the rates collapse of 2015–16 broke that bet, and in 2017 the entire company was merged into Hapag-Lloyd. Barzan kept her Qatari fortress name and swapped her hull colours for Hamburg orange.
She remains the quiet cautionary tale of this board: building the biggest ships is not the same as surviving the market they sail in.

ships.name: SS Ideal X
ships.headline: The first container ship — 58 boxes from Newark to Houston in April 1956, and the world changed.
ships.identity: Ideal X was a converted wartime tanker carrying 58 metal boxes on deck. That voyage, on 26 April 1956, is the founding event of everything else on this board.

specs.label: Type
specs.metric: Converted T2 tanker with a spar deck for containers

variants.name: The experiment
variants.note: One of two T2 tankers Malcom McLean's Pan-Atlantic converted in 1955–56; purpose-built cellular container ships followed within a decade and made the conversion concept obsolete.

operators.note: Malcom McLean's Pan-Atlantic Steamship Company — renamed Sea-Land in 1960, the company that built the container age.
operators.name: Pan-Atlantic Steamship (Sea-Land)

prose: On 26 April 1956, a converted Second World War tanker left Port Newark for Houston with fifty-eight aluminium boxes bolted to a deck frame. Loading them had cost 15.8 cents a ton. Loading the same cargo loose, by hand, cost $5.86 a ton — a thirty-seven-fold difference that trucking entrepreneur Malcom McLean had computed and nobody in shipping had wanted to believe.

Ideal X was not much of a ship — a standard T2 tanker built at Marinship in 1945 — and that was the point. The revolution was the box: standardised, sealed, lifted from truck to ship to truck without anyone touching the freight inside. Longshoremen's unions saw the future immediately; one famously remarked he'd like to sink 'that son of a bitch.'

Every 24,000 TEU giant on this site is the compound interest on that voyage. The container cut the cost of moving goods so far that distance nearly stopped mattering, and modern global trade — Asia's export economies included — is the direct result. Ideal X herself was storm-damaged and scrapped in 1964, three years before the first purpose-built cellular ships made her breed obsolete.

ships.name: Seawise Giant
ships.headline: The longest ship ever built — 458.45 metres, longer than the Eiffel Tower is tall. Sunk in a war, raised, and sailed on.
ships.identity: Seawise Giant is the longest self-propelled ship in history and probably always will be: 458.45 metres, 564,763 tonnes deadweight, bombed and sunk in the Iran–Iraq War, refloated, and worked for another two decades.

variants.name: Five names, one hull
variants.note: Seawise Giant (1981) → Happy Giant (1989) → Jahre Viking (1991) → Knock Nevis (2004, as a floating storage unit off Qatar) → Mont (2009, for the final voyage to the breakers).

operators.note: A chain of owners from C.Y. Tung's Orient Overseas group to Norway's Jahre and Fred. Olsen interests — the ship outlasted several of them.
operators.name: C.Y. Tung / later owners

prose: No ship has ever been longer, and none is likely to be. Seawise Giant left Sumitomo's Oppama yard in 1979 already enormous, and her first owner — Hong Kong shipping magnate C.Y. Tung — promptly had her cut open and lengthened to 458.45 metres and 564,763 tonnes deadweight. Fully laden she displaced around 657,000 tonnes, drew nearly 25 metres of water, and needed over nine kilometres to stop. The English Channel was too shallow for her. So was the Suez Canal. So was the Panama Canal, by a factor of ten.

In May 1988, shuttling Iranian crude during the Tanker War, she was attacked by Iraqi aircraft in the Strait of Hormuz, burned, and settled to the bottom in shallow water — the largest ship ever sunk. The story should end there; instead, after the war, she was refloated, towed to Singapore, repaired with 3,700 tonnes of new steel, and returned to service in 1991 as Jahre Viking.

She worked another two decades — the last of them anchored off Qatar as the storage hulk Knock Nevis — before making a final voyage under the name Mont to the beach at Alang, India, where she was broken up in 2010. Her 36-tonne anchor survives at the Hong Kong Maritime Museum. On this site she is the fixed end of the length scale: the ship even the Eiffel Tower, laid on its side, cannot reach past.

ships.name: Batillus
ships.headline: The largest ship ever built as one piece — a 553,662-tonne French supertanker scrapped after just nine years.
ships.identity: Batillus was the biggest ship ever launched whole — Seawise Giant only passed her after being cut apart and stretched — and the market she was built for vanished before her paint was worn.

specs.label: Fate
specs.metric: Laid up from 1982; scrapped at Kaohsiung, 1985

variants.name: Batillus class
variants.note: Four sisters for French oil interests: Batillus (1976), Bellamya (1976), Pierre Guillaumat (1977), Prairial (1979) — the only class of half-million-tonne ships ever built. All were scrapped or converted young except Prairial.

operators.note: Built for Shell's French arm to run Persian Gulf crude to Europe around the Cape — a trade the oil shocks erased.
operators.name: Société Maritime Shell

prose: Batillus came from the same Saint-Nazaire slipways that later built Queen Mary 2 and the Oasis-class cruise giants, and in 1976 she was the largest ship ever constructed in one piece — a title she technically never lost, since Seawise Giant only out-grew her by being cut open and lengthened.

She was designed for a world that had just ended. The 1973 oil crisis closed the economics of the Cape-route supertanker while she was still on the slipway; by 1982, with tanker rates collapsed, she was laid up in a Norwegian fjord alongside her sisters, a half-million-tonne asset with no cargo worth carrying.

In 1985, nine years old and with remarkably few voyages behind her, she was sold for scrap at Kaohsiung for a fraction of her build cost. The Batillus class remains the cautionary tale of maximum scale: four of the five largest ships ever built by deadweight, none of which reached fifteen years of age in trade.

ships.name: Pierre Guillaumat
ships.headline: The largest ship ever built by deadweight — 555,051 tonnes, scrapped at six years old having barely worked.
ships.identity: Pierre Guillaumat holds the absolute deadweight record — 555,051 tonnes — and the saddest career on this site: too big for almost every port on Earth, scrapped after six years.

specs.label: Fate
specs.metric: Scrapped in South Korea, 1983 — six years old

variants.name: Batillus class
variants.note: Third of the four half-million-tonners. Named for a French statesman and founding figure of Elf Aquitaine, the oil company she was built for.

operators.note: Compagnie Nationale de Navigation, carrying crude for Elf — when a cargo could be found that any port could receive.
operators.name: Compagnie Nationale de Navigation

prose: By pure deadweight — the mass of cargo, fuel and stores a ship can lift — Pierre Guillaumat is the largest ship ever built: 555,051 tonnes, a record nothing since has approached. It made her almost unusable.

Fully laden she drew nearly 29 metres. That closed the Suez and Panama canals, the English Channel, and all but a literal handful of the world's ports; much of her short career was spent lightering — pumping her cargo into smaller tankers offshore because no berth on the delivery coast could take her. She was a logistics theory that reality declined to accommodate.

Delivered in 1977 into the post-oil-shock rate collapse, she was scrapped in South Korea in 1983 at six years old — likely the shortest life of any record-holding ship in history. Her record is safe precisely because her career proved no one should try to beat it.

ships.name: Esso Atlantic
ships.headline: The half-million-tonne ULCC that actually worked — 25 years of service the French giants never got.
ships.identity: Esso Atlantic and her sister Esso Pacific were the third and fourth of the five ships ever built past 500,000 tonnes deadweight — and the only ones that had full careers.

variants.name: Two sisters
variants.note: Esso Atlantic and Esso Pacific (1977), built in Japan for Exxon's international fleet — the only half-million-tonners with normal-length working lives.

operators.note: Exxon's Esso international tanker fleet, mostly on the Gulf–Europe and Gulf–Caribbean crude trades.
operators.name: Esso (Exxon) International

prose: Five ships in history have exceeded half a million tonnes deadweight: Seawise Giant, the French pair Batillus and Pierre Guillaumat, and Exxon's Japanese-built twins Esso Atlantic and Esso Pacific. Only the Exxon pair earned their keep.

The difference was the operator. Exxon owned the cargo, the terminals and the trade, so its giants were never hostage to a spot market that had collapsed — they shuttled the company's own crude for a quarter of a century, riding out the same slump that sent the French ships to the breakers before their tenth birthdays.
Esso Atlantic was finally beached at Gadani in 2002, twenty-five years old — the only proof in this class that the half-million-tonne tanker could work as a business, given a business that needed it.

ships.name: TI Europe
ships.headline: The largest trading ship afloat — one of the last two ULCCs still working the oceans.
ships.identity: TI Europe is the largest ship in active trade by deadweight: 441,893 tonnes, one of the four TI-class giants of 2002–03 and one of only two still sailing.

variants.name: TI class
variants.note: Four sisters delivered 2002–03 as the Hellespont quartet, renamed TI Africa, Asia, Europe and Oceania under the Tankers International pool. Africa and Asia became floating storage off Qatar in 2010; Europe and Oceania kept trading.

operators.note: Owned within the Euronav fleet, now CMB.TECH after the 2024 merger; trades and stores crude worldwide.

prose: The TI class was a deliberate anachronism: in 2002, three decades after the ULCC era ended in lay-ups and early scrappings, Greece's Hellespont group ordered four 441,000-tonners from DSME — the first ultra-large crude carriers built in a generation, and the largest double-hulled ships ever constructed.

TI Europe, delivered as Hellespont Tara, carries around 3.2 million barrels of crude — a day and a half of the entire United Kingdom's consumption in one hull. Since Knock Nevis (the former Seawise Giant) went to the breakers in 2010, she and her sister TI Oceania have been the largest ships afloat by deadweight.

Their careers have alternated between trading and floating storage, parked full of oil whenever the market pays more for waiting than for moving — including famously through the 2020 price collapse. Two of the four sisters were converted permanently to storage off Qatar; Europe and Oceania remain the last true ULCCs at sea.

ships.name: TI Oceania
ships.headline: TI Europe's twin — the other half of the last ULCC pair still trading.
ships.identity: TI Oceania shares with her sister TI Europe the title of largest ship in active trade — the final survivors of the ultra-large crude carrier breed.

variants.name: TI class
variants.note: Delivered as Hellespont Fairfax; renamed under the Tankers International pool that gave the class its initials.

operators.note: Euronav/CMB.TECH fleet; alternates between crude trading and floating-storage charters.

prose: TI Oceania is the second half of the last ULCC pair on Earth. Delivered by DSME in 2002 as Hellespont Fairfax, she is dimensionally identical to TI Europe: 380 metres, 68 across, around 441,600 tonnes deadweight and 3.2 million barrels of crude when full.

The TI quartet were built double-hulled from the keel up — the post-Exxon Valdez regulations that retired the old single-skin giants were exactly what made room in the market for them — and they remain the largest double-hulled ships ever built.

With her sisters Africa and Asia long since converted to storage hulks, Oceania and Europe carry the entire living memory of the supertanker era between them. When these two go, the ULCC will exist only on record boards like this one.

ships.name: Exxon Valdez
ships.headline: The tanker whose 1989 grounding rewrote the rules — every double hull afloat traces to Bligh Reef.
ships.identity: Exxon Valdez was an ordinary modern tanker until 24 March 1989, when she grounded on Bligh Reef, Alaska — and became the reason tankers are built the way they are.

specs.label: Grounded
specs.metric: 24 March 1989, Bligh Reef, Prince William Sound

specs.label: Oil spilled
specs.metric: ≈10.8 million US gallons (≈37,000 t) of crude
specs.source: Exxon Valdez Oil Spill Trustee Council

specs.label: Fate
specs.metric: Renamed repeatedly; beached for scrapping at Alang, 2012

variants.name: The afterlife
variants.note: Repaired and renamed Exxon Mediterranean, then SeaRiver Mediterranean, then Dong Fang Ocean and finally Oriental Nicety — banned by law from ever returning to Prince William Sound.

operators.note: Exxon Shipping Company, on the Alaska crude run from the Valdez terminal.
operators.name: Exxon Shipping Company

prose: Exxon Valdez is on this board for consequence, not size. Minutes after midnight on 24 March 1989, outbound from the Alaska pipeline terminal with a full cargo, she left the shipping lane to avoid ice and ran hard onto Bligh Reef. Eight of her eleven cargo tanks tore open; roughly 10.8 million gallons of North Slope crude went into Prince William Sound, eventually oiling around 2,100 kilometres of coastline in one of the worst environmental disasters in American history.

The regulatory response changed the entire industry. The US Oil Pollution Act of 1990 and matching international rules mandated double hulls on tankers and set phase-out dates for single-skinned ships worldwide — which is why every tanker elsewhere on this site, from the TI giants down, carries two complete skins of steel around its cargo.

The ship herself was repaired, exiled by name and by law — she was legally barred from Prince William Sound — and wandered the world under four more identities, ending as the ore carrier Oriental Nicety on the beach at Alang in 2012. The herring fishery she damaged has never fully recovered.

sources.name: Exxon Valdez Oil Spill Trustee Council

ships.name: Mozah
ships.headline: The first Q-Max — the largest LNG carriers ever built, moving a small city's winter gas in one hull.
ships.identity: Mozah leads the Q-Max class: 345 metres and 266,000 cubic metres of liquefied natural gas, the largest gas carriers ever constructed.

specs.label: Propulsion
specs.metric: Twin slow-speed diesels with onboard reliquefaction

variants.name: Q-Max class
variants.note: Fourteen sisters built 2008–10 for Qatar's LNG programme — 'Q-Max' meaning the maximum ship able to berth at Qatar's terminals. Named for the Emir's mother.

operators.note: Qatar Gas Transport Company (Nakilat), shuttling Qatari LNG to Europe and Asia.

prose: Mozah carries cold. Her five insulated membrane tanks hold 266,000 cubic metres of liquefied natural gas at minus 162 degrees Celsius — enough energy, regasified, to heat a city of a million people through a winter month — and no gas carrier before or since has held more.

She is the lead of Qatar's Q-Max class, fourteen ships sized to the absolute limit of the country's own terminals and built in Korea as the floating half of the project that made Qatar the world's dominant LNG exporter. Q-Max introduced onboard reliquefaction — boil-off gas is captured, re-cooled and returned to the tanks rather than burned.

Named for Sheikha Mozah, mother of the current Emir, she was delivered by Samsung in 2008 and remains, with her sisters, the upper bound of gas shipping — the LNG trade's equivalent of the megamax container ship, and a class that Qatar's newest expansion programme deliberately chose not to exceed.

ships.name: Prelude FLNG
ships.headline: The largest floating structure ever built — 488 metres, longer than any ship in history, and it cannot sail.
ships.identity: Prelude is the asterisk on every 'longest ship' list: at 488 metres she out-measures even Seawise Giant, but she is a moored gas factory, not a self-propelled ship.

specs.label: Function
specs.metric: Floating liquefied natural gas facility — produces, liquefies, stores and offloads at sea

specs.label: On station
specs.metric: Browse Basin, off Western Australia, since 2017; first LNG 2019

specs.label: Propulsion
specs.metric: None — towed to station and permanently moored by turret

variants.name: One of a kind
variants.note: The only vessel of her class ever completed at this scale. Moored through a 93-metre turret to seabed chains designed to hold her through a Category 5 cyclone — she is engineered never to leave.

operators.note: Operated by Shell with joint-venture partners including INPEX, KOGAS and CPC, on the Prelude gas field.
operators.name: Shell

prose: Prelude settles the pub argument on a technicality. Is she the longest ship ever? She is 488 metres — nearly 30 longer than Seawise Giant — and she floats, but she has no engines and will never make a voyage: she was towed from Korea to a gas field off Western Australia in 2017 and moored there, by design, for a working life of twenty-five years. Longest floating structure ever built: yes. Longest ship: this site gives that to Seawise Giant, and says why.

What she actually is may be more impressive than the record: an entire LNG plant — wells' processing, liquefaction to minus 162 degrees, storage and tanker offloading — compressed onto one hull holding around 260,000 tonnes of steel, displacing some 600,000 tonnes fully ballasted, built to ride out Category 5 cyclones swinging on her mooring turret rather than leaving station.

Her operating record has been as rocky as her construction was audacious: an electrical fire and months-long shutdown in 2021–22, industrial action, and repeated production halts have made Prelude the case study both for and against floating LNG. She remains, either way, the largest object humanity has ever set afloat.

ships.name: Berge Stahl
ships.headline: For 25 years the largest bulk carrier on Earth — an iron-ore shuttle only two ports could receive.
ships.identity: Berge Stahl carried Brazilian iron ore to Rotterdam for a quarter of a century as the world's largest bulk carrier — fully laden, only two ports on the planet could take her.

variants.name: One of one
variants.note: Built as a single ship for Norway's Bergesen group. Held the largest-bulk-carrier title from 1986 until Vale Brasil's delivery in 2011.

operators.note: Bergesen and successor Berge Bulk, on the Ponta da Madeira–Rotterdam iron ore shuttle for most of her life.

prose: Berge Stahl spent twenty-five years as the largest bulk carrier in the world doing essentially one thing: hauling 350,000-tonne loads of iron ore from Brazil to Europe's blast furnaces, back and forth, like a planetary conveyor belt with a crew of thirty-three.

Fully laden she drew 23 metres, which meant exactly two ports on Earth could receive her: Ponta da Madeira in Brazil, where the ore was loaded, and Rotterdam's Maasvlakte, where it was discharged — and Rotterdam only at high tide, with centimetres under the keel. Her route was not a choice but a definition.

Built by Hyundai in 1986 for Norway's Bergesen group, she held her title until Vale's purpose-built Valemax fleet arrived in 2011, and was finally retired in 2021 after three and a half decades on the same run — one of the longest single-trade careers of any giant on this site.

ships.name: Vale Brasil
ships.headline: The first Valemax — the 400,000-tonne ore carriers that took the bulk record and started a trade war.
ships.identity: Vale Brasil opened the Valemax era in 2011: the largest bulk carriers ever built, ordered by a mining company to bend the economics of the Brazil–China ore run.

variants.name: Valemax
variants.note: Lead of the 400,000-tonne class — dozens of first- and second-generation sisters built in Korea and China, designed to the 'Chinamax' port standard of 360 × 65 × 24 m.

operators.note: Ordered by Brazilian miner Vale; later sold into Chinese ownership and chartered back, sailing as Ore Brasil.
operators.name: Vale (later Chinese owners)

prose: Vale Brasil was ordered not by a shipping line but by a mine. Brazil's Vale, watching Australian ore reach China in half the sailing time, decided to erase the distance with scale: a fleet of 400,000-tonne carriers — the largest bulk ships ever built — cutting the per-tonne cost of the Brazil–China run by roughly a third.

China's shipowners saw a customer becoming a competitor and lobbied Valemaxes out of Chinese ports for years; the giants discharged at transhipment hubs in Malaysia and the Philippines until 2015, when a deal — Vale selling ships to Chinese owners and chartering them back — opened the ports. Vale Brasil herself was renamed Ore Brasil in the reshuffle.

The class was designed to the 'Chinamax' standard — 360 by 65 metres on a 24-metre draft, the largest dimensions China's ore terminals accept — and it worked: second-generation Valemaxes followed, and the Brazil–China ore bridge they created now moves a measurable share of all the iron on Earth.

ships.name: Pioneering Spirit
ships.headline: The largest ship ever built by gross tonnage — a twin-hulled giant that lifts entire oil platforms in one piece.
ships.identity: Pioneering Spirit is the biggest ship in history by internal volume and the widest ever built — a catamaran that picks up whole North Sea platforms in a single lift.

specs.label: Bow slot
specs.metric: 122 m long × 59 m wide, between twin bows

variants.name: One of one
variants.note: Conceived by Allseas founder Edward Heerema and originally to be named Pieter Schelte for his father — renamed after protests over the elder Heerema's wartime record. Nothing comparable has been built since.

operators.note: Allseas' flagship for single-lift platform installation and removal, and among the fastest pipelay vessels afloat.

prose: Pioneering Spirit is the category-bender of this entire site. By gross tonnage — the measure of internal volume — she is the largest ship ever built, half again the size of the biggest cruise ship. At 123.75 metres she is by far the widest. And she does not carry cargo at all.

She is a tool: a twin-hulled giant whose bows straddle an oil platform through a 122-by-59-metre slot, grip the topsides with hydraulic beams, and lift the entire structure — accommodation block, drilling gear, the lot — in one motion measured in seconds. Her 2017 removal of the 24,200-tonne Brent Delta topsides remains the heaviest lift ever performed at sea; decommissioning jobs that once took offshore crews years of cutting now take her an afternoon.

Built by DSME for the Swiss-Dutch contractor Allseas, she was nearly named Pieter Schelte until public outcry over the namesake's wartime record forced a change — the rare ship renamed by public opinion. Between platform jobs she lays pipe, including the deepest large-diameter pipelines ever installed.

lines.name: MSC
lines.headline: The largest container line in the world — a private, family-owned fleet bigger than any rival's.
facts: The world's largest container line by a wide margin — around 7.3 million TEU of capacity and roughly a 21.6% share of the global fleet as of mid-2026.
Became the first carrier in history to operate 1,000 ships, a milestone reached in 2026.
Still privately owned by the Aponte family, who started in 1970 with a single second-hand ship — and now also own MSC Cruises.

lines.name: Maersk
lines.headline: The Danish giant that defined modern container shipping — and now runs the Gemini network with Hapag-Lloyd.
facts: Held the world's-largest-line title for a quarter of a century before MSC overtook it in 2022.
Launched the Gemini Cooperation with Hapag-Lloyd in 2025 — a hub-and-spoke network built around schedule reliability above 90%.
Its Triple-E class made fuel efficiency, not speed, the design goal of every giant that followed.

lines.name: CMA CGM
lines.headline: France's shipping champion — the family group that put the first giant container ships on LNG.
facts: The Jacques Saadé class made it the first line to run 23,000 TEU ships on liquefied natural gas.
The largest ships ever registered under the French flag all sail for CMA CGM, homeported in Marseille.
Founded by Jacques Saadé, a Lebanese émigré, in 1978; still run by the Saadé family.

lines.name: COSCO Shipping
lines.headline: China's state shipping giant — and, through OOCL, keeper of two generations of size records.
facts: Owns Hong Kong's OOCL, whose OOCL Hong Kong was the first ship past 21,000 TEU and whose OOCL Spain briefly held the world record in 2023.
One of the largest shipping groups on Earth across containers, tankers, bulk and terminals combined.
Its Ocean Alliance with CMA CGM and Evergreen operates the biggest vessel-sharing network on the Asia–Europe trade.

lines.name: Hapag-Lloyd
lines.headline: Hamburg's 175-year-old line — consolidator of fleets, now Maersk's partner in the Gemini network.
facts: Traces to 1847 and the great German emigrant lines; the modern company has absorbed CP Ships, CSAV and the entire UASC fleet, flagship Barzan included.
Its unmistakable orange hulls are among the most recognisable liveries at sea.
Co-founded the Gemini Cooperation with Maersk in 2025, reorganising the east–west trades around hub-and-spoke reliability.

lines.name: Ocean Network Express
lines.headline: Japan's three great lines merged into one — instantly recognisable by the magenta hulls.
facts: Formed in 2018 by merging the container arms of Japan's big three: NYK, MOL and K Line — a century and a half of history in one brand.
Paints its ships bright magenta, the loudest livery in the industry, chosen deliberately so the new brand could never be ignored.
Operates MOL Triumph, the first ship in history rated past 20,000 TEU.

lines.name: Evergreen
lines.headline: Taiwan's green giant — every ship named 'Ever', including the one the whole world learned in 2021.
facts: Operator of Ever Given, whose six-day blockage of the Suez Canal in March 2021 held up an estimated ten billion dollars of trade a day.
Its Ever Alot was the first container ship past 24,000 TEU; sister Ever Ace missed the round number by eight boxes.
Every vessel in the fleet carries the 'Ever' prefix — a naming rule kept since founder Chang Yung-fa's first ship.

lines.name: HMM
lines.headline: South Korea's flag carrier, rebuilt around twelve of the largest ships ever ordered at once.
facts: Ordered the twelve-ship Algeciras class — the world's largest ships at their 2020 delivery — as a state-backed statement after the Hanjin collapse left Korea without a major line.
HMM Algeciras departed on her maiden voyage with a then-record 19,621 laden containers, straight into the pandemic cargo boom.
Formerly Hyundai Merchant Marine; the initials survived the restructuring, the Hyundai name did not.

lines.name: Yang Ming
lines.headline: Taiwan's second container major — a fleet built on the 11,000–14,000 TEU workhorse classes.
facts: Traces its lineage to the China Merchants Steam Navigation Company of 1872, making the heritage older than almost any rival's.
Partner in the Premier Alliance with ONE and HMM on the east–west trades.
Deliberately sat out the 24,000 TEU race, betting on mid-size ships that fit more ports — full coverage of its fleet is in build on this site.

lines.name: ZIM
lines.headline: Israel's global carrier — the niche-trade specialist that listed on Wall Street.
facts: Founded in 1945 to carry immigrants to the new state; today a NYSE-listed niche carrier rather than a size-race player.
Charters most of its fleet rather than owning it — the asset-light strategy that made its pandemic profits famous.
Full coverage of its fleet is in build on this site.

lines.name: Royal Caribbean
lines.headline: The line that builds the biggest — every world's-largest cruise ship since 2009 has been theirs.
facts: Operates the largest cruise ship in the world — a title it has held continuously since Oasis of the Seas in 2009, currently with Legend of the Seas.
Runs Starlink internet fleet-wide, the first major line to do so.
The Icon and Oasis classes give it eight of the ten largest cruise ships ever built.
lines.fleetText: Royal Caribbean sails the largest cruise fleet on Earth by tonnage — 28 ships as of mid-2026. The Icon class leads it (Icon, Star and the record-holding Legend of the Seas), above the six Oasis-class giants (Oasis, Allure, Harmony, Symphony, Wonder and Utopia of the Seas), five Quantum-class ships, four Freedom class, five Voyager class, four Radiance class and the veteran Vision class. A fourth Icon-class ship is under construction for 2027.

lines.name: Carnival Cruise Line
lines.headline: The 'Fun Ships' — the biggest cruise brand in the world by passengers carried.
facts: Carries more passengers per year than any other single cruise brand.
Its Excel-class ships carry BOLT, the only rollercoaster at sea.
Parent Carnival Corporation is the largest cruise company on Earth, spanning nine brands including Costa, AIDA, Princess and Cunard.
lines.fleetText: Carnival Cruise Line fields about 27 ships as of mid-2026 — the most passengers carried of any line. The Excel class leads (Mardi Gras, Carnival Celebration, Carnival Jubilee, with Festivale due 2027), above the Vista class (Vista, Horizon, Panorama), the Dream and Splendor classes, the long-serving Conquest and Spirit classes, and ships transferred in from Costa sailing as 'Carnival Fun Italian Style'.

lines.name: MSC Cruises
lines.headline: The cruise arm of the MSC shipping dynasty — Europe's largest cruise line.
facts: The largest privately owned cruise line in the world, and the biggest based in Europe.
Its World class, led by MSC World Europa, are the largest LNG-powered ships ever built for a European line.
Shares the Aponte family ownership with MSC's container fleet — the same house controls the largest cargo line and Europe's largest cruise line.
lines.fleetText: MSC Cruises operates around 23 ships as of mid-2026, Europe's largest fleet. The LNG-powered World class tops it — MSC World Europa and World America (2025), with World Asia building — above the Meraviglia-plus ships (Grandiosa, Virtuosa), the Seaside-EVO pair (Seascape, Seashore), the original Meraviglia and Seaside quartets, and the Fantasia, Musica and Lirica classes that built the brand.

lines.name: Norwegian Cruise Line
lines.headline: The inventor of Freestyle cruising — mid-size giants with no set dining times.
facts: Pioneered 'Freestyle' cruising in 2000 — no fixed dining times or assigned tables, a format the whole industry has drifted toward.
Norwegian Aqua introduced the Aqua Slidecoaster, the first hybrid rollercoaster-waterslide at sea.
Its hulls wear signature artwork — commissioned artists paint each ship's bow.
lines.fleetText: Norwegian Cruise Line runs about 20 ships as of mid-2026. The Prima class leads it — Norwegian Prima, Viva, Aqua (2025) and Luna (2026) — above the Breakaway-Plus quartet (Escape, Joy, Bliss, Encore), the Breakaway pair, Epic, and the Jewel, Dawn and Sun classes behind them.

lines.name: Disney Cruise Line
lines.headline: Disney afloat — fewer passengers, more space, and ships that play the horn in movie themes.
facts: Its ships' horns play Disney melodies — 'When You Wish Upon a Star' has echoed across Port Canaveral since 1998.
Deliberately builds lower-capacity ships: Disney Wish carries around 2,500 guests at double occupancy in a hull many lines would fill with 4,000.
The Wish class are Disney's first LNG-powered ships.
lines.fleetText: Disney Cruise Line runs seven ships as of mid-2026: the Wish class (Wish, Treasure, and Destiny from late 2025), the Triton-class Disney Adventure based in Singapore, the Dream-class pair, and the originals Magic and Wonder that started it all in 1998.

lines.name: Cunard
lines.headline: The transatlantic institution — running the Southampton–New York crossing since 1840.
facts: Operates Queen Mary 2, the only true ocean liner in service anywhere in the world.
Has run scheduled transatlantic crossings since 1840 — Samuel Cunard's first mail steamer predates the telephone by four decades.
Its red-and-black funnel colours are among the oldest continuously used brand marks in existence.
lines.fleetText: Cunard sails four Queens as of mid-2026: flagship Queen Mary 2 — the only true ocean liner in service — alongside Queen Victoria, Queen Elizabeth and the 2024-delivered Queen Anne.

lines.name: Costa Cruises
lines.headline: Italy's cruise line — Mediterranean giants under the yellow funnel.
facts: Italy's largest cruise line, sailing under the yellow 'C' funnel since 1948 — the brand began as an olive-oil shipping family from Genoa.
Costa Smeralda and Costa Toscana are among the largest LNG-powered ships in the Mediterranean.
Part of Carnival Corporation since 2000, anchoring the group's southern European market.
lines.fleetText: Costa Cruises operates about 9 ships as of mid-2026, led by the LNG Excellence-class pair Costa Smeralda and Costa Toscana, with the Venezia-class ships now sailing for Carnival and the Diadema and Fascinosa generations completing the fleet.

lines.name: AIDA Cruises
lines.headline: Germany's cruise market leader — the smiling lips on the bow, and the world's first LNG cruise ship.
facts: AIDAnova was the first cruise ship in the world able to run entirely on liquefied natural gas.
Every ship in the fleet wears the painted lips and eyes designed by artist Feliks Büttner — the most recognisable bows afloat.
Dominates the German-speaking cruise market from its Rostock headquarters.
lines.fleetText: AIDA Cruises sails around 11 ships for the German market as of mid-2026, led by the LNG Excellence-class sisters AIDAnova and AIDAcosma, above the Hyperion-class AIDAprima and AIDAperla and the long Sphinx-class series behind them.

lines.name: White Star Line
lines.headline: Titanic's owner — the Liverpool line that bet on size and comfort over speed.
facts: Ordered the Olympic class — Olympic, Titanic and Britannic — as the largest moving objects yet built, challenging Cunard on luxury rather than pace.
Lost Titanic in 1912 and Britannic, serving as a hospital ship, to a mine in 1916; only Olympic had a full career.
Merged with arch-rival Cunard in 1934 during the Depression — the combined company's ships flew both house flags for decades.

sources.name: National Museums Liverpool — White Star Line

lines.fleetText: White Star Line's fleet belongs to history: the Olympic class (Olympic, Titanic, Britannic) crowned an Edwardian fleet of more than twenty liners before the company merged into Cunard-White Star in 1934.

lines.name: CMB.TECH (Euronav)
lines.headline: The tanker giant of Antwerp — owner of the last two ULCCs afloat.
facts: Owns TI Europe and TI Oceania, the largest trading ships in the world by deadweight and the last ultra-large crude carriers at sea.
Formed from the 2024 combination of Euronav with the Saverys family's CMB group, pivoting the crude giant toward future fuels.
Its ancestors include the Compagnie Maritime Belge, on the water since 1895.

lines.name: Nakilat
lines.headline: Qatar's gas fleet — the largest LNG shipping company in the world.
facts: Operates the world's largest LNG fleet, including all fourteen Q-Max giants led by Mozah.
Created in 2004 purely to move Qatar's gas — the shipping half of the project that made Qatar the dominant LNG exporter.
Its Q-Max and Q-Flex classes remain the largest gas carriers ever built.

lines.name: Allseas
lines.headline: The offshore contractor that built the biggest ship in history to lift oil platforms whole.
facts: Owns and operates Pioneering Spirit, the largest ship ever built by gross tonnage and the widest ever built.
Performed the heaviest lift in marine history — the 24,200-tonne Brent Delta topsides, removed in one motion in 2017.
Founded and still owned by Edward Heerema; the company has laid more subsea pipeline than any other contractor.

lines.name: Berge Bulk
lines.headline: The dry-bulk heir of Norway's Bergesen dynasty — keeper of the Berge Stahl legend.
facts: Operated Berge Stahl, the largest bulk carrier in the world for a quarter of a century, on her two-port iron-ore shuttle.
Descends from Sig. Bergesen d.y. & Co, the Norwegian house that ran many of the twentieth century's biggest tankers and bulkers.
Names its ships for mountains — Berge Stahl ('steel mountain') set the pattern.

lines.fleetText: Princess Cruises sails 17 ships as of mid-2026. The Sphere class crowns the fleet — Sun Princess (2024) and flagship Star Princess (2025), with a third on order — above six Royal-class ships (Royal, Regal, Majestic, Sky, Enchanted, Discovery Princess), the Grand and Crown classes that defined 2000s Princess, and the smaller Coral-class pair for the Panama Canal.
lines.name: Princess Cruises
lines.headline: The Love Boat line — destination cruising at scale, crowned by the Italian-built Sphere class.
facts: The 1970s TV series The Love Boat was filmed aboard Princess ships and made the brand a household name before most people had ever cruised.
Its Sphere-class flagships Sun Princess and Star Princess are the largest cruise ships ever built in Italy.
A pioneer of large-scale Alaska cruising, pairing ships with its own network of wilderness lodges.

lines.fleetText: P&O Cruises runs seven ships from Southampton as of mid-2026: the LNG Excellence-class sisters Arvia and Iona at the top, mid-fleet Britannia, the family ships Azura and Ventura, and the adults-only pair Aurora and Arcadia.
lines.name: P&O Cruises
lines.headline: The world's oldest cruise company — carrying Britain to sea since 1822.
facts: The oldest cruise company on Earth: P&O began carrying passengers in 1822, ninety years before Titanic sailed.
Its flagship Arvia (185,581 GT) is the largest ship ever built for the British market.
Every ship is homeported in Southampton and built around the British holiday calendar — Norway and the Med in summer, Caribbean fly-cruises in winter.

lines.fleetText: Celebrity Cruises operates 16 ships as of mid-2026. Five Edge-class ships lead — Edge, Apex, Beyond, Ascent and flagship Xcel, with Xcite due 2028 — above the four-ship Solstice class (plus Equinox, Eclipse, Silhouette, Reflection), the refreshed Millennium class, and two expedition ships in the Galápagos.
lines.name: Celebrity Cruises
lines.headline: Royal Caribbean Group's design-led premium brand — smaller ships, bigger architecture.
facts: Sister brand to Royal Caribbean but plays the opposite game: its flagship Xcel is barely half an Icon's tonnage, by choice.
The Edge class carries the Magic Carpet — a cantilevered venue that rides up and down 13 decks of the hull.
Celebrity Xcel (2025) is the fleet's first methanol-capable ship.

lines.fleetText: Holland America Line sails 11 ships as of mid-2026: the three Pinnacle-class flagships (Rotterdam, Nieuw Statendam, Koningsdam), two Signature-class, four Vista-class, and the smaller R-class pair Volendam and Zaandam — a deliberately mid-size fleet in an era of giants.
lines.name: Holland America Line
lines.headline: 153 years at sea — the transatlantic dynasty that became cruising's quiet classicist.
facts: Founded in 1873 as a Netherlands–America shipping line; it carried hundreds of thousands of emigrants to the New World before it ever carried a holidaymaker.
Seven ships named Rotterdam since 1872 — the current flagship recreated the original's maiden crossing for the line's 150th anniversary.
Deliberately caps its biggest ships near 100,000 GT, betting on space, itineraries and live music over scale.

dataNote: Every specification is compiled from operator, builder and registry sources and cited per row. Fleet and status data are snapshots, marked ≈ where approximate and dated on every page.

metrics.name: Passengers per metre
metrics.why: How densely a passenger ship uses her length. Mega cruise ships cluster around 15; Queen Mary 2, built for ocean crossing rather than capacity, sits far lower — the number is a design philosophy in one figure.

metrics.name: TEU per metre
metrics.why: The packing race made visible. With every megamax pinned at about 400 m, capacity gains show up here: Emma Mærsk carries ≈39 TEU per metre of hull, MSC Irina ≈61 — same length, a different era of stacking.

metrics.name: Length-to-beam ratio
metrics.why: The classic shape number. Ocean liners built for speed run long and narrow (QM2 ≈8.4); box-maximising giants run fat (megamaxes ≈6.5); Pioneering Spirit, at ≈3.1, barely counts as ship-shaped — which is the point of her.

methodology.caveats: Derived metrics use the core sourced figures on each ship's page. Passenger counts use double occupancy where a range exists; TEU figures are nominal ratings, not laden records; beam is the principal published (moulded) figure. Where a core figure is approximate (≈), the derived metric inherits that softness.

yards.name: Meyer Turku
yards.tagline: The Finnish yard that builds the largest cruise ships in the world.
prose: Shipbuilding in Turku dates to 1737, but the modern yard's signature era began when it delivered the Oasis-class lead ships as STX Finland and then, under the Meyer family from 2014, took on the Icon class — the largest cruise ships ever built. Icon, Star and Legend of the Seas all took shape in its single vast drydock on the Baltic.

The yard's specialty is extreme-scale passenger ships with LNG propulsion: alongside the Icon class it built Costa Smeralda and the Mein Schiff series. Hull blocks arrive by barge from partner yards and are assembled under some of the largest gantry cranes in Europe.

yards.name: Meyer Werft
yards.tagline: The family yard on a river — cruise giants built inland and floated to the sea.
prose: Meyer Werft has built ships in Papenburg since 1795, seven generations of the same family. Its oddity is geography: the yard sits 36 kilometres up the narrow river Ems, so every giant it builds — including Disney Wish, AIDAnova and Carnival Jubilee — must be conveyed downriver to the sea in a slow-motion spectacle watched by thousands.

Its covered building docks are among the largest enclosed shipbuilding halls in the world, and it delivered the first LNG-powered cruise ship in history, AIDAnova, in 2018 — the pathfinder for the gas-fuelled giants that now top the cruise tonnage table.

yards.name: Chantiers de l'Atlantique
yards.tagline: Saint-Nazaire's giant-maker — from Normandie and the half-million-tonne tankers to the Oasis class.
prose: Few yards have built more record-holders: the liner Normandie, the France, the largest supertankers ever constructed in one piece — Batillus and Pierre Guillaumat — and, in the modern era, Queen Mary 2, the Oasis-class cruise ships and MSC World Europa all came down the same Loire-estuary slipways.

State-owned since 2018 after passing through Alstom and STX ownership, the Saint-Nazaire yard remains one of only a handful on Earth capable of a 360-metre passenger ship, and its order book runs years deep with the next generation of LNG-powered giants.

yards.name: Fincantieri
yards.tagline: Europe's biggest shipbuilder — cruise ships from Monfalcone and a navy besides.
prose: Fincantieri is the largest shipbuilding group in Europe, with the Monfalcone and Marghera yards turning out cruise ships for nearly every major brand — Norwegian Aqua and the Prima class among the latest — alongside naval vessels, ferries and superyachts from its other Italian sites.

Its cruise pedigree runs from the Grand-class ships of the 1990s through today's Prima Plus generation; by cumulative passenger tonnage delivered it is among the most prolific cruise builders in history.

yards.name: Harland & Wolff
yards.tagline: Belfast's yard of legends — builder of Titanic and the Olympic class.
prose: Harland & Wolff built the Olympic-class liners — Olympic, Titanic and Britannic — under the twin gantries of Queen's Island, Belfast, when they were the largest moving objects ever made. At its Edwardian peak the yard employed over 30,000 people and launched more tonnage than any other in the world.

The great slipways are now the Titanic Quarter museum district, watched over by the yellow Samson and Goliath cranes; the modern company survives at a fraction of its old scale, doing fabrication and defence work — but the name remains welded to the most famous ship ever built.

yards.name: HD Hyundai Heavy Industries
yards.tagline: Ulsan — the largest shipyard on Earth.
prose: Hyundai's Ulsan complex is the biggest shipyard in the world, a ten-drydock city that has delivered more ships than any other single site in history. Founder Chung Ju-yung famously won the first orders in 1971 with photographs of an empty beach and a 500-won banknote showing a sixteenth-century turtle ship.

Its giants on this site span forty years: Berge Stahl, the world's largest bulk carrier for a quarter of a century, and Barzan, UASC's 18,800 TEU flagship. Today the yard builds everything from megamax boxships to LNG carriers under the HD Hyundai group.

yards.name: Samsung Heavy Industries
yards.tagline: Geoje Island's record factory — Ever Ace, the Q-Max fleet and Prelude itself.
